parse.why: HCGPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported properties directly for HCGPE include: ionic conductivity 4.8 mS cm^-1 at 30°C and lower activation energy 0.16 eV vs HCE 0.18 eV, indicating easier Li+ migration (Results: “The ionic conductivity of HCGPE reaches 4.8 mS cm^-1 at 30°C… the activation energy of the HCGPE (0.16 eV) is lower than that of the HCE (0.18 eV)”). Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.56 for HCGPE (Results: “the tLi+ of HCGPE (0.56) is slightly higher than the HCE (0.48)”). Oxidative behavior: HCGPE shows an oxidation peak at ≈3.9 V and enhanced oxidation stability vs HCE (Results: “HCGPE shows an oxidation peak at ≈3.9 V… HCE shows a lower onset oxidation potential compared to HCGPE”). Application performance in cells is shown for systems employing HCGPE with separators: Li/Cu CE ≈99.3% for HCGPE-PE and 98.45% for HCGPE-PP; Li/Li symmetric cycling stable >950 h at 2.0 mA cm^-2 / 1.0 mAh cm^-2 for HCGPE-PE; full-cell Li/NCM811 with thin Li shows 91% capacity retention over 200 cycles with HCGPE-PE. These cell results involve HCGPE integrated with specific separators (PE or PP).

HCE (LiFSI/DME, LiFSI:DME=1.9) is used as the electrolyte in Li metal batteries and as a control to HCGPE. Reported HCE performance metrics include: CE ≈99.3% in Li/Cu cells (“both HCGPE-PE and HCE exhibit high CE values of ≈99.3%.”); Li/Li symmetric cell short-circuits after ≈740 h at 2.0 mA cm^-2 / 1.0 mAh cm^-2 (“the symmetric battery with HCE experiences a short circuit after ≈740 h.”); exchange current density 0.984 mA cm^-2 (“HCGPE shows…0.518 mA cm^-2 compared to HCE (0.984 mA cm^-2).”); Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.48 (“tLi+ of…HCE (0.48)”); activation energy Ea = 0.18 eV (“activation energy…HCE (0.18 eV)”). HCE also shows a lower onset oxidation potential than HCGPE. These confirm its application as a lithium battery electrolyte, but they do not map cleanly to the schema fields (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration), so those are left null.

LAWTP is reported as a solid electrolyte material. The Results state: "The LAWTP sample has a room temperature ionic conductivity of 1.07 mS/cm, which is higher than that of the LATP electrolyte (0.38 mS/cm)." This directly supports LAWTP’s application as a Li+ solid electrolyte.

PVDF-HFP-SN (without LAWTP) is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li-ion batteries. Ionic conductivity from EIS: PVDF-HFP-SN shows 1.07×10^-5 S/cm at 30 °C and 3.52×10^-5 S/cm at 50 °C (Fig. 4b). Electrochemical stability window: 4.7 V (Fig. 4e). Li-ion transference number: 0.296 (Table 2). Full-cell performance with LiFePO4/Li: at 50 °C and 0.5 C, initial 144.12 mAh/g, 127.62 mAh/g after 200 cycles (88.55% retention); at room temperature, initial 128.35 mAh/g, 114.30 mAh/g after 500 cycles (89.05% retention) (Fig. 8–9).

The sample PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP is explicitly prepared and named: "The SCEs prepared using 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 g LATWP were denoted as PVDF-HFP-SN, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP and PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP, respectively." Electrochemical testing as solid electrolyte for LIBs is described: SS/SCE/SS cells for ionic conductivity (EIS, 30–90 °C), and Fig. 4a–b present Nyquist plots and ionic conductivities for PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP-SN and PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP with various LAWTP contents. However, numerical values are only given for the 0.1 g sample (optimum), not for 0.05 g; thus no numeric conductivity for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP can be extracted without reading plots.

PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP is a solid composite electrolyte for Li-ion batteries. Results show: ionic conductivity 3.03×10^-5 S/cm at 30°C and 1×10^-4 S/cm at 50°C (Fig. 4a–b; Conclusions). Electrochemical stability window (decomposition voltage) 4.8 V (Fig. 4e). Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.478 (Table 2; Fig. 6c–d). Li/PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP/Li symmetric cell operates stably for 1000 cycles at 0.1 mA/cm^2 without short circuit, indicating dendrite suppression (Fig. 7b). In quasi-solid LFP/PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP/Li cells: at 50°C and 0.5C, initial 158.06 mAh/g, 133.82 mAh/g after 200 cycles (84.66% retention) (Fig. 8b,d); at room temperature and 0.5C, initial 142.69 mAh/g, 136.41 mAh/g after 500 cycles (95.60% retention) (Fig. 9b,d). With 5 μL liquid electrolyte infiltration, conductivity increases to 3.64×10^-4 S/cm (30°C) and 7×10^-4 S/cm (50°C), and t_Li+ becomes 0.323; stability window ~4.5 V (Fig. 5; Fig. S5).

The study prepares PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP solid composite electrolytes with different LAWTP loadings, explicitly naming the 0.2 g sample as "PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP" and using these SCEs for lithium-ion battery electrolyte applications: "The SCEs prepared using 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 g LATWP were denoted as PVDF-HFP-SN, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP and PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP, respectively." Electrochemical testing (ionic conductivity, LSV, tLi+, Li/Li symmetric cycling, and LFP cell tests) provides specific metrics for the 0.1 g sample, while no explicit numerical performance values are reported for the 0.2 g sample in the provided text; thus only the application scenario and target species are recorded for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP.

Neat [HMG][FSI] was directly evaluated for electrochemical stability: cyclic voltammetry at 75°C showed a wide electrochemical window (~8 V), with cathodic limit ≈ -4 V and anodic limit ≈ +4 V vs Pt quasi-reference. No specific ionic conductivity values for neat [HMG][FSI] were reported (conductivity data with explicit numbers pertain to Li-salt mixtures), so numeric fields are left null.

The work investigates solid binary polymeric systems (PMMA)0.91(PIL)0.09 including (PMMA)0.91-(IM14)0.09 as candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs: "we focused on protic ionic liquids (PILs) as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs." Solid-state NMR shows retained PIL mobility within the PMMA matrix, "indicating the potential for PIL-based polymeric electrolytes with enhanced ion mobility." Thermal analysis indicates enhanced thermal stability of PMMA when PILs are present. No ionic conductivity or battery performance metrics are reported for (PMMA)0.91-(IM14)0.09.

The solid samples, referred to as (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09, (PMMA)0.91(TFSI)0.09, and (PMMA)0.91(TFO)0.09, were prepared by blending PMMA and PIL in a fixed mole ratio... Thermal analysis and solid-state NMR (ssNMR) were employed to comprehensively characterize the solid samples. In this manuscript, we focused on protic ionic liquids (PILs) as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs. The article reports DSC, TGA, and NMR analyses for (PMMA)0.91(TFSI)0.09; no ionic conductivity or device performance metrics are provided for this sample.

The paper includes (PMMA)0.91(TFO)0.09 among the solid binary polymeric systems and reports only DSC/TGA and ssNMR characterizations (e.g., Table 1; Fig. 4–6). No application performance metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity, battery tests, capacity/selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration) are provided for this specific sample. Potential electrolyte applications and PFG-NMR transport data are discussed for IM14-based gel-like systems, not for the TFO-based PMMA sample.

The study considers gel-like systems for use as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices and as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs. Specifically for (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40, pulsed-field gradient NMR provided ion-transport metrics: “Considering the potential application of gels as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices, a ternary polymeric sample was prepared... Notably, the addition of PMMA resulted in a significant increase in the viscosity... leading to their classification as IM14-gel-like systems.” The self-diffusion coefficients of (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 are reported (Table 2): at 40°C, D_DB​UH+ = 1.85E-12 m2/s (w = 0.92), D_IM14- = 1.23E-12 m2/s; at 55°C, D_DB​UH+ = 4.90E-12 m2/s (w = 0.96), D_IM14- = 3.51E-12 m2/s; at 70°C, D_DB​UH+ = 1.08E-11 m2/s (w = 0.97), D_IM14- = 7.54E-12 m2/s. The paper notes: “The presence of PMMA leads to a significant reduction in the diffusion of both the DBUH+ cation and IM14− anion,” indicating the system’s ion-transport behavior relevant to electrolyte applications.

The ternary gel-like sample (DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39 was prepared by blending DBUH-LiIM14 with PMMA (10 wt%) “considering the potential application of gels as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices” (Section 3.3). The study focuses on PIL-based polymeric materials “as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs” (Conclusions). For this specific material, PFG-NMR quantified self-diffusion of DBUH+ and IM14− at 40/55/70 °C (Table 2), while “the Li+ self-diffusion coefficient measurement … was not feasible due to its very short T2,” indicating the system contains Li+ as the targeted charge carrier for LIB electrolyte application.

No application/performance metrics are reported for the monomer Pentafluorophenylacrylate (M1) itself. The paper describes M1 as a synthesized monomer and precursor (“Pentafluorophenylacrylate and -methacrylate, M1 and M2, were prepared…”) and then evaluates reactivity/conversion and kinetics for the polymer PM1, not for M1 (“The obtained polymers, PM1 and PM2…”, “A detailed study of the reactivity was performed… PM1…”, Table 1 and Table 2). Therefore, no scenario/target/capacity/breakthrough/regeneration specific to M1 can be extracted.

No application/performance metrics are reported for the monomer Pentafluorophenylmethacrylate (M2) itself. The paper provides synthesis and properties (e.g., stability, solubility) for M1/M2, and performance/conversion/kinetic data only for the derived polymers (e.g., PM2 in Table 1 and FT‑IR kinetics), not for M2.

FT-IR kinetic study for PM1: “In case of the reaction with primary amines the carbonyl peak of the active ester vanishes completely after 1 h, i.e. 100% conversion” at 50 °C in THF (Fig. 2). Table 2 gives kinetic constants for PM1 with hexylamine: k = 1.85 × 10^-3 s^-1, τ1/2 = 6 min at 50 °C in THF. Table 1 (NMR, 24 h, 50 °C in DMF, 1 eq) shows PM1 + hexylamine gives 99% conversion.

PM2 (Poly(pentafluorophenylmethacrylate)) shows 65% conversion with hexylamine under polymer analogous reaction conditions. Evidence: Table 1 (Results of the polymer analogous reaction at 50 °C in DMF for 24 h): Hexylamine, 1 equivalent → PM2: 65%. Supporting context: Conclusions state that under comparable reaction conditions primary amines reacted with 65% conversion with PM2. Additional PM2 data (for completeness): N-hexylmethylamine, 1 equivalent → 65%; Aniline, 1.5 equivalents → 0%; 1-Hexanol + triethylamine, 1 equivalent each → 0%; 1-Hexanol + triethylamine, 7 equivalents each → 0%.

syn_extract_result.error: Error code: 502

parse.why: XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.3 is explicitly tested as a solid electrolyte by AC impedance: “A typical impedance spectrum of the XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.3 electrolyte at 90 °C is shown in Fig. 2… Conductivity, σ, at a given temperature, T, was calculated…”; “The ionic conductivity (σ) of the liquid- and solid-based electrolytes was determined as a function of the temperature in between 30 and 90 °C… Fig. 7a shows σ … at wLiTFSI = 0.30.” For this specific sample, only relative conductivity is given (“The conductivity of XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30 is larger than that of XL-PFPE_0.30 by factors ranging from 10 to 100.”) without an absolute value at 90 °C, so the conductivity value is left null. Thermal stability data for this exact composition are reported (onset Td = 262 °C), supporting applicability as a solid electrolyte, but the schema has no stability field.

The specific sample is explicitly identified as a solid electrolyte: "The morphologies of two representative solid electrolytes, XL-PFPE_0.40 and XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35, were determined by scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM)." Ionic conductivity was measured for this class of solid electrolytes using stainless-steel symmetric cells and impedance spectroscopy: "The ionic conductivity (σ) of the liquid- and solid-based electrolytes was determined as a function of the temperature in between 30 and 90 °C." For XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35, STEM shows homogeneous salt distribution (no precipitation), supporting its function as an electrolyte: "Typical micrographs of two crosslinked samples XL-PFPE_0.40 and XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35 are shown in Fig. 3a and b, respectively. These micrographs show no evidence of salt precipitation." No explicit conductivity value is reported for XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35, so capacity is left null.

Fig. 7b reports ionic conductivity at 90°C versus salt loading; for XL-PFPE electrolytes it reaches a plateau of 6.5 ± 0.2 × 10^-5 S/cm for w_LiTFSI ≥ 0.40, which includes XL-PFPE_0.40. Additionally, STEM/EDS on XL-PFPE_0.40 shows a homogeneous morphology with uniform S distribution (Figs. 3a and 4a), supporting its suitability as a solid Li+ electrolyte.

PFP (PETMP-TFEMA-PEGDA) is blended with PEO and LiTFSI (EO:Li = 16:1) to form solid polymer electrolytes. The optimized 80PEO20PFP exhibits ionic conductivity of 3.7 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Conclusion). Additional PFP-containing electrolyte results: oxidative stability reaches 4.4 V vs Li+/Li for 80PEO20PFP (Fig. 3c); lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.39 for 80PEO20PFP (Fig. 4a,b); Li|80PEO20PFP|Li symmetric cell cycles stably >1700 h at 60 °C with ~95 mV overpotential (Fig. 5b); Li|80PEO20PFP|LiFePO4 delivers ~150 mAh g^-1 at 1C at 60 °C and maintains capacity over 100 cycles (Fig. 5c, S8); at 45 °C, ionic conductivity 1.66 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 and t_Li+ = 0.3 (Fig. S11); at 28 °C with SN-coated LFP cathode, 128.24 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles (88% retention) with ~100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5d,e). All these data are for electrolytes containing PFP (e.g., 80PEO20PFP).

Direct performance reported for PEP: “the t_Li+ of the 80PEO20PEP electrolyte was also tested and found to be approximately 0.18 (Fig. S5), higher than that of the PEO electrolyte…” (Results and discussion). No ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, or cell cycling data were provided for PEP; those data were for PFP-based electrolytes.

FR-PU is used as a solid polymer electrolyte matrix; the FR-PU-20% Li solid electrolyte shows ionic conductivity of 1.51 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C: “The ionic conductivity of FR-PU 20% Li solid electrolyte at 70 °C can up to 1.51 × 10^-4 Scm^-1” (Section 3.4; Table 3 lists the same value). Additional electrochemical context: electrochemical window ~4.81 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 7c); LiFePO4/FR-PU-20% Li/Li cell maintains 155.1 mAh g^-1 after 120 cycles at 0.2 C and 70 °C with 98.3% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 8c; Table 3).

Pure PU is the non-flame-retardant control membrane. Table 1 reports Pure PU LOI 16.8%, Tp 402.4 °C, PHRR 55.4 W·g−1, THR 6.1 kJ·g−1, ID/IG 2.137. Fig. 2a notes Pure-PU burns violently with molten dripping after ~5 s of ignition and almost burns out after ~10 s.

FR-PU solid electrolytes with LiTFSI mass fractions including 5% were prepared ("The mass ratio of FR-PU and LiTFSI are 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20 and 75:25..."). Ionic conductivities for FR-PU solid electrolytes with different LiTFSI contents were measured (Fig. 7a,b), implying Li+ conduction, but specific numerical conductivity values are only reported explicitly for 20% Li (Table 3, text). The text states lithium-ion conduction occurs via coordination/dissociation in FR-PU electrolytes ("it can realize the conduction of lithium ions in the FR-PU solid electrolytes"). No explicit electrochemical window or battery cycling data are given for FR-PU-5% Li; figures for conductivity provide curves without tabulated values for this composition.

FR-PU-10% Li is a flame-retardant polyurethane solid polymer electrolyte with 10 wt% LiTFSI (Section 2.3; Table 2 lists this composition). The work evaluates FR-PU solid electrolytes for all-solid-state lithium batteries (Sections 2.5.2, 3.4, 3.5). Figure 7a shows ionic conductivity versus LiTFSI content for multiple compositions, but explicit numeric ionic conductivity values and battery cycling data are only reported for FR-PU-20% Li (e.g., 1.51×10^-4 S/cm at 70 °C and electrochemical window 4.81 V). No quantitative electrochemical performance values are provided specifically for FR-PU-10% Li beyond thermal/mechanical properties in Table 2, so numeric fields are left null.

FR-PU solid electrolytes with various LiTFSI contents, including 15 wt%, were prepared: “The mass ratio of FR-PU and LiTFSI are 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20 and 75:25… the flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolytes with the mass fraction of lithium salt of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% could be prepared.” The ionic conductivities for different Li contents (thus including FR-PU-15% Li) were measured and shown in Fig. 7a,b, but no numeric values for the 15% sample are reported: “The ionic conductivities of the FR-PU solid electrolytes are measured… The ionic conductivities of FR-PU with different content of LiTFSI are shown in Fig. 7a…”. Quantitative electrochemical window and battery performance are only provided for the 20% Li sample, not for 15% Li. Table 2 confirms the existence of the FR-PU-15% Li sample and lists its thermal/mechanical properties, but these are not direct electrochemical performance metrics.

The paper reports: "The ionic conductivity of FR-PU 20% Li solid electrolyte at 70 °C can up to 1.51 × 10^-4 Scm^-1" (Fig. 7b; Table 3). It also notes that FR-PU-20% Li "displays excellent electrochemical stability" with an electrochemical window of ~4.81 V vs. Li+/Li at 70 °C (Fig. 7c), and cells using FR-PU-20% Li achieve 155.1 mAh g^-1 after 120 cycles at 0.2 C and 70 °C (Fig. 8c; Table 3).

FR-PU-25% Li denotes the FR-PU solid polymer electrolyte with 25 wt% LiTFSI prepared per: “The mass ratio of FR-PU and LiTFSI are 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20 and 75:25… the flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolytes with the mass fraction of lithium salt of … 25% could be prepared.” Table 2 gives its properties: “FR-PU-25% Li: Td,5% 221.1 °C; Residue char 9.62%; Tg −11.4 °C; Tensile strength 2.62 MPa; Elongation at break 111.9%.” Electrochemical metrics (ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cell performance) are reported specifically for FR-PU-20% Li (e.g., 1.51 × 10^-4 S/cm at 70 °C; 4.81 V window; LiFePO4 cell cycling), and the text states that conductivity decreases when LiTFSI > 20% without providing numeric values for 25% (“When the mass fraction of LiTFSI reaches at 20%, increasing the content of lithium salt will decrease the ion conductivity…”). Therefore, no quantitative electrolyte performance specific to FR-PU-25% Li can be extracted.

Electrolyte membrane Ta-ESM was evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte. Reported room-temperature ionic conductivity: "Ta-ESM's room temperature ionic conductivity is ... 4.858 × 10^-3 Scm^-1 and increases to 6.026 × 10^-3 Scm^-1 at 70 °C." Lithium-ion transference number: "0.61 for Ta-ESM." Interfacial stability: Li/Ta-ESM/Li symmetric cell showed stable stripping-plating for 567 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 without short-circuit; interfacial resistance fluctuated 45–62 Ω cm^-2 over 12 days. Electrochemical stability window: "LLZTO incorporated electrospun membrane has an electrochemical stability window beyond 5 V." Full-cell (Li/Ta-ESM/LiFePO4) performance: rate capacities 178.63 (0.1 C) to 153.26 mA h g^-1 (1 C), with near-100% coulombic efficiency; cycling at 1 C maintained stable capacity and ~100% CE over 100 cycles.

parse.why: LBOB is reported as an electrolyte salt for Li-ion batteries. Table 2 gives ionic conductivity for 0.10 mol dm−3 LBOB solutions at 298.2 K: PC = 5.56 mS cm−1; PC + DME = 6.79 mS cm−1. Table 1 reports LBOB solubility in PC (0.362 mol dm−3) with corresponding conductivity 6.76 mS cm−1 at 25 °C. Section 3.5 states its electrochemical oxidation stability is ca. 4.5 V vs Li+/Li.

AOEM_2 is described only as a synthesized intermediate: “Synthesis of 3-(2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy)prop-1-ene (AOEM2). AOEM2 was synthesized by a etherification reaction…” and then used to prepare fluorosilanes: “MFSM2 was synthesized by a two-step reaction of hydrosilylation AOEM2…” and “The detail synthetic procedures of DFSM2 were similar… using… AOEM2.” All electrochemical/cell tests and performance discussions are for DFSM2 (and MFSM2), e.g., “Considering its higher conductivity and higher oxidative potential, DFSM2 was chose as electrolyte solvent…”. No application/performance data is reported for AOEM_2 itself.

MFSM2 is proposed and evaluated as an electrolyte solvent for Li-ion batteries: “These desirable properties make these fluorosilane compounds potential electrolyte solvents for LIBs.” MFSM2 (1 M LiTFSI) shows room-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.4 mS cm-1 and an oxidative decomposition potential of 4.5 V vs. Li+/Li, with a reductive decomposition potential of 1.0 V (Table 1). The text notes that “both fluorosilane compounds (doping 1M LiTFSI) show intrinsic ionic conductivities up to the order of 10^-3 S cm^-1 at room temperature,” which applies to MFSM2, supporting its use as an electrolyte solvent.

Table 1 reports ionic conductivity σ = 1.8 mS cm-1 at room temperature for DFSM2 with 1 M LiTFSI. DFSM2 also shows an oxidative decomposition potential of 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li (Table 1), and is used as a co-solvent in high-voltage cells (E3: 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% FEC) showing improved cycling of LCO/Li and LCO/graphite at 4.4 V, but specific cycle data pertain to the mixed electrolyte, not DFSM2 alone.

E0 is defined as "1M LiPF6 EC/EMC (3/7 in vol.)". In LCO/graphite full cells at 0.5C with 4.4 V upper cutoff, "E3 exhibits considerable improvement in capacity retention of 92.5% vs. 66.8% for E0 after 135 cycles." The same comparison gives E0 coulombic efficiency of the first/135th cycle as 81.4%/99.6%. For graphite/Li half cells, E0 shows initial coulombic efficiency of 88.8%. Thermal stability (DSC) with E0 shows exothermic peaks with lithiated graphite at ~138°C, 260°C, and ~300°C, and with 4.4 V-charged Li_xCoO2 at 215°C and 283°C.

E1 is defined as: "E1 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.)". For graphite/Li half cells with electrolyte containing 30 vol% DFSM2 (E1), the first-cycle discharge and charge capacities are 332.4 and 455.7 mAh g^-1, respectively, with an initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 72.9%. In cycling, the graphite/Li cell using E1 shows capacity increasing from 287 to 304 mAh g^-1 after 50 cycles at 0.2C.

E2 composition: 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% VC. In graphite/Li half cells, initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) = 78.3%. Cycling stability with E2 is poor: capacity drops from ~330 mAh/g in the first three formation cycles (0.1C) to 200 mAh/g for the first cycle at 0.2C, then to 75 mAh/g after 10 cycles at 0.2C. These values are explicitly reported for E2 in the Results and Discussion (Fig. 2 text).

parse.why: For 1M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2 vol.), the Li+ transference number measured by AC impedance + DC polarization in Li||Li symmetric cell is 0.426 (Table 2: "Experiment tLi+ 0.426"). The electrochemical stability window (oxidative stability) extends to ~5.31 V (Fig. 2a and text: "the value of 1M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2, by vol.) can be extended to ~5.31 V"; conclusion: "~5.31 V vs. Li/Li+").

FBCP_20 is one of the fluorine-containing triblock copolymers (PFMA-b-PEO-b-PFMA) used to prepare solid polymer electrolyte films (FBCPEs) with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 (sections 2.4, 3.4). Electrochemical performance data (ionic conductivity, tLi+, ESW, cycling) are explicitly reported for FBCPE_30; for FBCPE_20 no specific numeric performance values are given, only that three FBCPEs (including FBCPE_20) were prepared and characterized morphologically (SEM/AFM/TEM/SAXS) and appear in the conductivity comparison (Fig. 6a) without extractable numbers: “FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity … than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” Therefore, only the application scenario and target species are recorded for FBCP_20; other fields remain null due to lack of explicit values.

No direct application/performance data is reported for FBCP_30 alone. All battery electrolyte and electrochemical results are for the LiTFSI-doped films labeled FBCPE_30 (e.g., ionic conductivity 2.68×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70°C; LSV decomposition at 4.9 V; Li/FBCPE_30/Li symmetric cycling >600 h; LiFePO4/FBCPE_30/Li capacities and cycling). The paper consistently distinguishes FBCP_30 (polymer) from FBCPE_30 (electrolyte), so application metrics cannot be attributed solely to FBCP_30.

FBCP_40 (PFMA-b-PEO-b-PFMA, 40 wt% PFMA) was formulated into an electrolyte film (FBCPE_40) with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 (Section 2.4) and included in the Arrhenius ionic conductivity comparison (Fig. 6a). The text states only that FBCPE_30 has higher conductivity (2.68e-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C) than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40; no numeric value, Li+ transference number, ESW, or cell cycling data are reported specifically for FBCPE_40. Thus no quantitative performance specific to FBCP_40 can be extracted.

syn_extract_result.error: Error code: 502

parse.why: DPPA1 is explicitly defined as a gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries: "DPPA and TFOA were weighed at a 1:1 ratio... The mixture was stirred... to form the polymer electrolyte named DPPA1." (Section 2.4). Application tests and quantitative performance metrics reported in the text are primarily for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm, tLi+ = 0.61, long cycling), not DPPA1. The only statement covering DPPA1 is generic: "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs" (Fig. 2 discussion), without DPPA1-specific numbers. Therefore, no numeric performance fields can be reliably extracted for DPPA1.

DPPA2 is defined as a DPPA:TFOA = 2:1 gel polymer electrolyte composition: “DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively.” The electrolyte is used for lithium metal batteries (e.g., Li|GPEs|Li and NCM811|GPEs|Li cells). A general result applicable to all TFOA-containing samples (thus including DPPA2) states: “all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs.” No DPPA2-specific numeric performance values are reported.

DPPA3 gel polymer electrolyte shows ionic conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm and t_Li+ = 0.61 at 30°C (Fig. 2b,c; Highlights). Li|DPPA3|Li cells cycle stably for >2000 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 3a; Highlights). In NCM811|DPPA3|Li cells, capacity retention is 88.2% after 300 cycles at 3C, 30°C and 101.5 mAh/g at 10C (Results; Conclusion).

DPPA4 (DPPA:TFOA = 4:1) was explicitly prepared among the DPPAx GPEs (Section 2.4). The paper states that "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs," which applies to DPPA4. However, specific numerical performance (e.g., ionic conductivity, tLi+, cycling) is only reported for DPPA3 (e.g., 2.52×10^-3 S cm^-1, tLi+ = 0.61, >2000 h), and the figure showing different ratios provides curves without tabulated values for DPPA4. Therefore, no quantitative metrics specific to DPPA4 can be extracted.

DPPA5 is defined as a DPPA:TFOA = 5:1 gel polymer electrolyte sample among the set (DPPA1–DPPA5): "DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively." Electrochemical tests were performed across different ratios, and a general statement applies to all TFOA-containing samples (including DPPA5): "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs" (Fig. 2 a,b). However, specific numeric performance values (e.g., ionic conductivity, t_Li+, cycling data) were only reported for DPPA3; DPPA5 has no explicit numeric data in text/tables. Figures provide curves but no readable values for DPPA5; therefore metrics are left null.

At 30 °C, the ion conductivity (σ) of FG-GPE-0 is 3.42 × 10^{-4} S cm^{-1} (Fig. 2e). The ESW value for FG-GPE-0 is 4.54 V (Fig. 2a–d). These data indicate its application as a lithium battery electrolyte.

FG-GPE-3 (0.3 wt% FG) is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Performance for FG-GPE-3: ESW = 4.55 V (Fig. 2b); ionic conductivity σ = 2.67 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 2e); t_Li+ = 0.55 (Fig. S9). Symmetric Li|FG-GPE-3|Li cell: stable cycling for 4500 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with ~10 mV overpotential; at 0.1 mA cm^-2, a sudden voltage drop occurs after 1160 h (Fig. 4). Full cells: LFP|FG-GPE-3|Li shows 65.2% capacity retention after 1800 cycles at 3C and 30 °C (Fig. 5c); NCM811|FG-GPE-3|Li shows initial CE 90.66% at 0.1C and 64.6% capacity retention after 400 cycles at 3C (Fig. 6b–c).

syn_extract_result.error: Error code: 502

parse.why: FG-GPE-7 (0.7 wt% FG) is a gel polymer electrolyte evaluated for Li-ion batteries. Specific data for FG-GPE-7 include an electrochemical stability window of 4.71 V (Fig. 2d) and ionic conductivity of 2.24×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 2e). No Li+ transference number, symmetric-cell cycling, or full-cell (LFP/NCM811) capacity-retention results were reported for FG-GPE-7.

ILPE-EMImTFSI (1 M LiTFSI in EMImTFSI, PI membrane) used in Li/ILPE/LiFePO4 cells showed: "With an initially high discharge capacity of 143 mAh g^-1... at 0.1 C" (room temperature). Rate data: "ILPE-EMImTFSI delivered reversible discharge capacities of 143 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C, 133 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C, and 109 mAh g^-1 at 1 C" (25 °C). Related electrolyte properties: ionic conductivity 5.3×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C for ILPE-EMImTFSI; anodic stability 5.5 V vs Li.

Table 1 shows MnO2@PVDF/TBAC has the largest ionic conductivity of 2.95×10^-3 S/cm and smallest bulk resistance of 1.02 Ω; LSV gives an electrochemical stability window of 3.9 V. In assembled LIC cells using MnO2@PVDF/TBAC, the specific capacitance reached 19.5 F/g at 0.5 C (also 35 F/g at 0.1 C and ~25 F/g at 0.2 C) with 67.20% capacity retention after 10,000 cycles. Thermal shrinkage at 170 °C was 7.17%.

F-GPE is used as the electrolyte in Li||LCO and LCO||Gr lithium-ion batteries. Reported cell performance includes: (i) LCO||F-GPE||Li coin cell delivered 89.4 mAh g^-1 at −40 °C (0.1C), which was 72.1% of room-temperature capacity (Fig. 5d: “The discharge capacity of the LCO||F-GPE||Li battery was 89.4 mA h g^-1 when the operating temperature is reduced to −40 °C, which was 72.1% of capacity at room temperature.”). Supporting metrics for the electrolyte include wide-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.2, 7.6, and 12 mS cm^-1 at −40, 30, and 60 °C (Fig. 2d text), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.51 (Fig. 2c text), and oxidative stability of 5.6 V (Fig. S2 text). Additional whole-cell results with the same F-GPE show 96.3% capacity retention for 100 cycles at 1C at room temperature (Fig. 5a) and, for the LCO||F-GPE||Gr pouch cell, 0.83 Ah at −40 °C and up to 1.35 Ah at 60 °C (Fig. 7b).

LE is defined as 1 M LiPF6 in EP/EMC/FEC (4:1:1) and is used/commercial electrolyte benchmark in Li metal/LCO cells. Reported LE-specific performance metrics include: (i) Electrochemical stability window 4.8 V ("the electrochemical windows of 1 M LiPF6 EC: DEC and LE were at 3.8 V and 4.8 V, respectively."); (ii) Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.44 at room temperature ("the t_Li+ for LE was only 0.44, as shown by Fig. S3 and Table S1"); (iii) Symmetric Li||LE||Li overpotential up to 200 mV and short-circuit after 420 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 ("the highest overpotential of the symmetric Li||Li battery with LE electrolyte was 200 mV ... the battery short-circuited after 420 h of cycling"); (iv) Li||LE||Cu average Coulombic efficiency CE_avg = 80.62% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 ("CE_avg of Li||LE||Cu cell is shown in the inset ... 80.62%"); (v) Desolvation activation energy Ea = 58.466 kJ mol^-1 ("The Ea values of F-GPE, LE and 1 M LiPF6 EC: DEC were 63.175, 58.466 and 64.314 kJ mol^-1, respectively"); (vi) Thermal stability: LE weight loss 54% at 100 °C ("The thermal stability of LE was the worst when the temperature was raised to 100 °C, with a weight loss of 54%."). No discharge capacities for LCO||LE||Li are provided; hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration fields are left null.

PVDF was investigated as the polymer matrix in an ionic liquid ([PMPyrr][TFSI], 40 wt%) solid polymer electrolyte. Electrochemical parameters reported specifically for the PVDF blend: ionic conductivity 5.3 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 at 20 °C and 2.5 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C; activation energy 14 kJ/mol; Li+ transference number 0.12 (Table 4). Cyclic voltammetry shown for the PVDF sample indicates low currents (<1×10^-8 A) between 0–5 V, evidencing electrochemical stability (Fig. 4c). However, no battery cycling was performed with PVDF-based SPE due to its low Li+ transference number and high crystallinity, so discharge capacity data are not available for PVDF (Section 3.4).

P(VDF-HFP) with 40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI] was used as the solid polymer electrolyte in Li/LFP half-cells. Fig. 5a reports an initial discharge capacity of 140 mAh/g at C/10 and room temperature for the P(VDF-HFP)-based sample. Table 4 gives electrochemical properties for P(VDF-HFP): σi(20 °C)=6.2×10^-5 S/cm, σi(60 °C)=2.4×10^-4 S/cm, and Li+ transference number t_Li+=0.35, confirming its SPE functionality in the solid-state battery.

Cycling tests with LFP//Li cells at room temperature and C/10 show an initial discharge capacity of 146 mAh/g for the P(VDF-TrFE)-based SPE (40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI]) (Section 3.4). Table 4 reports for P(VDF-TrFE) a Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.53 and ionic conductivity σi = 4.6 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 at 20 °C (2.1 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C).

P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) was used as the solid polymer electrolyte (40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI]) in Li/LFP half-cells. Battery testing at room temperature: “An initial discharge capacity value of … 133 mAh/g was achieved for … P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) … After 50 cycles, the blend sample based on P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) displays very stable discharge capacity of 122 mAh/g, with a high capacity retention of ~92%.” Additionally at 1C: “initial and final discharge capacity value is 91 and 68 mAh/g, respectively… The coulombic efficiency is ~90%.” Electrolyte metrics for this sample: Table 4 gives σi (20 °C) = 2.3 × 10^-5 S/cm, σi (60 °C) = 3.3 × 10^-4 S/cm, activation energy = 22 kJ/mol, and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.71. CV shows low currents up to 5 V, indicating stability for battery use.

For 31VPIF/OZ: room-temperature ionic conductivity σ = 3.58×10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4(b) text: “To ensure normal operation of SPEs at room temperature (σ = 3.58×10^-4 S cm^-1)…”). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.52 at 40 °C (Fig. 4(e)). Electrochemical window reaches 5.4 V at 40 °C (Fig. 4(d)). Symmetric cell Li|31VPIF/OZ|Li cycles 1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 without short circuit (Fig. 5(c)). Full cell Li|31VPIF/OZ|LFP shows 91.5% capacity retention after 600 cycles at 0.5C, 25 °C (Fig. 6(g)).

The TFF electrolyte (TEP:FEMC=1:3 with 10 vol% FEC, 1.5 mmol LiFSI per mL) enables high-voltage Li batteries: LSV shows ~0 μA until 5.6 V (Fig. 2a); chronoamperometry shows ~0 μA leakage current for 15 h at 4.4–4.6 V (Fig. 2b). In LCO||Li cells it delivers ~100 mAh g^-1 at 20C (Fig. 2g, S8); retains 141 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C after 300 cycles (76% retention, Fig. S9a) and 74.9% capacity stability after 400 cycles at 5C (Fig. 2h). Li||Li symmetric cells last >2200 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4f). LCO||Gr pouch cells show 0.87 Ah with 95.5% retention after 150 cycles at 4.5 V (Fig. 5c). Nonflammability is confirmed by SET = 0 s g^-1 (Fig. 1b, Table S1) and lower temperature rise during 2 A fast charging (24.6→30.9 °C, Fig. 5g).

The paper prepares a comparison sample explicitly termed a TEP electrolyte: "a TEP electrolyte of 1.5 mmol LiFSI dissolving in 1 mL TEP was prepared for comparison" (Sec. 2.1). It is discussed as LiFSI-TEP: Raman analysis contrasts TFF with "1.5 M LiFSI in TEP (denoted as LiFSI-TEP)" (Fig. 1e). For application relevance, the authors note graphite issues: "SEM image ... confirms the stabilized Gr structure, while it is cracked for LiFSI-TEP (Fig. S21)" and LCO||Gr cycling with TFF is "much higher than ... LiFSI-TEP" (Fig. 5b), but no quantitative electrochemical performance, safety (SET), LSV, or cycling metrics are reported for the TEP electrolyte in the provided text. Therefore, only the scenario (battery electrolyte) can be filled; all performance fields remain null.

In LCO || Gr cells at 4.5 V, the commercial LB-063 electrolyte delivered 18.92 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles with 13.42% retention (Fig. 5b). The paper also reports LB-063 is flammable with SET = 22.77 s g^-1 (Fig. 1b, Table S1) and shows oxidative current from ~4 V in LSV (Fig. 2a), but only the capacity value is recorded here per schema.

In Li|IG|LFP cells using ionogel with 15 wt% LLZTO 6 h as active filler, the cell delivered a specific discharge capacity of 162 mA h g^{-1} after 100 cycles with a capacity retention of 99.3% after 200 cycles and an average Coulombic efficiency of 99.93% (Fig. 8; Section 3.3). Electrochemical stability window toward oxidation reached 5.27 V vs. Li+/Li for 15% LLZTO 6 h (Fig. 5b). Symmetric Li|IG|Li cells showed a stable overpotential of 100 mV for 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^{-2} (Fig. 6c). Ionic conductivity for 15% LLZTO 6 h IG was the highest among samples and reported as >0.4 mS cm^{-1} at 20 °C and >1 mS cm^{-1} at 40 °C (Conclusions). These results are specific to the formulation containing LLZTO 6 h filler.

For the ionogel with LLZTO 15 h filler ("15% LLZTO 15 h"), the oxidation stability increased to 4.98 V vs Li+/Li at 5 µA cm^-2 (Fig. 5b: "...increased the oxidation potential from 4.65 to 4.98 V for 15% LLZTO 15 h and 24 h..."). In Li|IG|LFP cells (0.2C at 40°C after activation), "15% LLZTO 15 h displayed a large capacity retention of 83.3% after 200 cycles" (Fig. 8 text).

LLZTO 24 h is used as a silane-functionalized active filler in ionogel electrolytes for lithium-metal batteries. For the composite with 15% LLZTO 24 h, the electrochemical stability toward oxidation was reported as 4.98 V vs. Li+/Li at 5 µA cm^-2 (Fig. 5b: “The insertion of the inorganic filler increased the oxidation potential from 4.65 to 4.98 V for 15% LLZTO 15 h and 24 h”). Stripping/plating tests noted a rising overpotential at 0.05 mA cm^-2 and noisy voltage profiles at higher current, indicating sluggish Li+ transport and uneven Li deposition (Fig. 6e description). Cycling in Li|IG|LFP cells was described as ‘rather stable cycling with a lower capacity delivered’ for 15% LLZTO 24 h, but without explicit numeric capacity/retention values (Fig. 8e text). No standalone numeric ionic conductivity values for the 24 h sample were provided; conductivity trends were discussed qualitatively relative to other variants (Fig. 5a). Because explicit numeric capacity or conductivity for LLZTO 24 h were not given, the corresponding fields are left null.

application.target_species: EMITFSI-based precursor solution (12 wt% C1-4TFSI + 0.1 wt% AIBN)

parse.why: The PDDATFSI porous membrane shows a high uptake ability for the precursor solution (251.2%, Fig. S5d). The precursor is defined as 12 wt% C1-4TFSI and 0.1 wt% AIBN in EMITFSI-based electrolyte. Uptake η was measured by soaking until saturation (Eq. 2). Supporting context: contact angle of the precursor on PDDATFSI is 5.3°, and the membrane has porosity 57.5%, which the authors relate to the high uptake.

Li-HPILSE is used as a solid electrolyte in Li-ion batteries. Electrolyte properties: ionic conductivity 1.06×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3a); electrochemical stability up to ~4.4 V vs. Li/Li+ (CV, Fig. 3b); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.20 (chronoamperometry/EIS, Fig. 3c); non-combustible in flame test (Fig. 2d) and only 6.5% weight loss at 300 °C in TGA (Fig. 2e); Li/Li-HPILSE/Li symmetric cells show low voltage fluctuation over 300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 indicating stable Li plating/stripping (Fig. S10). Battery performance with LiFePO4 cathode: specific reversible capacities of 162.4, 155.0, 141.5, 111.6 and 81.4 mAh g^-1 at 0.05C, 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C and 1C, respectively; 99.1% capacity recovery when rate returns to 0.05C (Fig. 4a,b). Cycling at 0.1C: 147.1 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles with 97.7% capacity retention and ~100% coulombic efficiency after the first cycle; interfacial resistance remains nearly stable (Fig. 4c,d; Table S3).

Na-HPILSE is used as a solid electrolyte for Na-ion systems. Reported performance: ionic conductivity 1.15×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3a); electrochemical stability up to 4.6 V vs. Na/Na+ (inset of Fig. 3b); Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.22 (Fig. 3d). In Na0.9[Cu0.22Fe0.30Mn0.48]O2/Na-HPILSE/Na cells: reversible capacities of 101.0, 93.4, 82.5, 70.5, 59.9 mAh/g at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 C, respectively; 95.9% capacity recovery when returning to 0.05 C; after 100 cycles at 0.1 C, 85.6 mAh/g with 85.5% retention and near-100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5b–c).

G2 is the commercial electrolyte benchmark tested in Li metal batteries. In Li||LFP cells, after the first activation at 0.1C, the G2 electrolyte delivers an initial discharge specific capacity of 154.8 mAh·g^-1 (CE 93.7%). Additional G2-specific performance reported includes: oxidative stability onset at ~3.5 V vs Li/Li+; in Li||Cu at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, initial CE 86.7% and average CE 91.2% within 80 cycles; polarization 59 mV (10th) and 89 mV (50th); in Li||Li symmetric cells, about 140 h cycle life at 1.0 mA·cm^-2 with voltage reaching 613 mV at 180 h before short circuit, and at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 polarization increased to ~280 mV after 430 h.

Li||Cu at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2: “the initial CE of the F1 electrolyte is only 61.7%”. In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, F1 showed an overpotential of 216 mV and later “reaching 1.30 V after 400 h with the largest interfacial impedance of 50.2 Ω”. These directly report F1’s electrochemical performance as an electrolyte.

F4 is a fluorine-free sulfone-based electrolyte (TMS+EMC). Oxidative stability: although F4 shows a lower oxidation potential than TMS alone, it “can still be used as an electrolyte for 5 V high-voltage lithium metal batteries.” (Section 3.2). Li||Cu: “Since both TMS and EMC are unable to form a stable SEI… there is an obvious ‘diving’ phenomenon for the CE of F4 electrolyte.” At 1.0 mA·cm^-2, “the CE of F1 and F4 electrolytes remains very low” (Section 3.3). Li||Li symmetric: at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, “F4 electrolyte demonstrates the shortest cycle life… the polarization voltage … increased significantly, up to the cut-off voltage at <50 h.” Corresponding interfacial impedances were “125 Ω (1st) and 87.3 Ω (5th)” (Section 3.4). At 1.0 mA·cm^-2, “the polarization voltage of F4 electrolyte dramatically increased to 1.4 V within 100 h.”

F7 electrolyte in Li||LFP cells (2.5–4.2 V): initial discharge specific capacity 155.7 mAh·g−1 at 0.1C (CE 95.9%); after 100 cycles at 0.5C, capacity retention 94.3% and average CE 99.8%. In Li||Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA·cm−2 and 1.0 mAh·cm−2: initial CE 91.6%, average CE 95.9% for 130 cycles (>500 h); overpotential 33 mV (10th) and 43 mV (50th). At 1.0 mA·cm−2: average CE 93.5% for 100 cycles. In Li||Li symmetric cells: cycle life >400 h at 0.5 mA·cm−2 and about 480 h at 1.0 mA·cm−2 with ~60 mV overpotential.

The material serves as a solid polymer electrolyte matrix (FPCSPE) based on crosslinked-P(VEC-co-TFEMA). Reported for FPCSPE 3-40 at room temperature: “FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited the highest σLi+ of 5.02 × 10−5 S cm−1 at room temperature” and “The tLi+ value reached ~0.44 at room temperature.” It also shows a wide electrochemical window up to 5.7 V and stable Li||Li cycling over 300 h at 0.1 mA cm−2, and enables NCM811||Li cells with 218 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and ~70% capacity retention after 300 cycles.

DSLE2 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB=10:2, total salt 1.2 M in FEC). Measurements of ionic conductivity and anodic stability were performed on the DSLE series, but only ranges or DSLE4-specific values are given; no DSLE2-specific performance numbers are reported. Evidence: “The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively, and the total salt concentration was maintained at 1.2 M… EIS… The ionic conductivities of DSLEs were lower than that of LiTFSI/FEC and decreased with increasing LiDFOB content. LSV… DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V)… DSLE4 had the best anodic stability… Therefore, DSLE4 was used for further investigation.” Hence only the application scenario (battery electrolyte) is asserted for DSLE2; other performance fields are left null.

DSLE4 (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 8:4, 1.2 M in FEC) is used as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte for lithium-metal batteries. Measured performance: anodic stability (LSV) up to 4.74 V vs. Li/Li+; Li|Al cells showed no Al corrosion with DSLE4; Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.52; Li|Cu half-cell coulombic efficiency = 95.18%; symmetric Li|DSLE4|Li short-circuited after 938 h; in Li|DSLE4|LCO (3 mg cm^-2, 1 C, 3.0–4.5 V), initial CE = 91.97%, average CE = 99.45%, and 73% capacity retention after 300 cycles.

DSLE6 (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 6:6, total salt 1.2 M) is one of the dual-salt liquid electrolytes prepared for Li-metal batteries. The DSLE series (including DSLE6) showed improved anodic stability (4.57–4.74 V) versus LE and LiTFSI/FEC, and ionic conductivity trends were reported for the DSLEs as a group (conductivity decreased with increasing LiDFOB). However, specific battery performance data (e.g., Coulombic efficiency or cycling) were only provided for DSLE4 and AFPE10, not for DSLE6. Evidence: “The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively… The ionic conductivities of DSLEs were lower than that of LiTFSI/FEC and decreased with increasing LiDFOB content. … DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V) … DSLE4 had the best anodic stability… Therefore, DSLE4 was used for further investigation.”

DSLE8 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 4:8 at 1.2 M in FEC: “The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively, and the total salt concentration was maintained at 1.2 M.” DSLEs (including DSLE8) showed improved anodic stability vs. LE and LiTFSI/FEC, but only a range (4.57–4.74 V) was reported without DSLE8-specific values: “DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V)… DSLE4 had the best anodic stability (up to 4.74 V).” Ionic conductivity of DSLEs decreased with increasing LiDFOB content, but no numeric value for DSLE8 was given. Subsequent battery tests focused on DSLE4 and AFPE10: “Therefore, DSLE4 was used for further investigation.” Hence, no extractable DSLE8-specific performance metrics (CE, capacity retention, energy density, etc.) are provided.

DSLE10 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 2:10 (total salt concentration 1.2 M), but the study proceeds to select DSLE4 for further investigation. Performance and battery tests reported thereafter compare LE, DSLE4, and AFPE10 only. Although a grouped statement notes DSLEs have improved anodic stability (4.57–4.74 V), no DSLE10-specific metrics (ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cycling, CE, etc.) are provided.

AFPE5 is one of the in situ polymerized all-fluorinated polymer electrolytes (AFPEs) designed for high-voltage lithium-metal batteries. LSV shows AFPE5 is stable up to 4.76 V (Fig. 1d), and EIS gives an ionic conductivity of 2.12 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 1e-f). Further electrochemical cycling and CE data were reported only for AFPE10; thus no Li|Cu CE, Li|Li symmetric cycling, or full-cell performance values are available for AFPE5 in the text.

AFPE10 was used as the electrolyte in Li|AFPE10|LCO cells. Reported results directly tied to AFPE10 include: pouch cell initial discharge capacity 52.7 mAh with 94% retention after 50 cycles (Fig. 5b-c); coin cells at 4.5 V showed 92% capacity retention after 500 cycles and average CE 99.82% (Fig. 4a, Table S2); Li|Cu half-cell CE 98.88% (Fig. 2a); energy densities of 507 Wh/kg (14 mg cm^-2) and 546 Wh/kg (25 mg cm^-2) (Fig. 4e and text); electrochemical window 4.89 V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 1d); ionic conductivity 1.88 mS cm^-1 and t_Li+ = 0.61 (Fig. 1f, Fig. S5c).

AFPE20 is one of the prepared all-fluorinated polymer electrolytes (AFPEs). Evidence directly for AFPE20: “Three AFPEs were prepared with 5, 10, and 20 vol.% ... denoted as AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively.” LSV results: “AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20 were stable up to 4.76 V, 4.89 V, and 4.95 V, respectively.” EIS/ionic conductivity: “the AFPEs' ionic conductivities decreased with increasing polymer content, achieving 2.12 mS cm^-1, 1.88 mS cm^-1, and 1.31 mS cm^-1 for AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively (Fig. 1e-f).” No CE/cycling performance was reported for AFPE20 (AFPE10 was used for further investigation).

c-GPE-50 was used as the electrolyte in Li|c-GPE-50|LFP batteries, delivering 134 mAh g^-1 and ultra-long lifespan of 2000 cycles with 78% capacity retention at 2 C (Fig. 2b,c). It also enabled Li|c-GPE-50|NMC622 with 160 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C and 80% retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 5a). Additional electrolyte performance: ionic conductivity 2.36 mS cm^-1 at RT; Li||Li symmetric cell overpotential ~15.2 mV stable over 2800 h; critical current density up to 3 mA cm^-2.

F-HV was used to assemble Li||NCM811 and Li||LFP cells. At a high cut-off voltage of 4.5 V, the Li||NCM811 cell with F-HV shows a high initial discharge capacity of 186.3 mAh g^-1, a high capacity retention rate of 82.3%, and a high average CE of 99.1% after 150 cycles (Fig. 5c). Additional application-relevant performance with F-HV includes: Li||LFP at 1 C and 30 °C shows 144.0 mAh g^-1 initially and 124.4 mAh g^-1 after 3000 cycles (86.3% retention; 99.9% CE) (Fig. 4a); Li||Li symmetric cells exhibit ~128 mV overpotential and 1200 h cycle life (Fig. 3a); electrochemical stability window 4.9 V (Fig. 2b); ionic conductivity 1.97 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C and 4.64 mS cm^-1 at 90 °C (Fig. 2c).

Ionic conductivity for PL at 25°C is 0.5 mScm^{-1} (Figure 2c). Electrochemical stability window (LSV) for PL is 4.78 V (Figure 2e). Critical current density (Li||Li) for PL is 0.65 mAcm^{-2} (Figure 2g). Li||Li symmetric cells with PL exhibit unstable voltage and rapid failure at 0.10 mAcm^{-2} at 25°C (Figure 2h). In LFP||Li full cells at 25°C: Rohm=114 Ω and Rp=437 Ω (Figure 3a); rate capacities: 1C average 125.3 mAhg^{-1}, 2C 74.0 mAhg^{-1}, 3C 48.1 mAhg^{-1} (Figure 3b); long-term cycling at 1C reaches steady specific capacity 128.7 mAhg^{-1} with capacity retention 81.5% (Figure 3e). Activation energy for Li+ conduction in PL is 0.24 eV (Figure 2d).

The PLL slurry without NH4HCO3 addition (PLL-0) suffers from serious gelation and fails to derive a flat and smooth film. Therefore, further investigations about PLL-0 will not be conducted in the following analysis. No electrochemical or battery performance data were reported for PLL-0.

PLL-15 is used as a composite solid electrolyte (CSE) in solid-state Li metal batteries. Reported metrics for PLL-15: ionic conductivity at 25°C is 1.2 mScm^{-1} (Figure 2c); electrochemically stable voltage window is 5.09 V vs Li/Li+ (Figure 2e). Additional application-relevant evidence: critical current density (CCD) is 0.80 mA cm^{-2} (Figure 2g); Li||Li symmetric cell shows stable operation for up to 800 h at 0.1 mA cm^{-2} and 25°C (Figure 2h), and stability up to 600 h at higher current densities of 0.2 and 0.5 mA cm^{-2} (Figure S3); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.54 (Figure S4, Table S2). In LFP||Li full cells at 25°C with PLL-15: rate capacities are 155.7, 148.2, 134.0, 109.0, and 81.9 mAh g^{-1} at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 C, respectively (Figure 3b); long-term cycling at 1 C shows steady specific discharge capacity of 135.8 mAh g^{-1} with 95.4% retention at the 240th cycle (Figure 3e); ohmic and polarization resistances are R_ohm = 32 Ω and R_p = 246 Ω (Figure 3a).

Ionic conductivity for PLL-25: "The ionic conductivities at 25°C for PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.5, 1.2, and 0.6 mScm^-1, respectively." Electrochemical stability window: "The electrochemically stable voltages of the PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 4.78, 5.09, and 4.96 V, respectively." Critical current density: "The CCD values of PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.65, 0.80, and 0.45 mAcm^-2, respectively." Symmetric cell stability: "Li symmetric cells with PL and PLL-25 exhibit unstable voltage over the cycling period and experience rapid failure."

E-FPAESI was fabricated into single-mode optical waveguides and tested at 1.55 μm: "Loss measurements were performed using a laser diode with the 1.55 μm wavelength." and "Propagation loss of the straight waveguides was measured by the cut-back method." Figure 5: "The propagation losses, which were obtained from the slope, are less than 0.42 and 0.40 dB/cm for the TE and TM polarization, respectively. This result indicated that the E-FPAESI is a good candidate as a core material for the optical waveguide devices." Conclusions: "The optical loss was measured to be less than 0.40 dB/cm."

No direct application performance data are reported for FPAESI. Optical waveguide fabrication and propagation loss measurements were performed on E-FPAESI, not FPAESI: "To fabricate single-mode optical waveguides ... E-FPAESI film was spin-coated ..." and "The propagation losses ... are less than 0.42 and 0.40 dB/cm for the TE and TM polarization, respectively. This result indicated that the E-FPAESI is a good candidate as a core material for the optical waveguide devices." Additionally, FPAESI is noted as soluble in common solvents ("FPEASI was soluble in common solvents such as acetone, tetrahydrofuran, DMF, and DMSO."), while insoluble cross-linked films required ethynyl termination and curing (E-FPAESI). Therefore, no application metrics (e.g., optical loss) specific to FPAESI can be extracted.

HBPS-(PTFEMA-b-PPEGMA)27 was fabricated into all-solid-state polymer electrolyte membranes with LiTFSI. The highest room-temperature ionic conductivity reached 2.4×10^-5 S/cm at [EO]/[Li]=30 (Section 3.5, Fig. 4(a)). At 80°C, conductivity reached 4.1×10^-4 S/cm under the same [EO]/[Li] (Section 3.5). The Li+ transference number was 0.26 at 60°C for [EO]/[Li]=30 (Table 2; Fig. 5(a)). The oxidative stability window extended to 4.9 V vs. Li/Li+ at 25°C (Section 3.7; Fig. 5(b)). In Li/SPE/LiFePO4 cells, the electrolyte enabled an initial capacity of 139 mAh/g and a maximum of 147 mAh/g at 0.1 C and 60°C with ~100% coulombic efficiency and stable cycling over 100 cycles (Section 3.9; Fig. 7). Thermal decomposition onset of the polymer was 374.7°C (Section 3.4; Fig. 3(c)).

For FPE-6 specifically: Li/FPE-6/Li symmetric cell possesses the lowest interface resistance at 426.9 Ω and can stably cycle over 1000 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 without short circuit; FPE-6 shows a lithium ion transference number of 0.46 and an electrochemical stability window of 5.0 V. In LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li full cells, the first-cycle discharge capacity is 154.2 mAh/g at 0.1C; long-time cycling at 0.5C gives ~99.9% coulombic efficiency and 86.5% capacity retention after 424 cycles over 1350 h; rate performance decreases from 149.3 mAh/g at 0.2C to 103.9 mAh/g at 3C and recovers to 148.0 mAh/g at 0.2C with 99.13% capacity retention.

PE-6 is the non-fluorinated polymer electrolyte reference used in Li/PE-6/Li and LiFePO4/PE-6/Li cells. The text states: "the calculated lithium ion transference number (LTN) of FPE-6 is 0.46 ... and it is higher than 0.28 of PE-6 (Fig. S18)." It also notes Li/PE-6/Li shows unstable stripping/plating, but without numeric values.

FPDE is used as a fluorinated deep eutectic gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Key FPDE-specific performances: ionic conductivity 0.97 mS cm^-1 (HFBA:DES=1:3); electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V; Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.58. Li/FPDE/Li symmetric cells show stable plating/stripping for ~6000 h and reach critical current density of 1 mA cm^-2. In Li/FPDE/LFP full cells at 0.2C, initial discharge specific capacity is 150.6 mAh g^-1 with 96.7% initial Coulombic efficiency; after 600 cycles capacity is 121.1 mAh g^-1 (80.4% retention). Rate capacities with FPDE: 155.4 (0.1C), 151.8 (0.2C), 146.3 mAh g^-1 (0.5C), and recovery to 156.5 mAh g^-1 when returning to 0.1C. Safety: nonflammable, SET = 0 s g^-1. Self-healing within ~1 h at room temperature and improved mechanical strength are also reported.

PVDF (Dense) membranes (solution casting) were used as gel polymer electrolytes (GPE) for Li-ion batteries. Reported metrics specific to PVDF (Dense): Table I lists porosity 0% and electrolyte uptake 27%. The ion conductivities of GPEs for dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like membranes were 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1, and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively. No LSV window, battery capacity, or cycling data were reported for the dense PVDF; those were provided only for porous or blended membranes.

For PVDF (LIPS): “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively.” Also: “GPE with finger-like morphology anodic stability was as high as 4.4 V.” In coin-cell tests: “The cell assembled with this GPE … higher than the GPE with a finger-like morphology structure (26 mA hg−1) [at 1C].” Table I lists PVDF (LIPS) porosity 53% and electrolyte uptake 490%.

PVDF membranes prepared via the VIPS method (cellular/honeycomb-like morphology) were used as gel polymer electrolytes (GPEs) in Li/GPE/SS and Li/GPE/LiMn2O4 cells. For conductivity tests, “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively.” The same VIPS PVDF GPE showed anodic stability “up to 5.0 V versus Li+/Li.” In rate testing of LiMn2O4/GPE/Li, the PVDF GPE with cellular morphology (VIPS) exhibited stable reversible capacities of 158, 140, 120, and 75 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C, respectively.

The PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (70/30) blend membrane was selected for further electrochemical analysis. Its ionic conductivity was reported among compositions as: "the ionic conductivity of PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA blend membranes with compositions of 100:0, 70:30, and 50:50 was calculated to be 0.9 mS cm−1, 1.9 mS cm−1 and 2.8 mS cm−1". Table I lists PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (70/30) with electrolyte uptake 347% and porosity 48%. LSV shows the electrochemical stability of the PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (70/30) GPE to be 5.2 V (vs Li+/Li). The Li+/LiMn2O4 half-cells with this GPE delivered discharge capacities of 168, 150, 130, and 85 mAh g−1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C, respectively, and retained 88% (132 mAh g−1) after 50 cycles at 0.2C. The Li+ transference number t_Li+ was ~0.82.

For PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (50/50): Table I reports porosity 37% and electrolyte uptake 371%. In Results, the ionic conductivity for compositions 100:0, 70:30, and 50:50 was calculated to be 0.9, 1.9, and 2.8 mS cm−1, respectively. The LSV stability window (5.2 V) and battery cycling data were only provided for the 70:30 composition; the 50:50 membrane showed surface cracks and was not used for further electrochemical testing.

Pure PFPE ionic conductivity at 60 °C is reported as 1.55 × 10^−11 S/cm: "The fluorinated ether polymer blend electrolyte has 6 orders of magnitude difference in conductivity as compared to pure PFPE from 1.55 × 10^−11 to 2.26 × 10^−5 S/cm at 60 °C" (Conclusions). Additionally, the pure PFPE r = 0.05 electrolyte did not support Li|Li cycling: "The PFPE r = 0.05 electrolyte did not support LiLi cycling as the rigid polymer could not form a good interface with lithium metal ... and the cell quickly shorted" (Results, Electrochemical performance).

Electrochemical performance of FPCE-based full cells using LFP||Li at RT: "After 500 cycles at 1 C, the FPCE exhibited a capacity of 114.2 mAh g^-1" (Figure 4c). Rate performance with FPCE: "169.2, 158.5, 146.0, 129.7, and 104.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 C" (Figures 4a, S21). Additional FPCE-related metrics: Li|FPCE|Cu CE remained 98.25% after 300 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3e); Li|FPCE|Li symmetric cell operated >1100 h (Figure 3f); LFP|FPCE|Li sustained 5000 cycles at 10 C with 0.00448% per-cycle decay (Figure 4d); NCM811|FPCE|Li capacity retention 98.62% after 100 cycles (Figure 4f); pouch cell with FPCE: 0.78 Ah initial, 0.62 Ah after 600 cycles at RT (Figure 5b).

The copolymer P(VEC-co-TFEMA) is used as a solid polymer electrolyte matrix (FPCSPE). Reported properties for FPCSPE 3-40 at room temperature: highest ionic conductivity σ = 5.02×10^-5 S cm^-1 (Fig. 3, text: “FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited the highest σ_Li+ of 5.02×10^-5 Scm^-1 at room temperature”), and Li-ion transference number t_Li+ ≈ 0.44 (Fig. 3f, text: “The t_Li+ value reached ∼0.44 at room temperature”). Additional application-relevant evidence includes electrochemical window up to 5.7 V (Fig. 4a,b), stable Li||Li cycling >300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~0.05 V overpotential (Fig. 4c), and NCM811||FPCSPE||Li cell performance (218 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C, ~70% retention after 300 cycles) demonstrating suitability as a battery electrolyte (Fig. 5d,e).

Electrolyte P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI is used in all-solid-state sodium batteries. Reported metrics include: ionic conductivity of P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI was calculated to be 2.23 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 (Fig. 3a); the Na+ transference number for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI is 0.7 (Fig. 3b); the ESW of P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI remains around 5.55 V (Fig. 3f). Na/P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI/Na symmetric cell sustained stable cycling for 4690 h without short circuit with ~230 mV polarization at 0.1 mA cm^−2, RT (Fig. 4a). In NVP/P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI/Na full cells, capacity retention is 96.59% (99.1 mAh g^−1) and CE > 99.5% after 200 cycles at 0.1C; rate capacities are 102.2, 98.1, 81.4, and 39.4 mAh g^−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1C, respectively (Fig. 5). Activation energy Ea for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI is 0.279 eV (Fig. 3c).

The material P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly mentioned when comparing PEGMEA:TFEMA ratios: “Specifically, when the ratio of PEGMEA to TFEMA was 1:2, the insulated TFEMA increased the impedance, inhibiting the movement of sodium ions… Thus, the optimal ratio… was 1:1.” (Fig. 3a and text). No numeric ionic conductivity, t_Na+, ESW, or cycling data are reported for the 1:2 sample; therefore performance fields are left null.

The material P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly prepared as a composition variant (Section 2.2). However, quantitative performance results (e.g., ionic conductivity 2.23×10^-4 S cm^-1, t_Na+ = 0.7, ESW ~5.55 V, 4690 h Na//Na cycling, NVP cell capacities) are reported for P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI without specifying the 2:1 ratio or are attributed to the 1:1 optimum (Sections 3.2–3.4). Fig. 3a presents EIS spectra for various PEGMEA:TFEMA ratios (including 2:1) but provides no numeric readouts for the 2:1 sample. Therefore, no quantitative metrics can be uniquely assigned to P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI.

Electrochemical stability window (ESW) explicitly reported for PEGMEA-NaTFSI: "The ESW of the P-co-F-NaTFSI electrolyte surpasses 5.62 V, in contrast to the 4.53 V observed for PEGMEA-NaTFSI (Figure S10)." Additionally, PEGMEA-NaTFSI shows a nucleation overpotential of 110 mV (Figure S11), and its t_Na+ is lower than P-co-F-NaTFSI (0.41) but without a numeric value for PEGMEA-NaTFSI.

For PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI: (1) Na//Na symmetric cell: “a short circuit occurred after 536 h of charging/discharging cycling for Na/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na battery” at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and room temperature (Fig. 4a). (2) Full cell NVP/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na: “delivers a high initial discharge capacity of 101.3 mAh/g … ultimately undergoes a direct short circuit at the 67th cycle” (Fig. 5a). No numeric ionic conductivity, transference number, or ESW values were given for PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI.

Direct statements for P-co-F-NaTFSI: "The ionic conductivity of P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI was ... an order of magnitude increase in comparison to P-co-F-NaTFSI (2.63 × 10^-5 S cm^-1, by Figure S8a)." and "The sodium-ion transference number (t_Na+) for P-co-F-NaTFSI stands at 0.41...". Additional property (not mapped to fields): "The ESW of the P-co-F-NaTFSI electrolyte surpasses 5.62 V."

PPP is used as a PFPE derivative additive in DOL-based electrolytes for lithium metal batteries (3 wt% PPP in 1 M LiFSI/DOL). With PPP, the LFP | DOL-PPP | Li cell maintained a specific capacity of 136.6 mAh g^-1 after 150 cycles at 0.5C with 94.1% retention and 0.039% capacity decay per cycle. Supporting evidence of improved electrolyte properties due to PPP includes: Li | DOL-PPP | Li t+ = 0.61 (vs 0.42 for DOL), extended electrochemical stability window to 4.2 V, and stable polarization even after 1800 h cycling at 0.2 mA cm^-2 in Li | DOL-PPP | Li symmetric cells.

Electrolyte composition uses PPP in DOL: "3 wt% PPP (relative to the DOL monomer) was added to a 1 M LiFSI DOL solution". Performance with DOL-PPP electrolyte: "After 150 cycles at 0.5C, the LFP | DOL-PPP | Li cell maintained a specific capacity of 136.6 mAh g^{-1} ... resulting in a capacity retention of 94.1%." The material DOL-PPP is the electrolyte in these cells.

FBCP_20 was used to prepare an electrolyte film (FBCPE_20) with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li]=20:1 (Section 2.4, 3.4). Ionic conductivities of the three FBCPEs, including FBCPE_20, are presented in Fig. 6a, but only a numeric value for FBCPE_30 is reported; no specific conductivity value is given for FBCPE_20. Battery cycling and ESW performance were evaluated for FBCPE_30, not for FBCPE_20.

FBCP_30 is used to prepare FBCPE_30 electrolyte films (LiTFSI, [EO]/[Li] = 20:1). Reported: “FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^-4 S cm-1) at 70 °C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” The Li+ transference number: “As shown in Fig. 6b, the tLi+ was calculated to be 0.41.”

FBCP_40 is used to prepare FBCPE_40 (LiTFSI-doped) as a solid-state electrolyte for Li metal batteries. The paper presents ionic conductivity curves for FBCPE_20/30/40 (Fig. 6a) but provides a numeric value only for FBCPE_30 (2.68×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70°C). Other electrochemical metrics (t_Li+, ESW, Li/Li symmetric cycling, and full-cell performance with LiFePO4 or NCM622) are reported exclusively for FBCPE_30. Therefore, no explicit numerical performance values specific to FBCP_40 (or FBCPE_40) are given beyond qualitative inclusion in the conductivity plot.

P-FEM@Li was used as a lithium-metal anode with an artificial SEI, delivering practical cell performance: "The pouch cells achieved a specific energy of 428 Wh kg^{-1} and P-FEM@Li||NCM811 pouch cell maintained a capacity retention of 73% after 175 cycles at 0.2C charge/0.5C discharge". Additional evidence: Symmetric cell with P-FEM@Li cycled stably for >4400 h at 1 mA cm^{-2}/1 mAh cm^{-2}; Li||LFP full cells: initial 146 mAh g^{-1} at 1C, 127.7 mAh g^{-1} after 600 cycles (87.2% retention).

Li||Cu cells using P-FEM@Cu were tested for Li deposition/stripping. Under 1 mAcm^-2 and 1 mAhcm^-2, the cyclic CE for P-FEM@Cu showed almost no attenuation within 270 cycles, and the average CE was 99.78% after 100 cycles (Figures 3d–e).

FP-GPE is used as the gel polymer electrolyte in lithium metal batteries. Practical device data: the first reported solid-state 18650 cylindrical LMBs with FP-GPE (NCM811|Li, N/P=2.5) deliver a high capacity of 1.0 Ah and an energy density of 158.5 Wh kg-1 with 95.0% capacity retention after 30 cycles (Figure 3f,g). Electrolyte performance underpinning this application includes: ionic conductivity 4.45 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (Figure 1b), high-voltage stability with no remarkable oxidation up to 4.9 V vs Li/Li+ (Figure 1a), and high critical current density (CCD) of 4.5 mA cm-2 with Li||Li symmetric cells stable for 700 h (Figure S13). Cell-level cycling with FP-GPE shows ultralong life and high retention (e.g., NCM622|FP-GPE|Li: 1200 cycles with 80.1% retention; 1500 cycles with 71.5%, Figure 1f; Table 1), compatibility with multiple cathodes (NCM811 86.0% after 1000 cycles; LCO 79.7% after 1500 cycles; LFP 93.6% after 4000 cycles; Table 1), operation across -25 to 80 °C, including ionic conductivity 1.12 mS cm-1 at -25 °C and NCM622 cell initial 115.2 mAh g-1 with 78.4% retention after 200 cycles (Figure 4a,b). Safety: FP-GPE is nonflammable (Figure S6) and enables safe nail-penetration behavior in pouch and 18650 cells (Figure 4c–j). These data directly evidence FP-GPE’s application as a battery electrolyte and support the capacity value chosen from the industrially relevant 18650 cell.

P-0.5 is explicitly prepared and labeled as a solid electrolyte: “DESs were added … 0.5, 0.6, 0.7, and 0.8 mL… The solid electrolyte obtained was labeled as P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC.” FTIR includes P-0.5 among electrolytes: “FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC…”. However, the paper reports specific electrochemical performance values only for other samples (e.g., P-0.8, P-0.8-FEC), and no numeric performance metrics are provided for P-0.5.

P-0.6 is prepared and characterized but no specific electrochemical performance data are reported for it. Evidence: "The solid electrolyte obtained was labeled as P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC." (Experimental Section); "e) FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC…" (Figure 1 caption). Quantitative performance is given for other samples: "At room temperature, the ionic conductivity of P-0.8 reached 0.313 mScm^{-1}" and stability/cycling data for P-0.8-FEC (e.g., LSV to 4.6 V, Li/Li symmetric 500 h), but none specifically for P-0.6.

P-0.7 is one of the DES-modified PVDF-HFP solid polymer electrolytes intended for Li-metal solid-state batteries (e.g., FTIR includes P-0.7 in Figure 1e; Figure 3a discusses ionic conductivities of different DEEs). However, quantified performance values are only reported for P-0.8 and/or P-0.8-FEC (e.g., ionic conductivity, activation energy, LSV limit, cycling), not for P-0.7. Therefore only the application scenario (battery electrolyte for Li+ transport) can be assigned; numeric fields are left null.
At room temperature, the ionic conductivity of P-0.8 reached 0.313 mScm−1 (Figure 3a).

P-0.8-FEC is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li/LFP cells. The Li/P-0.8-FEC/LFP button cell delivered 139.9 mAh g^-1 after 500 cycles at 25 °C and 1 C with 97.8% capacity retention (Figure 5a,b). Additional P-0.8-FEC-specific performance: LSV stability up to 4.6 V (Figure 3d); Li/P-0.8-FEC/Li symmetric cells cycled stably for 500 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3e); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.435 (Figure 3g); self-extinguishing in 0.3 s upon open flame (Figure 2d). Rate capacities with P-0.8-FEC: 166.3, 156.6, 147.4, 131.7, 113.8, and 82.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 C, respectively, and recovery to 164.4 mAh g^-1 when returning to 0.1 C (Figure 5b,c).

P-L is a PVDF-HFP-based solid-state electrolyte with LiTFSI (no DESs/FEC). In Li/P-L/Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2, P-L short-circuited after only 30 h (Figure 3e), indicating poor interfacial stability in the battery electrolyte application. Room-temperature ionic conductivity is only described relatively (P-0.8 = 0.313 mS cm^-1 is ~2x P-L), so no numeric conductivity is given for P-L. The Arrhenius activation energy for P-L is reported as 0.183 eV (Figure 3c), but no explicit electrochemical window value is provided for P-L (only that P-0.8-FEC reaches 4.6 V).

PFMC-SPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li-based cells: Li/SPE/Li, Li/SPE/SS, Li/PFMC-SPE/LFP, and Li/PFMC-SPE/NCM622. The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS/cm. It also shows tLi+ = 0.57, electrochemical stability up to ~5 V, CCD = 0.8 mA cm−2, and enables Li symmetric cycling (~20 mV overpotential for 800 h) and Li//LFP full-cell cycling (119.6 mAh g−1 after 750 cycles at 1C).

FMC-SPE (excessive fluorinated meltblown cloth solid polymer electrolyte) was evaluated as the electrolyte in Li/SPE/Li and Li/SPE/SS cells. Reported performance specific to FMC-SPE includes: ionic conductivity 1.1 mS/cm at 25 °C (Fig. 3a); Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.21 (Figs. S7, S8); oxidation onset at 4.25 V in LSV (Fig. 3c); leakage current exceeding 100 μA at 4.3 V in floating test due to oxidation of residual monomer (Fig. 3d); critical current density (CCD) = 0.5 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 4b); in Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA/cm^2, after 50 h the overpotential increases from ~50 mV to ~5 V indicating unstable Li plating/stripping (Fig. 4d,f). These confirm its application as a solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries.

NFMC-SPE is a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported performance specific to NFMC-SPE includes: ionic conductivity 0.5 mS/cm at 25 °C (“The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS/cm, which is much higher than that of the NfMC-SPE (0.5 mS/cm at 25 °C).”). Additional NFMC-specific metrics (not mapped to fields): Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.19 (Fig. S8); oxidation onset at 4.7 V in LSV (“oxidation occurred at 4.25 V (FMC-SPE) and 4.7 V (NFMC-SPE)”); critical current density CCD = 0.35 mA/cm^2; in Li/Li symmetric cycling at 0.1 mA/cm^2 the NFMC-SPE cell short-circuited after 32 h; activation energy Ea = 0.28 eV.

FPASEI is applied as an artificial SEI for Li metal anodes and shows battery-relevant performance: electronic resistivity ~2.17×10^4 Ω cm and ionic conductivity 0.87×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 2d,e); Li+ transference number increases to 0.77 from 0.31 (Fig. 2h,i); exchange current density I0 = 0.274 mA cm^-2 for FPASEI-Li vs 0.127 mA cm^-2 for bare-Li (Fig. 2f); Li||Li symmetric cell with FPASEI-Li cycles stably >500 h at 1 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2 at 30 °C, and up to 900 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3a and S9); Li||Cu average Coulombic efficiency 97.88% with FPASEI-Cu vs 85.04% for bare-Cu (Fig. S8); FPASEI-Li||NCM811 full cell (8.3 mg cm^-2 loading) retains 80% capacity after 265 cycles (Fig. 5d,e); at 70 °C, FPASEI-Li||NCM811 retains 80% capacity after 120 cycles with CE ~99% (Fig. 5f,g). These metrics are directly tied to the FPASEI layer on Li anodes.

HCE-specific results: "both HCGPE-PE and HCE exhibit high CE values of ≈ 99.3%" (Figure 3a,b). In Li/Li symmetric batteries at 2.0 mA cm−2 / 1.0 mAh cm−2, "the symmetric battery with HCE experiences a short circuit after ≈ 740 h" (Figure 3j).

For HCGPE (gel polymer electrolyte itself), EIS shows ionic conductivity reaches 4.8 mS cm−1 at 30 °C (Figures S13, S14). The Li-ion transference number tLi+ of HCGPE is 0.56 (Figure S15). LSV indicates an oxidation peak at ≈3.9 V and higher onset oxidation stability than HCE (Figure 1h). Tafel plots report exchange current density of 0.518 mA cm−2 for HCGPE (Figure S22). Performance such as ≈99.3% CE and 950 h symmetric-cell cycling are for HCGPE-PE (with the ultrathin PE separator) and are not attributed solely to HCGPE.

The paper discusses POE as a Li+-conducting solid polymer electrolyte: "In all-solid-state cells, the POE would be employed as a Li+-conducting electrolyte and polymer binder on cathode films..." For C5-POE (without trifluoroacetate), oxidative stability was evaluated in LSV: "the C5-POE-Li counterpart, without the trifluoroacetate terminating unit, show a sharp oxidating peak starting at 4.8 V" (Figure 6d). No Li+ conductivity values were reported for C5-POE(-Li) specifically; conductivity and cell performance data were provided for C5-POE-F(-Li).

POE-F is used as a Li+-conducting solid polymer electrolyte: “In all-solid-state cells, the POE would be employed as a Li+-conducting electrolyte and polymer binder on cathode films” and “the mixtures of POE-F and LiTFSI … were cast … with the formation of membranes …” The study repeatedly evaluates “the conductivities of these POE-F-Li membranes” and their electrochemical behavior in Li cells (e.g., CV tests and Li/Li symmetric cells) demonstrating electrolyte functionality and interfacial stability. Specific numerical conductivities and high-voltage limits provided in the text are for C5-POE-F-Li (a subset of POE-F), so no quantitative value is assigned for generic POE-F.

C5-POE-F was used as a solid polymer electrolyte (with LiTFSI, 2.5:1 wt) and cathode binder in all-solid-state Li/C5-POE-F-Li/NMC811 cells. At 45 °C and 0.1 mA/cm^2, the cell showed a characteristic NMC811 profile with initial discharge 175 mAh/g and increased to 190.5 mAh/g by the 6th cycle; capacities of 157.7 mAh/g (100th) and 115.6 mAh/g (200th) with CE ~99.8% thereafter (Figure 9a–b). The C5-POE-F-Li membrane exhibited Li+ conductivity of 3.6×10^-5 S/cm at 45 °C (1.7×10^-4 S/cm at 65 °C) at POE-F:LiTFSI=2.5:1 (Figure 4d). Electrochemical stability: no obvious oxidation until >5.0 V in LSV (Figure 6c–d). Li/C5-POE-F-Li/Li symmetric cells showed stable plating/stripping for 320 h at 0.35 mA/cm^2 with ~±400 mV overpotential and no significant impedance rise (Figure 7a–b).

Naming and composition include the specific sample: "the SPE membranes containing varying weight of SN were named PBA-PVDF-0.2SN, PBA-PVDF-0.3SN, PBA-PVDF-0.4SN and PBA-PVDF-0.5SN" (Section 2.1). Performance plots (Fig. 3a) compare various SN loadings, which include 0.2SN, but the text provides explicit numeric values only for other cases (e.g., PBA-PVDF without SN and PBA-PVDF-0.4SN). The authors state: "Considering its optimal ion transport performance, the PBA-PVDF-0.4SN electrolyte was chosen for further investigations" (Section 3.3), so subsequent metrics are not specific to 0.2SN. Therefore, no numerical performance specific to PBA-PVDF-0.2SN is reported in the text; values are only shown in comparative curves without tabulated/readable numbers.

The electrolyte series explicitly includes PBA-PVDF-0.3SN: "the SPE membranes containing varying weight of SN were named PBA-PVDF-0.2SN, PBA-PVDF-0.3SN, PBA-PVDF-0.4SN and PBA-PVDF-0.5SN". Performance measurements relevant to Li+ transport (ionic conductivity and battery tests) are presented across the series (e.g., Fig. 3a Arrhenius plot for various SN loadings), but specific numeric values are only stated for other compositions (e.g., 0.4SN with 4.89 mS cm^-1). No direct, quantitated performance metrics are reported specifically for PBA-PVDF-0.3SN; therefore, only the application scenario (solid-state Li+ electrolyte) can be asserted.
