PCG1 SPE is explicitly prepared and tested as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries: “PVDF/PCL-PEG-PCL/LiTFSI SPEs, abbreviated as PCG SPEs, were prepared (according to different C=O:Li molar ratios, samples were named respectively PCG1 SPE, PCG5 SPE, PCG10 SPE).” Additionally, PCG1 is included in electrochemical evaluations: “Lithium-ion transference number results for PCG1 (molar ratio C=O:Li = 1:1) SPE were shown in Figure S3.” and “The cycling performance of PCG0 SPE, PCG1 SPE, PCG5 SPE, and PCG10 SPE at 1 C … is presented in Figure 6g.” No numerical performance values for PCG1 SPE are reported in the text body; values appear only in figures/SI without tabulated numbers.

PCG5 SPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Reported performance for PCG5 SPE includes: (1) ionic conductivity: "the room temperature ionic conductivity of PCG5 SPE is measured at 1.4×10−3 S cm−1 which increases to 2.5×10−3 S cm−1 at 70 °C." (2) lithium-ion transference number: "Compared to PCG0 SPE (tLi+ = 0.34), ... PCG5 SPE (0.63) was gained in Figure 2e." (3) electrochemical stability window: "PCG5 SPE demonstrated the optimal electrochemical window (5 V vs Li/Li+)." (4) Li|PCG5 SPE|Li symmetric cell stability: "maintaining an overpotential of 50 mV and cycling stably over 1800 h" at 0.2 mA cm−2 and 25 °C. (5) Full-cell performance with LiFePO4 cathode: rate capability "The capacity of LiFePO4|PCG5 SPE|Li at 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 C current density is 156, 150, 142, 127, and 95 mAh g−1, respectively... the capacity rebounds to 152 mAh g−1 when the current is reduced to 0.1 C"; cycling at 1 C: "achieves a peak capacity of 168.0 mAh g−1 after the activation process of the first five cycles. Even after 500 cycles, the capacity remains at 138.2 mAh g−1"; cycling at 2 C: "The capacity of the LiFePO4|PCG5 SPE|Li cell was at 122.0 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles (a capacity retention rate exceeding 91.3% and an average Coulombic efficiency of approximately 99.8%)." (6) High-voltage cathode compatibility: "the NCM811| PCG5 SPE|Li cell showed stable cycling over 50 cycles, with a specific capacity of 170 mAh g−1 for the first cyclic discharge, and a capacity retention of 90.7% after 50 cycles."

PCG10 SPE is evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. The text states: "Notably, the PCG10 (molar ratio C = O:Li = 10:1) SPE exhibits the highest ionic conductivity" and that for PCG10 "its activation energy is rather larger than that of PCG5 SPE". No numerical conductivity, transference number, ESW, or cell-performance values are provided for PCG10 in the text; detailed electrochemical tests and metrics are reported for PCG5 instead.

Reported for PCG0 SPE (PVDF/LiTFSI): room-temperature ionic conductivity is 1.8×10^-4 S cm^-1; lithium-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.34. Source: “the room temperature ionic conductivity of PCG0 SPE is only 1.8×10^-4 S cm^-1.” and “Compared to PCG0 SPE (tLi+ = 0.34) …”.

PEMVH-A is defined as the composite GPE membrane with nano-sized Al2O3 filler. In Table 2(a) (GPEs immersed in 1 M LiPF6/EC-DMC), PEMVH-A shows an ionic conductivity at 25 °C of 0.91 mS cm-1 (with liquid absorbability 96%, Rb 4.40 Ω, and t_Li+ = 0.75). These data evidence its application as a Li+ conducting gel polymer electrolyte.

Table 2 (Li battery): PEMVH-T shows ionic conductivity at 25 °C of 1.27 mS cm-1 and Li+ transport number tLi+ = 0.79 (liquid absorbability 101%, Rb 3.15 Ω). Fig. 4(b): Li/GPE/LiCoO2 cell with PEMVH-T gives initial discharge capacity 155.1 mAh g-1 and 150.1 mAh g-1 after 100 cycles (97% retention). Fig. 4(a): Li symmetric cell with PEMVH-T shows ~60 mV initially (unstable first ~80 h), then ~20 mV and stable thereafter.

PEMVH-S denotes the SiO2-filled PEMVH gel polymer electrolyte. Table 2(a) reports for PEMVH-S in 1 M LiPF6/EC–DMC: ionic conductivity at 25 °C = 2.02 mS cm-1 and Li+ transport number tLi+ = 0.84. LSV shows PEMVH-S//LiPF6/EC–DMC is stable up to 5.3 V vs Li/Li+. Symmetric Li/PEMVH-S/Li cells at 0.25 mA cm-2 exhibit ~10 mV overpotential stable for 1200 h. In Li full cells (Li/GPE/LiCoO2), initial discharge capacity 155.5 mAh g-1 with 99% retention after 100 cycles at 0.1 mA cm-2. For Na systems (also PEMVH-S), Table 2(b) shows 0.88 mS cm-1 and tNa+ = 0.86; LSV oxidation stability up to 4.9 V vs Na/Na+; symmetric Na cells show ~380 mV stable for 600 h; Na/GPE/Na3V2(PO4)2F3 cells: 106.7→98.9 mAh g-1 after 100 cycles (93% retention).
Fig. 2/Section 3.1: "PMMA-60 can entrap as much as 234 wt% of liquid." The membrane is used to immobilize the ionic liquid [BMIM]BF4 for lithium polymer battery electrolytes.

MIL-60 is used as the gel polymer electrolyte in Li/MIL-60/LiFePO4 coin cells at 30 °C. The first-discharge capacities are 157, 142, and 137 mAh·g−1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, and 0.5C, respectively (Fig. 8a). The electrolyte shows ionic conductivity ~1.4×10−3 S·cm−1 at 30 °C (Section 3.3), a stable electrochemical window up to 4.5 V vs Li+/Li (Section 3.6), and good interfacial stability with Li (R_intf stabilizes ~900 Ω by 144 h; Fig. 6). Cycling retention at 50th cycle is 96% (0.1C) and 95% (0.2C) (Fig. 8b).

GPE-1 is defined as PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) with EC: DEC (1:1) and NaCF3SO3 (Table I). Electrochemical impedance analysis reports Rb for GPE-1 as 50 Ω, and Table III lists its ionic conductivity as 1.0 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (with t_ion = 0.98 and t_Na+ = 0.36; Ea = 0.28 eV). These data demonstrate its application as a sodium-ion battery gel polymer electrolyte with measured ionic conductivity.

GPE-2 (PVDF-HFP: PMMA - EC: DEC - NaClO4) was used as the electrolyte in Na-ion half-cells. With a hard carbon anode, the cell demonstrated a stable charge/discharge capacity of 190 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles (~0.3C). The material showed highest ionic conductivity among prepared GPEs (1.2×10^-3 S cm^-1 at RT) and an electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V, supporting its suitability as a sodium-ion battery electrolyte. It was also tested with a CuO–TiO2 anode, delivering ~125 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles at ~0.2C.

GPE-3 (PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) + EC:DEC (1:1) + 0.5 M NaBF4) is a gel polymer electrolyte for sodium-ion batteries. Table III reports for GPE-3: ionic conductivity 7.4 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (room temperature), t_ion = 0.99, t_Na+ = 0.27, activation energy 0.30 eV. EIS bulk resistance for GPE-3 was 63 Ω. LSV indicates the GPEs are stable up to 4.8 V (applies to all prepared GPEs). Battery cycling demonstrations were performed with GPE-2, not GPE-3.

GPE-4 is defined as PVDF-HFP: PMMA (9:1) with EC:DEC plasticizer and NaPF6 salt (Table I). Table III reports for GPE-4: conductivity = 3.9 × 10^-4 S cm^-1, activation energy 0.33 eV, t_ion = 0.95, t_Na+ = 0.25. The study states that LSV confirmed the GPEs are stable up to 4.8 V. Battery half-cell performance was only demonstrated for GPE-2; no coin-cell data were provided for GPE-4.
For the LFP/iHSE/Li solid-state battery, at a discharge rate of 0.1C the battery attained a discharge-specific capacity of 159 mAh·g−1 (Fig. 6a; Section 3.3).

Sample (a) is the PVDF-HFP/TPU/PMMA fibrous membrane without in situ SiO2 fillers. After activation as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE), its ionic conductivity is reported as 4.2×10^-3 Scm^-1: “We could calculate ionic conductivity by eqn (3), the gel polymer electrolyte film has an ionic conductivity of 4.2×10^-3 Scm^-1 (GPE without in situ SiO2).” Electrolyte uptake for this membrane is 236 wt% within 2 min and reaches 337% after 15 min: “The fibrous film without in situ SiO2 has liquid electrolyte uptake up to 236 wt% within 2 min … reaches up to 337%.” Electrochemical stability window is 5.4 V: “the electrochemical stability of gel polymer electrolyte without in situ SiO2 is at 5.4 V.” In Li/GPE/LiFePO4 cell testing with this GPE, first-cycle charge/discharge are 164.84/162.15 mAh g^-1: “The gel polymer electrolyte film (GPE without in situ SiO2) delivers a charge capacity of 164.84 mAh g^-1 and discharge capacity of 162.15 mAh g^-1.”

Sample (b) is the PVDF-HFP/TPU/PMMA membrane with 1% in situ SiO2. Reported performance for this specific GPE includes: ionic conductivity 6.8×10^-3 S cm^-1 ("the gel polymer electrolyte film has an ionic conductivity of ... 6.8×10^-3 Scm^-1 (GPE with 1% in situ SiO2)"); electrolyte uptake 360% at 15 min ("... 360% (the membrane with 1% in situ SiO2)"); electrochemical stability 5.8 V ("The gel polymer electrolyte delivers a electrochemical stability of 5.8 V (GPE with 1% in situ SiO2)"); cell first charge/discharge capacities with Li/LiFePO4: 166.80/164.98 mAh g^-1 ("... 166.80 mAh g^-1 (GPE with 1% in situ SiO2) ... 164.98 mAh g^-1 (GPE with 1% in situ SiO2)"). Ionic conductivity is used as the primary metric.

Ionic conductivity (room temperature) reported for the PVDF-HFP/TPU/PMMA gel polymer electrolyte with 2% in situ SiO2: "8.5×10^-3 S cm^-1 (GPE with 2% in situ SiO2)" (Fig. 6 and text). Additional related performance for the same sample: electrochemical stability window up to 5.9 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 7), electrolyte uptake 395% after 15 min, and first charge/discharge capacities 168.23/166.58 mAh g^-1 in Li/GPE/LiFePO4 cell (Fig. 9).

Sample (d) with 3% in situ SiO2 (PVDF-HFP/TPU/PMMA GPE) shows bulk resistance 1.10 Ω and ionic conductivity 5.5×10^-3 Scm^-1; electrolyte uptake reaches 350% at 2 min and 415% after 15 min; electrochemical stability is 5.6 V. In Li/GPE/LiFePO4 cells, first-cycle charge/discharge capacities are 166.50/164.38 mAh g^-1.

No application/performance data are reported for sample PP (Pure PVdF-HFP). PP is listed in Table 1 as a reference material and characterized by XRD/FTIR/SEM; porosity is given (45%), but no ionic conductivity, electrochemical performance, or battery cell data are provided for PP. Conductivity and Li-metal polymer cell results are only reported for PS/PL-35 and PEMA-blended films (e.g., PE-5) (Table 3; Figs. 4–7; Section 4).

Table 1 defines PE as 'Pure PEMA'. Performance data (ionic conductivity, porosity, and LiV3O8//electrolyte//Li cell tests) are reported for PL-35 and PEMA-blended films (e.g., PE-5, PE-10, etc.), not for PE. The galvanostatic results are for LiV3O8/PE-5/Li, and Table 3 lists conductivities for PL-35 and PE-x blends only. For PE, the paper provides structural/FTIR characterizations without application metrics.

PS is defined as 85 wt% PVdF-HFP + 15 wt% LiClO4 (Table 1). The ionic conductivity at room temperature (303 K) is reported as 6.45 × 10^-5 S/cm: 'The conductivity of the polymer electrolyte increases from 6.45 × 10^-5 S/cm for PS to 3.15 × 10^-3 S/cm for PL-35' (Section 3.4.1). Impedance was measured with stainless steel blocking electrodes over 40 Hz–1 MHz at room temperature to 353 K.

PL-35 (50 wt% PVdF-HFP + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4) shows ionic conductivity 3.15 × 10^-3 S/cm at 303 K and increases with temperature (Table 3: 303–353 K values). The study frames these films as electrolytes for lithium metal polymer batteries, and PL-35 degrades only above ~500 °C, indicating suitability for lithium-based polymer battery application.

PE-5 (45 wt% PVdF-HFP + 5 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4) was used as the polymer electrolyte and showed the highest ionic conductivity: "The highest conductivity value of 2.14×10^-2 S/cm was observed for PE-5" (at 303 K; Table 3 and Section 3.4.2.1). It was applied in a lithium metal polymer cell: "The optimized polymer film (PE-5) ... used as the electrolyte" and the LiV3O8/PE-5/Li metal cell delivered 223 mAh/g (2nd cycle) and 180 mAh/g (10th cycle), with 24 mAh/g at the 100th cycle (Section 4).

Table 3 reports ionic conductivity for PE-10 (40 wt% PVdF-HFP + 10 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4): 5.12 × 10^-4 S/cm at 303 K (rising to 1.74 × 10^-3 S/cm at 353 K). Conductivity was obtained from AC impedance with stainless steel blocking electrodes.

Table 3 lists ionic conductivity for sample PE-15 (35 wt% PVdF-HFP + 15 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4) as 1.33 × 10^-4 S/cm at 303 K (increasing to 3.65 × 10^-4 S/cm at 353 K). This is a gel polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction; no battery cycling data were reported for PE-15 (only for PE-5).

PE-20 is a gel polymer electrolyte film (Table 1: 30 wt% PVdF-HFP + 20 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4). Electrical behaviour of all prepared gel polymer electrolytes was studied by AC impedance, and ionic conductivities are listed in Table 3. For PE-20, the ionic conductivity is 3.91 × 10^-5 S/cm at 303 K (increases to 6.75 × 10^-5 S/cm at 353 K); Ea = 0.47 eV, porosity 71%.

PE-25 is defined as 25 wt% PVdF-HFP + 25 wt% PEMA + 35 wt% EC + 15 wt% LiClO4 (Table 1). The work studies these as gel polymer electrolytes (section 3.4). Table 3 reports ionic conductivity for PE-25: 2.62 × 10^-5 S/cm at 303 K (increasing to 5.21 × 10^-5 S/cm at 353 K), with Ea = 0.47 eV and porosity 60%. Battery tests were only conducted with PE-5, not PE-25 (section 4), so no cell performance is available for PE-25.

Table 3 reports ionic conductivity for PE-40: 1.51 × 10^-5 S/cm at 303 K (increasing to 4.17 × 10^-5 S/cm at 353 K), indicating its use as a Li+ conducting polymer electrolyte. No galvanostatic cell data were provided for PE-40 (only PE-5 was tested), so other application metrics are unavailable.

PPAL5-D is a quasi-solid-state composite polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Table 4 reports its room-temperature ionic conductivity as 3.47×10^-4 S cm^-1 with Ea = 0.250 eV. Additional performance for PPAL5-D includes: Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.61 (Fig. 5i); electrochemical stability window up to 5.2 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 5e); Li|PPAL5-D|Li symmetric cell stable for >1200 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with ~20 mV overpotential (Fig. 6e, Conclusions); critical current density 1.75 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 6d). In full cells, LFP|PPAL5-D|Li delivers 160.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C after 300 cycles with 97.6% capacity retention and >99.5% CE (Fig. 8b), and 131.5 mAh g^-1 after 400 cycles at 1C with 78.3% retention and >99.5% CE (Fig. 8d). NCM811|PPAL5-D|Li shows 170.2 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C after 100 cycles with ~99.3% average CE (Fig. 9b).

Application: Membranes were activated by immersion in a liquid electrolyte to form polymer electrolytes: “The membranes were activated in a glove box by immersion in liquid electrolyte for 4 h to form polymer electrolytes. The liquid electrolyte consists of a 1 M solution of LiPF6 in a 1/1/1 (w/w/w) DMC/EMC/EC mixture.” (Section 2.3). Electrolyte uptake for pure PVDF: Table 2 lists “Electrolyte uptake (%)” for HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 0 (pure PVDF) as 113%.

Membranes were activated to form polymer electrolytes by immersion in a liquid electrolyte: 1 M LiPF6 in DMC/EMC/EC (1/1/1, w/w/w) (Section 2.3). The work targets lithium-ion battery polymer electrolytes (e.g., electrochemical stability vs Li+/Li in Fig. 12 and discussion in Sections 3.5 and Conclusions). For the HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 1/10 (w/w), Table 2 reports electrolyte uptake = 149% and porosity = 43.5%. Ionic conductivity at 20 °C was measured (Fig. 10), but the text provides explicit values only for higher ratios (conductivity exceeded 1e-3 S/cm at 2/10 and 1.76e-3 S/cm at 3/10); no numeric conductivity is stated for 1/10, so it is not extracted.

Table 2 reports electrolyte uptake for the blend membrane with HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio 2/10 (w/w) as 187%. The polymer electrolytes were prepared by immersing membranes in 1 M LiPF6 in a 1/1/1 (w/w/w) DMC/EMC/EC mixture. Fig. 10/text state that the ion conductivity at 20 °C exceeded 1×10^-3 S/cm when the HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio reached 2/10 (w/w), though no exact numeric value is given for this ratio.

Conclusions: 'the blend membrane with a HPE-g-MPEG/PVDF ratio of 3/10 showed ion conductivity as high as 1.76 × 10^-3 S/cm at 20 °C'. Fig. 11: for the 3/10 membrane, conductivity was >10^-3 S/cm even at 0 °C. Fig. 12: electrochemical stability remained >4.5 V vs Li+/Li for the 3/10 membrane.

PPK GPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in Li-based cells. Reported for PPK GPE: “At 25 °C, the PPK GPE exhibits a high ionic conductivity of 2.815 mScm−1...” and “The t_Li+ of PPK GPE (0.571) ...” (Fig. 4d). Electrochemical stability window: “the electrochemical stability window of the PPK GPE is 4.95 V” (Fig. 4e,f). Li||Li symmetric cells: “PPK GPE achieves high stability during long-term cycling of 1200 h at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 and a capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2.” Full cell LiFePO4||graphite with PPK GPE: “stable cycling with a stable CE of 99.92% … maintain over 80.5% of their discharge capacity after 1150 cycles at 0.5 C … deliver 137.1, 123.1, and 92.6 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 C, respectively; … recover to 120.3 mAh g−1 when switched back to 0.5 C.”

Z refers to commercial 50–80 nm ZrO2 nanoparticles used to fabricate CPE-Z. Battery performance specifically reported for CPE-Z: “the coin cells assembled with the CPE-PZ and CPE-Z can only release 208.7 and 198.3 mAh g−1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles” (Graphite cell). Additional CPE-Z metrics supporting its electrolyte application include room-temperature ionic conductivity 2.83 mS cm−1, TLi+ = 0.28, tensile strength 24.67 MPa, uptake 126.3% (Table 1); electrochemical stability window 5.2 V (LSV); interfacial resistance RP = 509.4 Ω, Rs = 2.114 Ω (Table 2); decomposition temperature about 430°C.

Performance for the PZ-doped membrane (CPE-PZ): ionic conductivity 3.26 mS cm−1 and Li+ transference number 0.38 at room temperature (Table 1). The electrochemical working window of the CPE membrane doped with PZ stabilizes at 4.9 V (Fig. 4C), corroborating its battery electrolyte applicability.

CPE-sPZ (P(VDF-HFP) doped with sPZ) shows ionic conductivity 3.59 mS cm-1 at room temperature (Table 1; Fig. 4B). Lithium ion transference number TLi+ = 0.41 (Table 1; Fig. 4D). Electrochemical working window stabilizes to 5.0 V (Fig. 4C). CPE membranes maintain shape after thermal exposure at 160 °C for 1 h (Highlights; Fig. 6). Li/CPE-sPZ/LiCoO2 cell: 126.4 mAh g-1 at 2.0C after 150 cycles with ~100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 8B). Li/CPE-sPZ/Graphite cell: 288.5 mAh g-1 at 0.5C after 80 cycles with ~100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 8D).
CPE-0 is the pure P(VDF-HFP) electrolyte (no doped particles). Table 1 reports at room temperature: ionic conductivity 1.95 mS cm-1 and lithium ion transference number (TLi+) 0.16.

Table 1 reports that CPE-Z (P(VDF-HFP) CPE doped with ZrO2) has ionic conductivity σ = 2.83 mS cm-1 at room temperature, uptake A = 126.3%, tensile strength T = 24.67 MPa, activation energy Ea = 6.04 kJ mol-1, and lithium-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.28. LSV shows the CPE membrane doped with Z can stabilize at 5.2 V (SS/CPE/Li). In Li/CPE/LiCoO2 coin cell EIS fitting (Table 2), the interface resistance Rp for CPE-Z is 509.4 Ω. TG indicates the CPE-Z membrane presents the highest decomposition temperature about 430 °C. For Li/CPE/Graphite cells, the coin cell with CPE-Z can release 198.3 mAh g-1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles (referenced to Figures S3 and S4).

CPE-PZ is a P(VDF-HFP)-based composite polymer electrolyte used in Li-ion batteries. In Li/CPE/Graphite coin cells, it delivered 208.7 mAh/g at 1.0C after 120 cycles ("the coin cells assembled with the CPE-PZ ... can only release 208.7 mAh g−1 at 1.0C after 120 cycles"). Supporting electrolyte metrics for CPE-PZ include ionic conductivity 3.26 mS/cm and Li+ transference number 0.38 at room temperature (Table 1), and an electrochemical stability window of 4.9 V (LSV).

For CPE-sPZ, Table 1 reports ionic conductivity at room temperature = 3.59 mS cm-1 and Li+ transference number TLi+ = 0.41; tensile strength 23.58 MPa and uptake rate 153.4% are also listed. Fig. 4(C) shows the electrochemical working window of CPE-sPZ is 5.0 V (SS/CPE/Li). The Highlights state ionic conductivity 3.59 mS cm-1 and tensile strength 23.58 MPa. Fig. 6 notes CPE membranes maintain shape after 160 °C for 1 h. Fig. 8 shows assembled Li/CPE-sPZ/LiCoO2 coin cells with high rate performance (e.g., ~126.4 mAh g-1 at 2.0C after 150 cycles) and Li/CPE-sPZ/Graphite cells with stable cycling, indicating suitability as a lithium-ion battery electrolyte.

Directly reported for this sample: “The P(VDF-HFP)-Li10-50LATP HSE exhibits the highest Li+ conductivity (7.88 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C) …” and “The decomposition voltage of the P(VDF-HFP)-Li10-50LATP HSE is 5.16 V …”. Symmetric cell data also confirm stable operation with this electrolyte (“… stable at 29 mV during 1000 h …”). Since the schema has no dedicated field for ionic conductivity, it is recorded under capacity with unit S_cm^-1.

This material is used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) in Li/GPE/LiFePO4 coin cells. For the PVDF-[POSS-(PMMA46)8] GPE: ionic conductivity is 4.85 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 8); electrochemical stability window is 6.01 V (Fig. 9); interfacial resistance is 256.15 Ω (Fig. 10). Battery performance at 0.1C shows a first discharge capacity of 164.5 mAh g^-1 among the compared samples (Fig. 11). Rate capability for this sample: ~166 mAh g^-1 (0.1C), ~150 mAh g^-1 (0.2C), ~133.7 mAh g^-1 (0.5C), ~114.7 mAh g^-1 (1C), ~62.9 mAh g^-1 (2C) (Fig. 12). Additional relevant properties of the corresponding composite membrane include porosity 60.1% and thermal shrinkage 6.2% at 160°C (Fig. 5, Fig. 7). GPEs were prepared by soaking membranes in 1 M LiPF6 in EC/EMC/DEC (1:1:1).

BPLE-1 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries and shows: (i) high room‑temperature ionic conductivity: “the BPLE-1 electrolyte containing BPE has a high room temperature ionic conductivity of 1.6 × 10−3 S cm−1” (Fig. 3g/S19); (ii) wide electrochemical stability window: “BPLE-1 exhibits excellent antioxidant properties, with an ESW of up to 4.8 V” (Fig. 3h,i); (iii) high critical current density: “the CCD of BPLE-1 is 2.4 mA cm−2” (Fig. 4a); (iv) symmetric cell stability: “Li||BPLE-1||Li symmetric cells can stably cycle for 800 h at the current density of 0.3 mA cm−2” (Fig. 4c); and (v) full‑cell performance with LFP: e.g., 149 mAh g−1 at 1C with 98.0% retention after 500 cycles; 142 mAh g−1 at 2C with 84.5% retention after 800 cycles (Fig. 5g,h,i). These data support BPLE-1’s application as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte.

For LFP||Li cells using the PHGB polymer electrolyte: “the specific capacity of the battery with PHGB polymer electrolyte reaches 153.94 mAh g^-1 in the first cycle, and … is 147.32 mAh g^-1 with a capacity retention of 95.7% after 100 cycles at 0.1 C.”

PVDF-HFP is tested as a control polymer electrolyte in LFP||Li cells. Reported data specific to PVDF-HFP: (i) “the first cycle discharge specific capacities of the battery with … PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte … 151.95 mAh g^{-1}. After 100 cycles at 0.1 C, … 95.49 mAh g^{-1} with capacity retention of 62.8%.” (ii) Platform voltage difference: “the platform voltage differences of the cells with … PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte … 190 mV.” (iii) Rate capacities for PVDF-HFP: “160.35, 138.63, 115.07, 78.29, and 30.51 mAh g^{-1} at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 C, respectively.” (iv) EIS/transport: “the interfacial impedance Rf … PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte … 905.7 Ω” and “D_Li+ = 3.34 × 10^{-17} cm^2 s^{-1}.” These values are explicitly given for the PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte in the Results section and figure captions.

PHG (PVDF-HFP/GO) polymer electrolyte in LFP||Li coin cells: first-cycle discharge specific capacity 145.14 mAh g^-1; after 100 cycles at 0.1 C, discharge specific capacity 124.84 mAh g^-1 with capacity retention 86%. Platform voltage difference 179 mV. At 2 C, utilization 53.72%. Interfacial impedance Rf = 239 Ω; Li+ diffusion coefficient D_Li+ = 1.01e-16 cm^2 s^-1. (All values explicitly reported for PHG in Fig. 6b,c,f,g,h text.)

LLZTO pellets (Li6.75La3Zr1.75Ta0.25O12) showed an ionic conductivity of 3.24×10^-4 Scm^-1 at room temperature (Fig. S2): "the ionic conductivity of LLZTO pellets is 3.24×10^-4 Scm^-1 at room temperature (Fig. S2)."

The material 50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF + 9% OX is used as a polymer/garnet composite solid electrolyte for Li metal batteries. For this exact composition, the highest ionic conductivity reached 2.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 55 °C (Fig. 1c). Its electronic conductivity is 4.9×10^-10 S cm^-1 at 55 °C (Fig. S6), and the Li+ transference number t+ = 0.45 at 55 °C (Fig. 1d). LSV shows no apparent current up to 5.0 V for 50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF + 9% OX, indicating an electrochemical stability window up to 5.0 V (Fig. 2a); tiny impedance changes after applying 4.8 V bias for 72 h at 55 °C (Fig. 2c). Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells using this electrolyte remain stable for 360 h at 0.3 mA cm^-2 and 55 °C (Fig. 3b), and interfacial impedance stabilizes around ~98 Ω after 15 days at 55 °C (Fig. 3a). Thermal stability: no pronounced shrinkage at 150 °C for 0.5 h (Fig. 2d). In full cells Al2O3@NCM|electrolyte|Li, initial discharge capacity 150.6 mAh g^-1 and capacity retention 86.7% after 80 cycles at 0.2 C and 55 °C were reported (Fig. 5a,d).

Conventional Al2O3@NCM (c-Al2O3@NCM) served as the cathode in solid-state Li metal batteries using 50[PEO8-LiTFSI]-40LLZTO-10PVDF + 9% OX electrolyte. At 55 °C and 0.2 C within 2.5–4.3 V, the c-Al2O3@NCM cell showed an initial discharge capacity of 142.9 mAh g^-1 and Coulombic efficiency of 68.9%. Under 1 C, the discharge capacity for the conventional cathode decreased to 58.6 mAh g^-1. EIS also reported a pristine interfacial resistance of 212 Ω for the conventional cathode cell, rising to 441, 1074, and 1564 Ω after 1, 25, and 50 cycles at 0.2 C and 55 °C.

The composite cathode (f-Al2O3@NCM) is defined as the Al2O3@NCM composite cathode after infiltrating PEO8-LiTFSI (Section 2.5). In solid-state cells, “The cell with composite cathode framework exhibits high initial discharge capacity (150.6 mAh g−1) and Coulombic efficiency (84.9%). By contrast, the initial discharge capacity of cell with conventional cathode is only 142.9 mAh g−1.” Additionally, rate data show for the composite framework: “After the end of cycling at high rate of 1 C, the discharge capacities of the cell with composite cathode framework is 103.7 mAh g−1… When the rate returned to 0.1 C again, the discharge capacity is recovered to 152 mAh g−1.” The conclusion attributes the long-term stability to the composite framework: “The solid-state Li battery assembled with … composite cathode framework demonstrates excellent cycling stabilities with the capacity retention of 86.7% after 80 cycles at 0.2 C and 55 °C.”

0LLZTO/PVDF denotes the PVDF nanofiber membrane with 0% LLZTO, used to form the composite electrolyte 0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI. In Li/Li symmetric cells at 50 °C and 0.3 mA cm^-2, the voltage of Li/0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI/Li "decreases rapidly from 0.12 V after 854 h and eventually causes the occurrence of a short-circuit phenomenon." In full cells at 1C (50 °C), the Li/0LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI/LiFePO4 battery shows a lower capacity retention rate of 54.6% compared to the 10LLZTO case.

LLZTO/PVDF nanofiber membranes with different LLZTO mass fractions (0%, 5%, 10% and 15%) were prepared... 5LLZTO/PVDF means that 5% LLZTO was added. The LLZTO/PVDF nanofiber membrane is used as the scaffold in LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI composite electrolytes for all-solid-state Li/Li and Li/LiFePO4 batteries. Li/Li symmetric battery assembled with 5LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte shows relatively poor cycle stability and short-circuited after 1125 h (0.3 mA cm^-2, 50 °C), confirming its use as a solid-state battery electrolyte component facilitating Li+ transport.

For the sample using the 10LLZTO/PVDF nanofiber membrane as the scaffold (10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte), the text states: "the ionic conductivity of 10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte can reach 9.30 × 10^−4 S cm−1 at 50 °C" (Fig. 5a). Additional related performance (not mapped to schema fields) includes Li/Li symmetric cells with the 10LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolyte showing stable cycling for 1200 h at 0.3 mA cm−2 at 50 °C (Fig. 6a,b; Conclusion) and an electrochemical stability window up to 4.9 V (Fig. 5d).

Li/Li symmetric battery assembled with 5LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI and 15LLZTO/PVDF-PEO-LiTFSI electrolytes also shows relatively poor cycle stability and short-circuited after 1125 h and 959 h, respectively (Figs. S1a and S1b). Tests were conducted at 50 °C with 0.3 mA cm^-2. Additionally, the complete thermal degradation temperature of the composite electrolyte with 15% LLZTO is 353 °C (Fig. 4c).

No application performance is explicitly reported for M0. Reported metrics (electrolyte uptake 35% for E0, ionic conductivity 3.097e-3 S_cm^-1 and T_Li+ 0.7922 for E90, Li/E90/LiFePO4 cycling, stability to 4.3 V) pertain to E-series gel electrolytes or E90, not specifically to M0. M0 appears only in morphology/FTIR/XRD figures without quantitative application data.

M10 is the porous membrane with PVDF/PMMA = 90/10 (Table 1). Its corresponding gel polymer electrolyte is E10. Table 2 reports the Li-ion transference number for E10 as 0.5957; thus M10, when activated as E10, shows T_Li+ = 0.5957. Figure 1 includes electrolyte uptake for M0–M100, but the text only gives numeric uptake for E0 (35%) and E90 (122.2%); no numeric uptake is provided for M10/E10, so capacity is left null.

No direct application/performance metrics are reported for sample M30. Reported quantitative data (ionic conductivity, Li-ion transference number, electrochemical stability, cell cycling) are for gel polymer electrolytes E* (not M*), with detailed values highlighted for E90 and a table for E0–E100 (Table 2). Liquid electrolyte uptake is discussed with explicit numbers only for E0 and E90; the figure caption mentions M0–M100 but provides no specific numeric value for M30. Therefore, no extractable application data specific to M30.

application.target_species: ionic liquid–salt mixture (0.2 mol kg^-1 LiTFSI in EMITFSI)

parse.why: Table 1 defines M50 as the porous PVDF/PMMA membrane (50/50 wt.%). Electrolyte uptake was measured for the polymer membranes (M0–M100) by immersing membranes in 0.2 mol kg^-1 LiTFSI in EMITFSI for 40 min (Experimental, Eq. (1)), and Figure 1 is captioned "Liquid electrolyte uptake for sample M0-M100." However, numeric uptake values in the text are only given for E0 and E90; no explicit value is reported for M50, so capacity is left null with unit retained.

Table 1 maps the porous membrane M70 (PVDF/PMMA 30/70) to its corresponding gel polymer electrolyte E70. Table 2 reports a lithium-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.5504 for E70 (with Ri0 = 3.670×10^3 Ω and Ri = 2.047×10^4 Ω), indicating application as a Li+ conducting electrolyte. No numerical ionic conductivity is provided for E70 (only E90 has an explicit value 3.097×10^-3 S cm^-1), and electrolyte uptake for M70 is shown only as a curve without tabulated values. Electrochemical cell testing was performed with E90, not E70. Therefore, for M70/E70, only the application scenario (Li+ battery electrolyte) can be asserted, while structured performance fields remain null due to lack of numeric values tied to M70/E70 in the text.

M90 is the porous PVDF/PMMA membrane (10/90 wt.%). It serves as the host to form the gel polymer electrolyte E90 used for lithium batteries: the Li/E90/LiFePO4 cell was tested. Numerical performance values reported in the paper (ionic conductivity 3.097×10^-3 S cm^-1; Li-ion transference number 0.7922) are given for E90 (Figure 5(a); Table 2), not directly for M90. Although the conclusion text states “only sample of M90 … gives the highest ionic conductivity … 3.097×10^-3 S cm^-1 … and … lithium-ion transference number, 0.7922”, these values are elsewhere attributed to E90. Therefore, for M90 we record the application (electrolyte host for Li+) without assigning numeric performance.

Table 1 maps PVDF/PMMA 0/100 to porous membrane M100 and its corresponding gel polymer electrolyte E100. Table 2 reports for film E100 (derived from M100) a lithium-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.2962.

Figure 1 states: "E0 reach the saturation with the electrolyte uptake at 35%." Table 2 lists for E0: "Lithium-ions transference number, TLi+ = 0.5472" (with Ri0=0.520×10^0 Ω and Ri=8.797×10^0 Ω for the Li/E0/Li cell used in the Bruce–Vincent method). These data directly pertain to E0 as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction.

Table 1 maps compositions to gel polymer electrolyte sample IDs: 90/10 (PVDF/PMMA) -> E10. Table 2 lists lithium-ion transference number (TLi+) for each film; for E10, TLi+ = 0.5957 (with Ri0 = 6.923×10^0 Ω and Ri = 2.018×10^1 Ω). These values were obtained from chronoamperometry and AC impedance measurements on Li/gel polymer electrolyte/Li cells.

Table 2 lists for sample E30 a Li-ion transference number T_Li+ = 0.5513, with resistance before dc polarization Ri0 = 1.552×10^2 Ω and after dc polarization Ri = 3.551×10^2 Ω, measured in Li/gel polymer electrolyte/Li cells.

Table 2 reports for sample E50 (gel polymer electrolyte from PVDF/PMMA 50/50): Lithium-ions transference number TLi+ = 0.5297 (with Ri0 = 4.464×10^2 Ω and Ri = 5.269×10^2 Ω). This directly characterizes Li+ transport for E50.

E70 is the gel polymer electrolyte from PVDF/PMMA 30/70 (Table 1). Table 2 reports the lithium-ion transference number for E70 as 0.5504 (with Ri0 = 3.670×10^3 Ω and Ri = 2.047×10^4 Ω). Full-cell and conductivity data were provided for E90, not E70; thus no other performance metrics for E70 are reported.

E90 is the gel polymer electrolyte derived from the 10/90 (PVDF/PMMA) membrane. Reported metrics for E90 include: ionic conductivity 3.097 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 ("The ionic conductivity of the gel polymer electrolyte is 3.097 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 for sample E90."); Li-ion transference number TLi+ = 0.7922 from Table 2; electrochemical stability window up to 4.3 V ("the polymer electrolyte was stable up to 4.3 V" for sample E90). Additional context for E90: electrolyte uptake 122.2% and Li/E90/LiFePO4 cell tests (first-cycle discharge capacity ~62 mAh g^-1; ~95% coulombic efficiency).

E100 is the gel polymer electrolyte derived from PVDF/PMMA = 0/100 (Table 1). Direct performance data for E100 include a Li-ion transference number T_Li+ = 0.2962 and electrode resistances before/after dc polarization of 2.171×10^3 Ω and 2.563×10^3 Ω, respectively (Table 2). Other battery tests and stability window values were reported for E90, not E100, and ionic conductivity for E100 is only shown in plots without numeric values.

PIL-GPE@IL is used as a solid-state gel polymer electrolyte in Li metal/LiFePO4 cells. It shows ionic conductivity of 1.76×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Table 1; Section 3.4, and Conclusions). Electrochemical stability window: 4.26 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 5b). Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.32 (Table 2). Cells Li/PIL-GPE@IL/LiFePO4 deliver discharge capacities of 152 mAh g^-1 (0.1C), 148 (0.2C), 144 (0.5C), 129 (1C), 72 (2C) (Fig. 8c,d). Cycling at 0.5C: ~143→139 mAh g^-1 over 120 cycles with ~99% coulombic efficiency; 97% capacity retention (Fig. 8f; Conclusions).

SPE is defined as a polymer electrolyte composed of PVDF and LiTFSI (weight ratio 1:0.5) and marked as SPE (Section 2.4). Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity (×10^-4 S cm^-1) at different temperatures: 2.78 (30 °C), 3.84 (40 °C), 5.36 (50 °C), 7.09 (60 °C), 9.31 (70 °C), 11.89 (80 °C). LSV shows the oxidation potential of SPE is 4.67 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 5b). These data confirm SPE’s role as a Li+ conducting polymer electrolyte.

GPE@IL is used as a polymer electrolyte (PVDF:[BMIM]TFSI:LiTFSI = 1:2:0.5) in Li/GPE@IL/LiFePO4 cells. Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity as 15.6×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (and up to 52.7×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 80 °C). Fig. 5(b) shows its oxidation stability at 4.40 V vs Li+/Li. Fig. 8(a,b,e) shows Li/GPE@IL/LiFePO4 rate and cycling: 151, 145, 126, 104, 67 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C–2C; initial 136 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C with 93% retention after 120 cycles.

PE-PM-PVH作为凝胶聚合物电解质（GPE）用于锂金属电池。关键性能：离子电导率0.81 mS cm^-1（25°C，Table 1）；Li+迁移数t_Li+ = 0.72（Fig. 5 & Table 1）；LSV分解电位上限≈5.0 V vs. Li/Li+（Fig. 6）；对称Li/GPE/Li在0.25 mA cm^-2下稳定镀/剥锂约500 h，电压滞后约50–60 mV（Fig. 9b）。全电池Li/GPE/LiCoO2在0.1 mA cm^-2下初始放电比容量为152.7 mAh g^-1（按LiCoO2质量计算），100圈后为149.6 mAh g^-1，容量保持率约98%，库仑效率>99%（除首圈）（Fig. 9c）。据此将容量字段记录为全电池初始比容量。

FPEC is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li-ion batteries. Reported performance: "The FPEC shows ion conductivity as high as 1.08 × 10^{-4} S/cm at 30 °C" and "The as-prepared FPEC displays a high ionic conductivity of 1.08×10^{-4} S/cm at 30 °C". Electrochemical context includes a stability window ~4.5 V vs Li+/Li and successful Li/FPEC/LFP cell tests, confirming its role as an electrolyte; no selectivity, breakthrough, or regeneration metrics are applicable.

Nafion 211 is used as the commercial PFSA proton-exchange membrane benchmark in vanadium redox flow battery tests. The paper reports that the ultrathin PFSA membrane shows higher energy efficiency (~76%) than a cell with a commercial Nafion 211 membrane at 200 mA cm^-2, where Nafion 211 achieves EE ~73% (“Even though the ion-selective ultrathin PFSA ionomer layer on the mechanical support (PC50) is 1000 times thinner than N211, the single cell with PCEW10-100 performed better in EE at 200 mA cm^-2, achieving approximately 76% EE compared to the commercial Nafion 211 (EE ~ 73%).”). VO2+ crossover is the specific species measured for permeation. However, the text does not provide numeric VO2+ permeance, resistance, or selectivity values for Nafion 211 (only comparative statements and curves), so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

The 40 wt% EMImTFSI-doped PEO-LiDFOB membrane was tested in Li/PEO-LiDFOB-40 wt% IL/LiFePO4 coin cells at 0.1 C and 25 °C. The initial specific capacity was 155 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 7). After 50 cycles, the discharge capacity was 134.2 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 8; Conclusions). The same sample shows ionic conductivity 1.85 × 10^-4 S/cm at 30 °C (Table 1; Conductivity studies), supporting its function as a Li+ conducting solid polymer electrolyte.

PH (PVDF-HFP fibrous membranes) is used as a separator/GPE host in Li metal and LFP|Li coin cells. Key PH-specific performance: ionic conductivity at room temperature = 3.83e-3 S_cm^-1 (Figure 2h); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.46 (Figure S4). In Li|PH|Li symmetric cells: at 0.1 mA_cm^-2 polarization fluctuation and failure occurred at ~510 h (Figure 3a); at 0.5 mA_cm^-2 failure at 120 h (Figure 3b). In Li|PH|Cu cells at 0.5 mA_cm^-2: nucleation and growth overpotentials 141.0 mV and 86.8 mV; initial CE 88.6%, average CE 89.0% over 100 cycles (Figure 3c,d). In LFP|PH|Li cells: CV peak separation ΔV = 280 mV (Figure S7); capacity retention 80.4% after 220 cycles at 0.2 C with average CE 96.6% (Figure 4d); interfacial impedance 125 Ω (initial), 50 Ω (50 cycles), 58 Ω (100 cycles) (Figure 4f). These results evidence the application as a lithium battery separator/electrolyte host. No direct metrics matching schema fields capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are reported for PH; thus set to null.

Rate performance of LFP|NVPH|Li: "delivers specific discharge capacities of 157.1, 155.8, 150.6, 143.2, and 130.1 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively" (Figure 4c). High-temperature durability: "After 250 cycles at 80 °C, the discharge specific capacity of the LFP|NVPH|Li cell at 1 C is 149.7 mA h g−1 with a capacity retention of 91.8%" (Figure 4h). Symmetric cells: Li|NVPH|Li shows stable plating/stripping "beyond 1000 h" at 0.1 mA cm−2 and survives ">600 h" at 0.5 mA cm−2 (Figure 3a,b). Additional electrolyte metrics: ionic conductivity at room temperature 2.89×10−3 S cm−1 and t_Li+ = 0.57 (Figure 2h,S4); electrochemical window up to 5.2 V (Figures 2i,S5).

This material (GPE 3:2) is used as an in-situ gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported metrics include: ionic conductivity 4.06×10^-3 S/cm at 30°C and Li+ transference number 0.87 (Fig. 5a–e); electrochemical stability window 5.35 V at 30°C (Fig. 5f); NCM811|GPE 3:2|Li shows specific capacity 135.80 mAh/g at 10C, with 181.82 mAh/g at 1C and 171.39 mAh/g at 2C (Fig. 6c). Long-term cycling: 300 cycles at 2C and 3C with capacity retention of 84% and 70% (Fig. 6d,e). Symmetric Li|GPE 3:2|Li overpotential ~9 mV stable before 1100 h (Fig. S5). LFP|GPE 3:2|Li delivers 128.06, 118.46, 104.53 mAh/g at 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and retains 76% after 1000 cycles at 3C (Fig. 7b,c).

For the PETEA-HFA-TCGG-PAN GPE (1:1): GPE 1:1 at 30 °C exhibits σ = 3.59×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 5a–c). The lithium-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.73 (Fig. 5d,e). LSV shows decomposition potential of 4.8 V at 30 °C (Fig. 5f). In NCM811|GPE 1:1|Li cells, the initial discharge specific capacity is 204.67 mAh g^-1 with a coulombic efficiency of 89.19% (Fig. 6b).

CPE(PVAc) serves as a gel polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. In LiFePO4|CPE(PVAc)|Li coin cells at 2C and 25°C, the initial discharge specific capacity is 135.4 mAh/g with 97.6% capacity retention after 500 cycles and ~99.7% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5d–e). At 1C and 55°C, the initial capacity is 157.5 mAh/g with 80% retention after 400 cycles (Fig. 5f–g). Supporting electrolyte metrics for CPE(PVAc): ionic conductivity ≈1.47×10^-3 S/cm at 30°C (Fig. 3c–d), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.75 (Fig. 4a), and electrochemical stability window ≈4.9 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 4c). Li|CPE(PVAc)|Li symmetric cells exhibit ~1500 h stable cycling at 0.1 mA/cm^2 with ~11 mV polarization (Fig. 4f). A trace LEF (~5 µL/cm^2) was added between electrolyte and electrodes during assembly to improve interfacial contact.

SHSPE3 is used as the solid polymer electrolyte in Li/SHSPE3/Li and LiFePO4/SHSPE3/Li cells. Rate performance (60 °C): LiFePO4/SHSPE3/Li delivers 163.9, 137.2, 121.6 and 104.7 mA h g^-1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C and 1C, respectively; when reset to 0.1C, capacity returns to the original value (Fig. 4d). Long-term cycling (60 °C, 0.2C): capacity increases to a maximum of 144.8 mA h g^-1 with coulombic efficiency >97%, and after 100 cycles remains 119.3 mA h g^-1 (capacity retention ~82%) (Fig. 4e; Conclusions). Li/SHSPE3/Li symmetric cell maintains a low overpotential of ~0.22 V at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4a). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.37 for SHSPE3 (Fig. S8). Electrochemical stability window initial oxidation potential >4.7 V vs Li/Li+ for SHSPE1/3/5 (Fig. 3b). Adhesion: two Li sheets bonded by SHSPE3 hold 200 g (Fig. 3f). Self-healing: scratches heal within 60 min at 25 °C (Fig. 5a) and the healed sample shows negligible change in Nyquist plots after 5 breaking/healing cycles (Fig. 6e). Thermal/flame safety: SHSPE3 maintains shape after >10 s flame exposure (Fig. 2a). The capacity value reported here (144.8 mA h g^-1) corresponds to LiFePO4/SHSPE3/Li at 0.2C and 60 °C.

PVFH-PMC-PEGC is a gel polymer electrolyte used in lithium metal batteries and symmetric Li|Li cells. Key performance directly reported for PVFH-PMC-PEGC: ionic conductivity 2.70 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C (optimized at 10 wt% PMC-PEGC); Li-ion transference number t+ = 0.77. Electrolyte uptake 245% and electrolyte retention 94% after 30 min. In Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|Li symmetric cells: stable cycling for 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with low overpotential of 13 mV; stable for 300 h at 1 mA cm^-2 and 5 mAh cm^-2; critical current density up to 4 mA cm^-2. In full cells using PVFH-PMC-PEGC as electrolyte: Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|LFP delivers reversible discharge capacities of 163.1, 157.1, 148.5, 136.7, 123.3, 110.0 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0C, respectively; returns to 160.1 mAh g^-1 when current returns to 0.1C; after 200 cycles at 0.5C retains 130.8 mAh g^-1 (90.0% of initial); long-term at 2C retains 96 mAh g^-1 with 0.032% capacity decay per cycle over 800 cycles and lower internal resistance growth than PP-LE. High-loading LFP (8.6 mg cm^-2): 128 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C after 150 cycles. Li–S: Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|S delivers 948 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C initially and 766 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles (77.6% retention), with internal resistance change from 53 Ω to 113 Ω. Li–NCM622: initial 160 mAh g^-1; after 100 cycles retains 150 mAh g^-1 (93.8%), outperforming PP-LE; pouch cell powers a phone and remains operational under bending, nail penetration, and cutting.

PMC-PEGC is used as a functional filler in a PVFH-based gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. With 10 wt% PMC-PEGC, the composite PVFH-PMC-PEGC exhibits ionic conductivity of 2.70 mS cm^-1 (optimized filler content), and a high Li+ transference number t+ = 0.77. Symmetric Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|Li cells cycle stably for 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with low overpotential (~13 mV) and reach a critical current density of 4 mA cm^-2. Full cells demonstrate: Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|LFP shows reversible capacities of 163.1/157.1/148.5/136.7/123.3/110.0 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2/3C; after 800 cycles at 2C, 96 mAh g^-1 remains with 0.032% capacity decay per cycle. Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|S delivers 948 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C and 766 mAh g^-1 (77.6% retention) after 200 cycles. Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|NCM622 delivers 160 mAh g^-1 initially and 150 mAh g^-1 (93.8% retention) after 100 cycles. These performances are explicitly attributed to introducing PMC-PEGC into the electrolyte membrane. Supporting materials: “At this optimized content, the ionic conductivity of PVFH-PMC-PEGC reaches 2.70 mScm^-1...” and “the t+ of PVFH... and PVFH-PMC-PEGC are... 0.77.” Also, Li symmetric cells: “no apparent overpotential fluctuations... for 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2... overpotential of 13 mV... critical current density... up to 4 mA cm^-2.” Full-cell data are reported in Fig. 5–6 sections.

PVFH is used as a gel polymer electrolyte matrix for Li batteries. Reported PVFH has an ionic conductivity of 0.19 mScm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3d: “PVFH has an ionic conductivity of 0.19 mScm^{-1} at 25 °C.”). Its Li+ transference number is 0.43 (Fig. 3b/S14). In Li | PVFH | Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2, stable cycling lasts ~80 h with ~80 mV overpotential before short-circuiting at ~180 h (Section 3.4). PVFH membrane shows electrolyte uptake 112% and retention 74% after 30 min (Fig. 2e), supporting its role as battery electrolyte.

Direct statements for CA/PVDF membrane (as GPE in Li batteries):
- "The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mS cm^-1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506" (Conclusion).
- "When the voltage is less than 4.8 V, the oxidation current of CA/PVDF-GPE is almost zero, verifying that the electrochemical stability window of CA/PVDF-GPE is about 4.8 V" (Fig. 3a text).
- "a stable voltage profile is observed for the GPE with no detectable failure in 350 h" at 1 mA cm^-2, 1.0 mA h cm^-2 (Fig. 4a-b text).
- "LiFePO4/GPE/Li cell delivers the initial reversible capacity of 101.2 mA h g^-1 at 5C with the capacity retention of 94.6% after 400 cycles" (Fig. 5d text).

CA/PVDF-GPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in lithium metal batteries. Reported metrics: “The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mScm−1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506” (Conclusion). Electrochemical stability window: “about 4.8 V” (Fig. 3a text). Symmetric Li/CA/PVDF-GPE/Li cell shows stable Li plating/stripping with “no detectable failure in 350 h” at 1 mA cm−2 (Fig. 4a) and is further described as “over 300 h” in the conclusion. Full-cell performance demonstrates applicability: LFP/GPE/Li initial 144.7 mAh g−1 with lower polarization, 150.8 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles (Fig. 5a-b), and 101.2 mAh g−1 at 5C with 94.6% capacity retention after 400 cycles (Fig. 5d).

ME91 is one of the ionogel polymer electrolyte membranes prepared (numbered by MPEGA/ETPTA ratios). The text explicitly states: "Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73)." No numeric performance values for ME91 (e.g., ionic conductivity, mechanical properties, or battery test data) are provided; detailed electrochemical and battery results are only shown for ME82.

ME82 is used as the electrolyte in LiFePO4/ME82/Li cells. Reported cell performance (0.5 C): “the initial specific discharge capacity and Coulombic efficiency of the battery are 151.0 mAh g^-1 and 98.94%, respectively… specific discharge capacity is still as high as 148.5 mAh g^-1 with a stable Coulombic efficiency of 99.80% after 200 cycles” at 25 °C. High-temperature cycling (55 °C, 0.5 C): “the initial specific discharge capacity is 160.9 mAh g^-1 with a capacity retention of 94.00% after 200 cycles.” Electrolyte properties relevant to application: “room temperature ionic conductivity of ME82 is … 1.19 mS cm^-1… when the temperature rises to 85 °C, the conductivity rises to 2.85 mS cm^-1”; “electrochemical stability window (ESW) of ME82 is about 5 V”; Li|ME82|Li symmetric cell: “after a 1000 h test period at … 0.1 mA cm^-2 … remained at this value until the end of the test (1000 h).”

Preparation assigns ME73 as an ionogel polymer electrolyte film: “According to the quality ratios of MPEGA/ETPTA, they were numbered as ME91, ME82, and ME73, respectively… After that, 1.6 g of Pyr13TFSI, 0.4 g of LiTFSI…”; Performance comparison explicitly includes ME73: “Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73).”

ME00 is the PVDF-HFP gel electrolyte without MPEGA/ETPTA. The paper reports morphology and mechanical properties for ME00 (e.g., "the pure PVDF-HFP gel electrolyte ME00 has the lowest elongation at break (47%) and mechanical strength (0.42 MPa)") and notes lower thermal stability compared to ME82 ("the decomposition temperature of ME82 at 5% mass loss is 60 °C higher than that of ME00"). However, electrochemical/application performance data (ionic conductivity values, electrochemical stability window, Li/ME00/Li symmetric cycling, or LiFePO4/ME00/Li battery tests) are not provided for ME00; those measurements were performed for ME82.

CPE-1 is a composite gel polymer electrolyte evaluated in lithium metal battery configurations. Its lithium-ion transference number was explicitly reported: "the calculated t_{Li+} of CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45" (measured on Li|CPE-n|Li cells at room temperature by chronoamperometry and EIS). No specific ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, symmetric-cell cycling time, or LiFePO4|CPE-1|Li battery capacities were provided for CPE-1.

CPE-3 is a composite gel polymer electrolyte used in Li metal batteries. Reported performance directly for CPE-3 includes: (i) highest ionic conductivity among CPE-n, up to 1.70 mS cm^-1 (room temperature), and reiterated as 1.70 mS cm^-1 at 30°C in the conclusions; also ~1.88×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30°C and 3.99×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 80°C (EIS, SS|CPE|SS). (ii) Electrochemical stability window extended to 5.0 V vs Li (LSV, Li|CPE-3|SS). (iii) Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.48 (Li|CPE-3|Li, DC polarization/EIS). (iv) Li|CPE-3|Li symmetric cell cycles stably for 1000 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 and 25°C with ~70 mV polarization. (v) Full cells LiFePO4|CPE-3|Li: at 25°C, rate capacities of 165.6, 158.1, 149.9, 139.8, 129.8 mAh/g at 0.2C–3C; capacity recovers to 164.7 mAh/g when rate returns to 0.2C; at 1C, initial 150.4 mAh/g with 88.7% retention after 500 cycles and ~99.80% CE. (vi) At 55°C, initial 160.3 mAh/g at 1C with 96.63% retention after 500 cycles, 80.2% after 1000 cycles, CE ~99.82–99.87%. (vii) Thermal stability: <5% mass loss before 136°C; no deformation at 150°C for 0.5 h. These collectively evidence the application of CPE-3 as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte; ionic conductivity (1.70 mS/cm) is reported explicitly and chosen as the primary quantitative metric.

Only a lithium-ion transference number is explicitly given for CPE-5: “the calculated tLi+ of CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45.” No CPE-5-specific ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, or cell performance (e.g., Li|CPE-5|Li cycling, LiFePO4|CPE-5|Li capacities/retentions) are reported; detailed cycling and rate data are provided for CPE-3 instead.

EVOH-graft-LiCPSI was used to prepare a gel single-ion polymer electrolyte (SIPE) membrane (blended with PVDF-HFP; EC/DMC plasticizer) for Li-ion batteries. Electrochemical performance: “The ionic conductivities of the gel SIPE at different temperature are shown in Fig. 6b. The ionic conductivity of the gel SIPE is 5.7 × 10^-5 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C” (Sec. 3.4). Lithium-ion transference number measured in Li|SIPE|Li cell is 0.88 (Sec. 3.4: “the lithium ion transference number was calculated to be 0.88”). As electrolyte/separator, the gel SIPE enabled LiFePO4/Li cells with stated capacities (Fig. 8), supporting its battery-electrolyte application.

The material explicitly referred to as the gel SIPE is used as the electrolyte/separator in Li-based cells. Its ionic conductivity is reported as 5.7×10^-5 mS cm^-1 at 25°C (EIS; Fig. 6b). Additional performance evidence for the same gel SIPE includes an electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 6c), Li+ transference number t+ = 0.88 (Fig. 6d), stable Li|SIPE|Li cycling with ~19 mV polarization for 300 h (Fig. 7a), and LiFePO4/Li cell capacities (e.g., ~100 mA h g^-1 at 1C; ~95 mA h g^-1 after 500 cycles) when using the gel SIPE (Fig. 8).

The PMS-PVDF GPE (specifically PMS-PVDF-55) is used as an electrolyte for Li metal batteries. The paper reports: "the PMS-PVDF membrane with a mass ratio of 5:5 exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of 5.62 mS cm−1." It further demonstrates application in Li/PMS-PVDF-55 GPE/LFP cells with rate capacities up to 106.5 mAh g−1 at 10 C and stable cycling, but the ionic conductivity directly characterizes the material’s electrolyte performance.

TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP fiber membrane was used to prepare a flame‑retardant GPE by soaking in 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC. For this material, the ionic conductivity is reported as 0.85 mS/cm at room temperature (Figure S9). Additional results with this electrolyte include: Li||Li symmetric cells cycling stably for 1000 h at 0.2 mA/cm2 − 0.2 mAh/cm2; Li||Cu Coulombic efficiency of 99.1% at 0.5 mA/cm2; Li||LiFePO4 capacity retention of 91.2% after 200 cycles at 0.5 C and 82% after 200 cycles at 1 C; pouch cells maintaining flame resistance even when surface temperature reaches ~370 °C.

Electrolyte property: “the ionic conductivity of TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP GPE is calculated as 0.85 mS cm−1 at room temperature” (Figure S9). Symmetric Li||Li lifespan: “the stable cycling behavior of Li||Li cells in TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP electrolyte can reach 1000 h at 0.2 mA cm−2 − 0.2 mAh cm−2” (Figure 4b). Coulombic efficiency: “the CE in TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP electrolyte is as high as 99.1% at 0.5 mA cm−2” (Figure 4h).

GTSSE is used as a solid-state electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Ionic conductivity: “The GTSSE presents the ionic conductivity of 2.91 × 10^-5 S cm^-1 at 20 °C and 1.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 50 °C.” Transference number: “For symmetric Li|GTSSE|Li cell, a relatively high (0.38) is acquired at room temperature (RT).” Electrochemical window: “the GTSSE can be stable up to 4.8 V.” Symmetric cell stability: “the Li|PEGDME|GTSSE|Li cell exhibits outstanding electrochemical stability … the initial polarization voltage … 25 mV, and after cycling for 2500 h is 48 mV” at 0.1 mA cm^-2 for 0.1 mAh cm^-2. Full-cell performance with GTSSE: LFP|GTSSE|Li at 50 °C, 0.5C: “initial discharge capacity of 142.5 mAh g^-1 … The discharge capacity stabilizes 135.4 mAh g^-1 after 150 cycles, corresponding to 95% of the initial discharge capacity.” NCM811|GTSSE|Li at 50 °C, 0.5C: “a high discharge capacity retention of 83% (137 mAh g^-1) … after 200 cycles … presents … 107 mAh g^-1 after an ultralong cycle life of 1000 cycles at 0.5C.”

PVDF/PP SSPEs function as the electrolyte in Li metal cells (LFP|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li; NCM523|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li). Reported ionic conductivity at room temperature: 1.53×10^-4 Scm^-1. Additional performance: electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V (LSV), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.24, symmetric Li|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li cycling ~400 h at 0.15 mA cm^-2 without shorting, and LFP cell discharge capacities of 134 mAh/g initially and 131 mAh/g after >180 cycles at 0.3C.

For the blank PVDF SSPEs used as electrolyte in solid-state LFP|PVDF SSPEs|Li cells at 0.3C and 25 C (2.8–3.6 V window), the paper reports: 'the discharge capacity of LFP| PVDF SSPEs|Li cells decays rapidly to 85 mAh g^-1 from the initial capacity of 125 mAh g^-1 (with the capacity retention of 68%) after only 64 charge–discharge cycles.' Symmetric Li|PVDF SSPEs|Li failed after ~43 h at 0.15 mA cm^-2. No explicit numeric ionic conductivity or Li+ transference number was given for the blank PVDF SSPEs; electrochemical stability onset was stated as 4.7 V but this metric is not captured by the schema fields.

HBA-PCEE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in lithium metal batteries: LFP||HBA-PCEE||Li full cells were assembled and tested (2.7–4.0 V). The text reports: "CNBA-PCEE and HBA-PCEE showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAh g^-1 and 70.47 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C, respectively, in the LFP full cells." It also notes an oxidation potential of 4.6 V for HBA-PCEE (LSV), confirming its electrolyte role.

Full-cell data with CNBA-PCEE are reported for LFP||CNBA-PCEE||Li: “CNBAPCEE … showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAh g−1 at 0.5C” and rate performance at 2C of 51.73 mAh g−1. No explicit numerical ionic conductivity or tLi+ values for CNBA-PCEE are given in the main text (FBA values are provided), so only the battery capacity is extracted.

FBA-PCEE was used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Performance data specific to FBA-PCEE include: (i) LFP||FBA-PCEE||Li cell: “initial discharge specific capacity of 137.3 mAh g−1 at 0.5C, retaining 85.38% after 200 cycles and 77.38% after 500 cycles” (Fig. 5a); (ii) NCM-622||FBA-PCEE||Li: “151.61 mAh g−1 initial discharge specific capacity at 0.1C and capacity retention of 91.84% after 50 cycles and 78.95% after 100 cycles” (Fig. 5f); (iii) Lithium symmetric cells: FBA-PCEE showed “average [polarization] 35 mV for 200–210 h … and 45 mV over 1000–1010 h” (Fig. 3c); (iv) Electrolyte properties at 30 °C for the chosen 5:5 SN:monomer ratio: “tLi+ (0.75)… oxidation potential (5.1 V) and ionic conductivity (2.02 mS cm−1 at 30 °C)” (Figs. S3–S6, Table S1). These directly support the battery-electrolyte application and the capacity value reported for the LFP full cell.

PFIGE is used as an ionogel electrolyte and integrated anode coating (PFIGE@Li) for lithium metal batteries. Electrochemical data include: Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.46; ionic conductivity increases from 0.17 mS m^-1 at 0 °C to 1.72 mS m^-1 at 100 °C (Ea = 0.21 eV); electronic conductivity 1.55×10^-9 S cm^-1. Li||Li symmetric cells with PFIGE retained ultrastable cycling over 300 h with suppressed dendrites. Li||NCM523 full cells: 92.1% capacity retention over 300 cycles at 0.5 C (2.8–4.3 V); 88.5% after 100 cycles at 1 C; at high cutoff 4.6 V, 78.9% capacity retention; at 80 °C, 61.4% capacity after 100 cycles. Pouch cells (0.4 Ah) with PFIGE@Li show 78.7% capacity retention over 50 cycles, and improved thermal safety metrics in ARC: T1 = 190.3 °C (vs 97.7 °C for LE), T2 = 225.6 °C (vs 139.8 °C), T3 = 286.3 °C (vs 402.1 °C for LE, reduced), and thermal runaway induction period extended from 1200 min (LE) to 3050 min. PFIGE also shows flame-retardant behavior with lower HRR (428 kW m^-2) and THR (42.43 MJ m^-2) than LE. These results directly evidence PFIGE’s application as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte enabling stable, high-voltage and high-temperature Li metal batteries.

SIC_GPE-40 is a single-ion conducting gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries (coin cells and Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric configuration). Reported properties specific to SIC_GPE-40 include ionic conductivity of 3.7×10^-7 S/cm at 25 °C and 3×10^-6 S/cm at 60 °C (Fig. 2a). The lithium transference number was determined to be ~0.85 ± 0.1 for both SIC_GPE-40 and SIC_GPE-60. CCD testing for SIC_GPE-40 showed increasing polarization up to the safety limit (5 V) at 0.60 mA/cm^2 (3C) (Fig. 2c). No full-cell cycling data, discharge capacity, or regeneration data were reported for SIC_GPE-40 specifically; therefore capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

SIC_GPE-60 is used as a lithium single-ion conducting gel polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. CCD: "SIC_GPE-60 ... maintaining a stable plateau at high current densities up to 2 mA cm^-2 (10C)... the stable CCD up to 2 mA cm^-2 showcases the material's strong potential" (Fig. 2d; text). Additional performance for SIC_GPE-60: ionic conductivity 1.1×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; LTN ≈ 0.85 ± 0.1 (Fig. 2a,b). Electrochemical stability: main oxidation over 5.0 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 3b), with minimal currents near 4.0–4.6 V (<1 µA cm^-2; Fig. 3c). Li symmetric cells show stable plating/stripping with overvoltage 160 mV at 50 °C and 300 mV at 25 °C at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3e). Full cells at 40 °C: LFP retention 95% after 50 cycles (CE 99.7%), V2O5 retention >96% after 50 cycles, NMC622 retention 97% (CE >99.0%) (Fig. 4). Highlights: "SIC_GPE-60 resists dendritic lithium growth and shows homogeneous Li plating."

SIC_GPE-80 (PEGDA 9.2 wt%, PEaMI-g-LiESTFSI 4 wt%, PVdF-HFP 6.8 wt%, PC 80 wt%) showed plasticizer oozing and was excluded from further investigation: "while no plasticizer oozing is observed in the SIC_GPE-40 and SIC_GPE-60 samples, it is highly evident in the SIC_GPE-80 composition... Therefore, this composition was excluded from further investigation." No electrochemical or battery performance data were reported for SIC_GPE-80.

Cells were built as Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 using [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF with 40 wt% IL: "Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells were produced with [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF ... with 40 wt% IL content." The 5th-cycle room-temperature performance at C/8 shows: "the cell with PVDF polymer exhibits the highest discharge capacity with about 148 mAh.g−1" (Fig. 9a). Electrochemical stability window for 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF shows "no oxidation/reduction peaks between 0.0 and 5.0 V" (Fig. 8d). The ionic conductivity for [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF at 25 °C is reported in Table 4 as "0.013 × 10−3 S/cm (25 °C)". Cycling shows large fade for the PVDF matrix: "capacity fade over 50 cycles is 70%" (Fig. 9b).

For 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF-HFP, the highest room-temperature ionic conductivity is 0.15 mS.cm−1 (Section 3.4: “The highest room temperature ionic conductivity value is 0.15 mS.cm−1 for 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF-HFP”). Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells using this SPE show 124 mAh.g−1 at C/8 and room temperature, with 16% capacity fade over 50 cycles and Coulombic efficiency >98% (Section 3.5; Fig. 9a,b; Table 4). CV shows no oxidation/reduction peaks between 0.0 and 5.0 V (Fig. 8d).

PE 4 is a polymer electrolyte used in Li|LiFePO4 cells. Table 3 reports for LFP|PE 4|Li: “discharge specific capacity after 50 cycles at the C/10 rate … is … 168 mAh g−1.” It also notes at 1C the discharge capacity remains ≥116 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles with ~99% coulombic efficiency. Additional properties directly measured for PE 4 include ionic conductivity 3.21 mS cm−1 at 25 °C (Table 2; reiterated in Conclusions: “The highest ionic conductivity of 3.21 mS cm−1 at 25 °C was obtained for … PE 4”) and a Li+ transference number of approximately 0.033 (“In the case of the polymer electrolyte PE 4 … t_Li+ … approximately 0.033”). The impedance of Li|PE 4|Li after galvanostatic cycling is reported as 120 Ω, indicating a low-resistance SEI.

The [LiTFSA]/[SL] = 1/3 solution is explicitly used as the parent liquid electrolyte for Li batteries and gel electrolytes. It is described as a glass-forming liquid that maintains the liquid state over a wide temperature range (Fig. S1). Its Li-ion transference number at 30 °C in Li/Li symmetric cells under anion-blocking conditions is reported as 0.56: “the PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n = 1) (0.45) was lower than that of the parent electrolyte (0.56).” Ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability of the parent solution are shown/compared in figures (Fig. 3a, S12) but without tabulated numeric values in the text; oxidative stability of gels is stated as comparable to the parent solution. These results support its application as a battery electrolyte transporting Li+.

For the solution of polymerized PMA (PPMA), the TFSA− S–N–S Raman peak was comparable to the [LiTFSA]/[SL]=1/3 solution and shifted slightly to lower wavenumber at higher PPMA content (n ≥ 1.5), indicating weak coordination of PPMA carbonyl oxygen to Li+ and slight dissociation of CIPs/AGGs. No quantitative electrolyte performance metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity, tLi+) were reported for the PPMA solution; such values were only given for crosslinked PPMA gel electrolytes.

PTFPMA-based gel electrolytes (prepared from [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA]) were applied in Li/LiCoO2 batteries. Key performance: the Li-ion transference number increased with polymer content and reached 0.70 for [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA] = 1/3/2 (text: "reached 0.70 for [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA] = 1/3/2"). In Li/LiCoO2 cells using the PTFPMA gel electrolyte ([LiTFSA]/[SL]/[monomer unit] = 1/3/1), the discharge capacity stayed nearly constant up to 0.75 mA cm^-2 and the capacity retention at 1.50 mA cm^-2 (1C) was 80% relative to the theoretical capacity (text: "The capacity retentions at 1.50 mA cm^-2 (1C) ... were ... 80% for the ... PTFPMA cell"). Specific adsorption/conversion capacities, selectivities, or t50-type breakthrough times are not directly defined for this electrolyte in the schema, so those fields are left null.

Evidence specific to PDEGMA solution: “for the solution of polymerized DEGMA (PDEGMA), the TFSA peak clearly shifted toward a lower wavenumber… suggesting that this polymer enhanced the dissociation of CIPs and AGGs.” and “the Raman peak for SO2 vibration of SL in the PDEGMA solution appeared at lower wavenumber… indicating that the fraction of free SL… was higher in the PDEGMA solution due to the complex formation of PDEGMA with Li+.” These establish its role as an Li+-coordinating electrolyte medium, but no quantitative transport metrics (e.g., conductivity, t_Li+) are reported for the PDEGMA solution itself; reported t_Li+ = 0.45 pertains to the PDEGMA gel electrolyte, not the solution.

For PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n=1) with [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[monomer unit] = 1/3/1 at 30 °C, the Li-ion transference number was reported as 0.45 under anion-blocking conditions in Li/Li symmetric cells: “the t_Li+ of the PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n = 1) (0.45) was lower than that of the parent electrolyte (0.56).” (Fig. 3b).
Li-ion transference number measured at 30 °C: "The t_Li+ value increased with the polymer content and reached 0.70 for [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA] = 1/3/2." (Fig. 3b caption states data at 30 °C).

Al-MCM41@HD shows a wider electrochemical stability window (≈4.83 V) (Fig. 2i). When the ratio of HFBA to DMA is 9:1, the ionic conductivity reaches 1.09×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 3c). Al-MCM41@HD exhibits a remarkable t_Li+ up to 0.81 (Fig. 3d). Li|Al-MCM41@HD|Li delivers steady Li plating/stripping for 4000 h with ≈153 mV polarization at 0.2 mA cm^-2/0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4c).

The PI/PV hybrid separator is used in Li-metal and Li-ion cells. Its Li+ ionic conductivity is reported as 3.3 mS cm-1 (EIS, Fig. 4d). The Li+ transference number tLi+ is 0.70 (chronoamperometry/EIS, Fig. 4e,f). Additional application evidence: LSV stability window 5.1 V (Fig. 5a); Li/Cu anode-free half cells at 0.5 mA_cm-2 and 2 mAh_cm-2 show CE 96.9% initially and 97% after 65 cycles (Fig. 5d); MCMB/NMC full cells deliver first discharge capacity 2.3 mAh_cm-2 with 86.1% retention after 200 cycles at 0.5 mA_cm-2, and 1.4 mAh_cm-2 at 2 mA_cm-2 (rate) (Fig. 6c–f); after 140°C storage for 1 h, Cu/NMC cell with PI/PV still gives 1.4 mAh_cm-2 at the 10th cycle with 94.5% CE (Fig. 6a).

Ionic conductivity: "A promising ionic conductivity of 2.0 × 10^-4 S/cm is achieved at 35 °C." (Figure 3a). Li+ transference number: "Using Bruce et al.'s equation, the t_Li+ of LiBFSIE was calculated to be 0.93..." (Figure 3c,d). Electrochemical stability: "No obvious oxidation peaks were witnessed, even at 4.8 V" (Figure 3b). Cell-level outcome: "a promising average Coulombic efficiency of 99.95% was observed... for 200 cycles" (Figure 6d).

P(OEM-DMS-FPM) was used as a solid polymer electrolyte and binder in Li/PE/NCM Li metal polymer batteries. For the P(OEM7-DMS2-FPM1) variant, the highest practical ionic conductivity was 1.1×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (and 1.1×10^-6 S cm^-1 at 20 °C); Li+ transference number T_Li+ = 0.38. The electrochemical oxidation threshold was ~4.5 V in LSV. In full cells, initial discharge capacity reached 126 mAh g^-1 at 4.0 V cutoff and retained 76% after 200 cycles at 4.1 V. Li/PE/Li symmetric cells showed stable cycling for 580 h at 0.6 mA cm^-2 with polarization <0.2 V.

PEO-based solid polymer electrolytes are used for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries and even commercially (e.g., Bollore BlueCar). However, pristine PEO suffers from low ionic conductivity (<1e-5 S_cm^-1 at room temperature), low Li+ transference number (0.1–0.2), and a narrow electrochemical stability window (~3.8 V). In this work, the control PEO/LiTFSI electrolyte shows an ionic conductivity of 2.06×10^-4 S_cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2e) and begins to decompose at ~3.8 V vs Li/Li+ by LSV (Fig. 2f). In Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cells it cycles ~130 h with an average overpotential of 128 mV before short-circuit. In LFP|PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells, discharge capacities are 148.9, 143.7, 136.8, and 121.4 mAh_g^-1 at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, and 1.0 C, respectively, and the capacity decays to near 0 mAh_g^-1 by ~350 cycles at 0.5 C (initial 118.9 mAh_g^-1). These data directly pertain to PEO-based electrolyte performance.

F-PEO based electrolyte shows an impressive ionic conductivity of 3.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (60 °C) and enhanced electrochemical window (4.3 V versus Li/Li+). The all-solid-state Li|Li symmetrical battery with the F-PEO/LiTFSI electrolyte showed an excellent cycling stability (2000 h) and a low average polarization potential (56 mV). The all-solid-state LFP|Li battery based on F-PEO/LiTFSI demonstrated a high capacity (151.0 mAh g^-1) and cycling stability after 500 cycles (capacity retention of 74.3%) at 0.5 C; at 1.0 C, the battery delivered 141.5 mAh g^-1 and long-term stability of 900 cycles. The temperature-related ionic conductivities ... The conductivity reaches to 3.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C. The tLi+ of the F-PEO/LiTFSI electrolyte attains 0.19 at 60 °C. LSV shows no decomposition below 4.3 V (vs. Li/Li+).

Direct data for PEO/LiTFSI: Ionic conductivity at 60°C is 2.06 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 2e: “the conductivity reaches to 3.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60°C… around 1.6 times higher than that of the PEO/LiTFSI (2.06 × 10^-4 S cm^-1)”). Additional PEO/LiTFSI performance (not encoded due to schema limits): LSV shows decomposition at ~3.8 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2f). Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell lifespan ~130 h with average overpotential 128 mV before short-circuit (Fig. 3a). Critical current density 0.25 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S13). Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Cu interface areal capacity consumption ~0.02 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. S14). In LFP|PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells: rate capacities 148.9, 143.7, 136.8, 121.4 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 C (Fig. 6a); at 0.5 C initial discharge 118.9 mAh g^-1 with rapid decay after 200 cycles to near 0 mAh g^-1 by ~350 cycles (Fig. 6c).

F-PEO/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. Reported performance: ionic conductivity of 3.32×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C (Fig. 2e); expanded electrochemical stability window to 4.3 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2f); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.19 at 60 °C; Li|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell cycles stably for 2000 h with low average polarization (~56 mV); LFP|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li delivers 151.0 mAh g^-1 initially at 0.5 C with 74.3% retention after 500 cycles, and at 1.0 C shows 141.5 mAh g^-1 initially and 99.8 mAh g^-1 after 900 cycles.
The provided text only includes associated content, author information, and acknowledgments. No R&D results, figures, tables, or explicit performance metrics related to sample FGPE are present.

In NCM811||Li cells, the LS-based separator delivered discharge specific capacities of 202, 195, 184, 174, and 160 mAh/g at 0.1–2 C (Figure 5a). It showed an initial 179 mAh/g at 0.5 C and 113 mAh/g after 400 cycles (capacity retention 62%) (Figure 5d). LS-based Li||Li symmetric cells operated >1000 h with 34 mV overpotential at 1 mA/cm2 and 1 mAh/cm2, and LS exhibited tLi+ = 0.80 and j0 = 1.963 mA/cm2 (Figures 2i–j, 3b; Conclusion).

PZI-F is used as the solid-state electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported cell performances using PZI-F include: LFP||Li first discharge capacity 154.53 mAh/g at room temperature (Fig. 4b); after 150 cycles capacity 144.16 mAh/g with 93.2% retention (Fig. 4b). Additional evidence of applicability: NCM811||Li initial 161.3 mAh/g with 70% retention after 400 cycles at 0.3C (Fig. 4c); at −10°C, LFP||Li cycled 800 cycles with 105.25 mAh/g (Fig. 4d). Electrolyte metrics supporting use: ionic conductivity 9.05×10^-4 S/cm at room temperature and 1.39×10^-4 S/cm at −30°C (Fig. 3a); Li+ transference number 0.83 (Fig. 3b); electrochemical window up to 5.3 V (Fig. 3c); Li||Li symmetric cells show ~0.05 V polarization for >1500 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 4a) and at −10°C ~78.4 mV overpotential stable to 4000 h (Fig. 6a).

PZI is used as a solid-state electrolyte for Li+ transport in Li metal batteries. Evidence: (1) Room-temperature ionic conductivity of PZI is 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 ('At room temperature, the ionic conductivities of PZI, PZI-F, PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI are calculated to be 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 9.05×10^-4 S cm^-1 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 and 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively.'). (2) Li+ transference number t_Li+ for PZI is 0.73 ('The t_Li+ values of PZI-F and PZI reached 0.83 and 0.73, respectively.'). (3) Electrochemical stability window of PZI is 4.7 V vs Li/Li+ ('PZI has an electrochemical window of 4.7 V'). (4) In Li||Li symmetric cells with PZI, the polarization voltage is ~0.045 V and a short circuit occurs after 547 h ('the Li||Li symmetric cell with PZI maintains a stable polarisation voltage of 0.045 V... short-circuited after 547 h').

PZI-LiTFSI is a single-salt solid polymer electrolyte (prepared with only LiTFSI). At room temperature, the ionic conductivities of PZI, PZI-F, PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI are calculated to be 7.11×10^-4, 9.05×10^-4, 5.24×10^-4 and 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively (Fig. 3a). The Li+ transference numbers: PZI-F and PZI reached 0.83 and 0.73, respectively, which were significantly higher than those of PZI-LiPF6 (0.63), PZI-LiTFSI (0.62) (Fig. S9). After the temperature is raised to 100 °C, the ionic conductivity of PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI of the single-salt system increase slowly below 100 °C, and even a decrease in ionic conductivity is observed.
Room-temperature ionic conductivity for PZI-LiPF6 is reported as 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 3a text). The Li+ transference number for PZI-LiPF6 is 0.63 (Fig. S9 mentioned in main text).

For LFPP 1%-Li specifically: “The symmetric cell with LFPP 1% -Li exhibits the lowest and most stable over-potential at 0.038 V for over 1000 h at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2.” Table 1 (Li//LFP): at 1C after rate performance, LFPP 1%-Li shows CE 98.8% and capacity retention 57.9%; at 0.5C, CE 100.0% and capacity retention 77.7%.

For LFPP 2%-Li anode: Table 1 reports capacity retention 75.8% and average coulombic efficiency 99.0% at 1C after 390 cycles in Li//LFP full cells. At 0.5C, CE is 100.0% with capacity retention 77.5%. In symmetric LFPP-Li||LFPP-Li cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and 1 mAh cm^-2, the samples with 5% and 2% precursor concentrations show stable over-potential of 0.7–1.0 V for about 950 h, then a sharp increase after ~1100 h.

EIS at room temperature with SS blocking electrodes showed: "lithium-ion conductivities of all the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs with various contents of PET4A can reach as high as up to 10^-3 S cm^-1 at room temperature" (Fig. 1a). This applies to LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF. The text also states specifically for this sample: "the ionic conductivity of LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF with 5.4 mol% of PET4A decreases obviously ... resulting in low ionic conductivity." No specific numeric value beyond the all-samples statement is provided for LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF.

LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF SIPE with 9.7 mol% of PET4A shows the highest ionic conductivity up to 5.81 mS cm^-1 at room temperature (Fig. 1a; Table 1). Table 1 lists ESW = 5.2 V for LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF. Temperature dependence (Fig. 5) also shows LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF reaches 5.81 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C and 1.28×10^-2 S cm^-1 at 60 °C. The material is a single-ion polymer electrolyte (SIPE) for lithium batteries.

LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF is used as a single-ion polymer electrolyte in Li batteries. It is reported as “the best balanced choice... showing an ionic conductivity of up to 2.32 mS cm−1 at room temperature” (Fig. 1a and 4d; section 2.4). The transference number of LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE is 0.91 (Fig. 6a). LSV on SS|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF|Li shows an electrochemical stability window up to 5.2 V (Fig. 6b). Symmetric Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF|Li cells cycle stably for ~1200 h at 1 mA cm−2 with ~60 mV polarization (Fig. 6c). In Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF|LFP cells at 0.2C, the initial discharge capacity is 138 mA h g−1 and reaches 140 mA h g−1 after 230 cycles (Fig. 1b; section 2.1/2.7).

LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF is listed in Table 2 (LiSFSI:PETMP:PET4A = 4:3:2; PET4A 22.2 mol%). The text states that “lithium-ion conductivities of all the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs with various contents of PET4A can reach as high as up to 10^{-3} S cm^{-1} at room temperature” (Fig. 1a section), and that when PET4A > 9.7% ionic conductivities decrease with increasing crosslinking density (which includes this sample). However, no specific per-sample ionic conductivity value, Li+ transference number, ESW, or battery cycling data are reported explicitly for LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF; detailed battery tests are shown for LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF. Thus only the application context (Li-ion battery polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction) can be assigned without guessing per-sample metrics.
