Celgard 2400 is explicitly used as a commercial PP separator for LIB comparison: "a Celgard 2400 membrane ... was used as a LIB separator for comparison" (25 μm). Its ionic conductivity is reported in Table 1: "σ (mS cm-1) ... PP 0.66" and derived from EIS blocking-cell measurements (stainless steel/electrolyte-soaked separator/stainless steel) at 25 °C. Additional related performance for Celgard 2400 in this work: porosity 40%, electrolyte uptake 60%, contact angle 45°, electrolyte retention after 5 days 52%; extensive thermal shrinkage at 150 °C for 1 h; TGA onset ~350 °C; LSV shows no measurable decomposition below 5 V vs Li/Li+; in LiFePO4||Li coin cells at 0.5 C and 25 °C, discharge capacity retention after 300 cycles is 86.1% (initial ~135.0 mAh g-1, rises to 137.9 mAh g-1 then declines), and at 60 °C retention is 77.2% (134.9→104.1 mAh g-1).

Table 1 reports ionic conductivity σ for FPEEK-0.25 as 3.12 mS cm−1. The text states: “the ionic conductivity was calculated to be … 3.12 mS cm−1 for … FPEEK-0.25” (Fig. 7a/Table 1). Additional related performance for this separator includes initial discharge capacity 150.5 mAh g−1 (Fig. 10) and ~93% capacity retention after 300 cycles for all electrospun separators (Fig. 9), and 85.3% retention at 60 °C after 300 cycles (Fig. 11).

FPEEK-0.5 was tested as an electrospun porous separator for Li-ion batteries. Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity σ = 2.86 mS cm−1 (unit normalized to mS/cm), with porosity 85% and electrolyte uptake 534%. Contact angle was 8°, and electrolyte retention after 5 days was 69%. In symmetric SS|separator|SS cells, σ values were calculated from EIS (R_b) at 25 °C. LSV showed no measurable decomposition below 5 V vs Li/Li+. In full Li/LiFePO4 coin cells (0.5 C, 2.7–4.2 V), the initial discharge capacity with FPEEK-0.5 separator was 146.9 mAh/g, and electrospun separators (including FPEEK-0.5) retained ~93% capacity after 300 cycles at 25 °C; at 60 °C the FPEEK-0.5 cell retained 83.0% after 300 cycles. FPEEK-0.5 exhibited negligible thermal shrinkage at 150 °C for 1 h and thermal degradation onset ~450 °C (TGA).

PEEK was electrospun into porous fibrous separators for application in LIBs (Section 2.3). The ionic conductivity of the electrolyte-soaked PEEK separator was reported as 2.71 mS cm-1 (Table 1; Fig. 7a discussion). Additional PEEK-specific performance/context: porosity 92% and electrolyte uptake 520% (Table 1); contact angle 9° (Fig. 5B); negligible thermal shrinkage at 150 °C and degradation onset ~450 °C (Fig. 6); electrochemical stability window >5 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 7b). Cells with electrospun separators (including PEEK) showed ~93% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 25 °C (Fig. 9), and for PEEK specifically 81.6% retention after 300 cycles at 60 °C (Fig. 11). Initial discharge capacity with PEEK separator: 143.9 mAh g-1 (Fig. 10b).

F-cHBC (T-F-cHBC anode) is used as an anode for Li-ion storage: "We carefully characterized the galvanostatic discharge-charge behavior of the F-cHBC P2_1/c crystal phase ... (0.02–3.00 V vs Li/Li+)." It "provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mA h g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1" (Figure 3A). High-rate performance also reported: "maintained a capacity of 100 mA h g−1 at a high current density of 7000 mA g−1" and long-cycle data: "Even at the 400th cycle, the anode had a stable coulombic efficiency of 99.4% and specific capacity of 200 mA h g−1" (Figure 3C,D).

Li-ion cell with T-F-cHBC anode: “provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mAh g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1” (Figure 3A). Rate capability: “maintained a capacity of 100 mAh g−1 at a high current density of 7000 mA g−1 and delivered a … recovery capacity of 160 mAh g−1 at the 42nd cycle (400 mA g−1)” (Figure 3C). Cycling: “Even at the 400th cycle, the anode had a stable coulombic efficiency of 99.4% and specific capacity of 200 mAh g−1” at 1000 mA g−1 (Figure 3D).

SPE membranes (EO/Li = 30:1) were prepared with PEO and lithium (alkoxyfluorovinylether) sulfonate salts including the monolithium salt 1; ionic conductivities (Scm^-1) were measured by EIS over 120 °C to room temperature (Fig. 7 shows Arrhenius plots for monolithium salts 1–3). For salt 1, VTF parameters are reported: T0 = -55 °C, A = 0.1 K1/2 S/cm, B = 563 K, Δμ = 10.0 ± 2.5 kJ/mol (Table 2). Conductivity curves are provided but no explicit numeric conductivity values for salt 1 are listed.

PEO-based SPE membranes (EO/Li = 30:1) were fabricated and evaluated by EIS; Arrhenius plots include the dilithium series (4–6), explicitly covering salt 4: “Arrhenius plots for SPEs made of LiTFSI, LiTf or a dilithium (alkoxyfluorovinylether) sulfonate salt (4-6) and PEO, EO/Li = 30:1.” Ionic conductivities for each sample were measured on cooling from 120 °C to room temperature using stainless-steel blocking electrodes. All SPEs show an abrupt conductivity drop near ~60 °C (melting/freezing of PEO), which applies to salt 4 as well. Quantitative conductivity values for salt 4 are only shown as plots (no tabulated σ), so no numeric conductivity is extracted. VTF fit parameters for salt 4 are tabulated (Table 2): T0 = -42 °C, A = 0.1 (K1/2·S/cm), B = 462 K, Δμ = 8.1 ± 2.1 kJ/mol; salt concentration in SPE = 21.7 wt%. Thermal properties for the salt-4 SPE (Table 1): Tg = -17 °C, Tm = 61 °C, ΔHcorr = -107 J/g, χDSC = 65%. The conclusions note that conductivities with new salts are generally lower than LiTFSI/LiTf, with the exception of salt 4.

DF_{0.24}-20 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Ionic conductivity at 60°C is explicitly given: “the ionic conductivities at 60°C obey the following order... DF_{0.24}-20 = 0.11 mS/cm” (Section 2.5). Long-term Li||Li symmetric cell stability with DF_{0.24}-20 is reported: “the cell applying DF_{0.24}-20 exhibits nearly 6000 h ultra-stable cycling, with a long-period stable overpotential (90–180 mV) for 300–5000 h” (Section 2.2 (2)); also “shows the best performance, with stable cycling for over 5000 h and an overpotential below 100 mV” (Figure 4A, Section 2.6). Full-cell validation includes Li|DF_{0.24}-20|LCO with 80% capacity retention after 300 cycles (Section 2.2 (3), Section 2.7), and improved retention for NCM cathodes (Section 2.7). These confirm the application as a battery electrolyte for Li+ transport.

Aquivion-87 in Li+ form solvated by EC-DMA was used as a polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. LFP|Li cells with Aquivion-87-EC-DMA showed initial discharge capacity of 142 mAh/g at 0.1 C (Results & Discussion: “initial discharge capacity of LFP|Li batteries with Aquivion-87-EC-DMA … was 142 mAh/g at 0.1 C”). The Li+ transference number for Aquivion-87-EC-DMA was 0.69 (Table 4: TLi+ = 0.69). Supporting performance metrics for the same material/solvent include σ(25 °C) = 1.08×10^-3 S/cm (Table 2), Li|Li overvoltage ±262 mV and interface resistivity 400 Ω/cm2 (Table 5), 136 mAh/g at 1 C and 98.8% capacity retention after 70 cycles (Results & Discussion). For comparison, Aquivion-87-EC-PC delivered 141 mAh/g at 0.1 C, TLi+ = 0.88, and σ(25 °C) = 0.9×10^-4 S/cm.

For Aquivion-98 in Li+ form as gel polymer electrolyte: LFP|Li cells with Aquivion-98–EC-DMA showed an initial discharge capacity of 136 mAh/g at 0.1C (Results: “The initial discharge capacity of LFP|Li batteries with Aquivion-87-EC-DMA and Aquivion-98-EC-DMA electrolytes was … 136 mAh/g at a cycling rate of 0.1C”). Li+ transference number TLi+ = 0.69 for Aquivion-98–EC-DMA (Table 4: “Aquivion-98-EC-DMA … TLi+ 0.69”). Supporting electrolyte properties for Aquivion-98–EC-DMA: σ(25 °C) = 9.8 × 10^-4 S/cm (Table 2) and overvoltage ±119 mV, interface resistance 206 Ω/cm2 (Table 5). For comparison, Aquivion-98–EC-PC shows 132 mAh/g at 0.1C and TLi+ = 0.93.

PT-0 is the non-fluorinated polymer electrolyte tested in Li|PE|Li and Li|PE|NCM811 cells. Reported metrics: (i) electrochemical stability (LSV, 30 °C): “the non-fluorinated sample PT-0 underwent anodic oxidation starting at approximately 4.5 V” (Fig. 3d); (ii) Li+ transference number: “PT-0 (0.42)” (Figs. 3f, S10); (iii) ion-transport activation energy: “calculated Ea values were 0.24, 0.25, 0.30, and 0.37 eV for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively” (Fig. 3c); (iv) full-cell behavior: “the Li||NCM811 cell containing non-fluorinated sample PT-0 showed rapid capacity fading and poor Coulombic efficiency due to its limited oxidative stability and inadequate interfacial compatibility” (Fig. S16).

PT-0.5 is a fluorinated PEGMEA–TFEMA copolymer electrolyte tested in Li|PT-0.5|NCM811 cells. Reported coin-cell discharge capacities: 181.5, 169.9, 145.3, and 120 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C, respectively (Fig. 5a,b). Long-term cycling: "Li|PT-0.5|NCM811 cell retained a reversible specific capacity of 118.06 mAh g^-1 with an impressive capacity retention of 81.47% ... through 200 ... cycles at 0.5C" (Figs. 5c and text). Additional electrolyte performance directly for PT-0.5: ionic conductivity 0.329 mS cm^-1 at 30°C (Fig. 3a,b); t_Li+ = 0.64 (Figs. 3f, S10); oxidative stability up to 5.2 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 3d) and low leakage current up to 4.8 V (Fig. 3e); Li|PT-0.5|Li symmetric cell stable plating/stripping >1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~39 mV overpotential (Fig. 4d–g), 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S12), and CCD of 1.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S13). Pouch cell with PT-0.5 delivered 144.96 mAh g^-1 with 87.9% retention over 100 cycles at 0.2C (Fig. 5f,g).

PT-1 is one composition of the PEGMEA–TFEMA copolymer electrolyte tested in Li|PE|Li and Li|PE|NCM811 cells. Quantitative metric explicitly reported for PT-1: “The calculated tLi+ values for the fluorinated copolymers PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 (0.64, 0.58, and 0.51, respectively)…” (Figs. 3f and S10). Additional property specific to PT-1 but not mapped to fields: activation energies Ea = 0.30 eV for PT-1 (“The calculated Ea values were 0.24, 0.25, 0.30, and 0.37 eV for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively.”). Other performance (ionic conductivity at 30 °C, LSV stability, overpotentials, cycling data) are not given as explicit numeric values for PT-1 in the text, so left null.

PT-2 is a fluorinated PEGMEA–TFEMA copolymer electrolyte used in Li-metal batteries (Li|PE|NCM811). For PT-2, the reported electrochemical metrics include: ionic conductivity at 30°C = 0.118 mS/cm (stated as increasing from 0.118 mS/cm at 1:2 (PT-2) to 0.329 mS/cm at 1:0.5 (PT-0.5)); activation energy Ea = 0.37 eV (values given explicitly for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, PT-2); oxidative stability window up to 5.4 V vs Li/Li+ (PT-2 showed further improved oxidative stability extending up to 5.4 V); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.51 (reported for PT-0.5, PT-1, PT-2 as 0.64, 0.58, 0.51). In Li symmetric and full cells, PT-2 exhibited higher polarization and micro short circuits and showed comparatively lower rate performance and capacity retention versus PT-0.5; no numeric full-cell capacities are provided for PT-2.

For F&C-PE: “the Li+ conductivity of F&C-PE reaches 1.06 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 30 °C” (Fig. 2d). In Li/Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm−2, “the Li/F&C-PE/Li cell cycles stably for 2000 h at a low polarization voltage of 50 mV” (Fig. 3a). Transference number: “t_Li+ for F&C-PE is 0.53” (Fig. 2f). Oxidative stability: “high oxidation resistance voltage (4.5 V)” (Conclusions). Full-cell performance evidence with this electrolyte: at 0.2C after activation, the Li/F&C-PE/NCM811 cell shows “capacity retention of 82.2% and Coulombic efficiency of 99.8% after 300 cycles” (Fig. 4b).

FGPE1:1 is reported as a fluorinated gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries, with full-cell data: "the full cell assembled with FGPE1:1 demonstrates stable cycling over 200 cycles at 0.2C within a voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V, with a capacity retention of 87%. Even at 1C, it delivers a high discharge capacity of 126.17 mAh g−1." Additional electrolyte metrics for FGPE1:1 include t_Li+ = 0.68 and ionic conductivity σ = 1.21 × 10^−3 S cm^−1 at 25 °C, and higher coulombic efficiency (92.78%) vs. LE (62.77%) over 10 cycles, supporting its role as a battery electrolyte.

Evidence that LLZTO is used as an application-relevant component in a solid-state electrolyte: "the LLZTO nanoparticles also act as the ion-conducting ceramic filler to incorporate with PVHM, resulting in the PVHML composite SSEs." For PE-40% LCE@PEG: ionic conductivity at 60°C = 1.21e-4 S/cm (EIS); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.415; electrochemical stability window shows no obvious oxidation peak until ~4.3 V vs Li/Li+ for PE-x% (includes 40%); critical current density in Li/PE-40%LCE@PEG/Li symmetric cell at 60°C = 1.1 mA/cm2. Specific capacity data in assembled LiFePO4//Li cells were only detailed for x=50%, thus capacity is left null for x=40%.

PE-50% LCE@PEG is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in solid-state Li metal batteries. Electrolyte properties for PE-50% LCE@PEG: ionic conductivity 1.73×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60°C; Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.425; electrochemical stability window with no obvious oxidation until 4.3 V vs Li/Li+. Li/Li symmetric cells with PE-50% LCE@PEG show a critical current density of 1.1 mA cm^-2 and stable cycling for 1200 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2 at 60°C. In LiFePO4//Li full cells, PE-50% LCE@PEG enables: no capacity decay over 150 cycles at 0.5 C and 60°C; rate capacities of 164.8/165/160/157/152/142/63.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2/3/5 C (60°C); at 80°C and 0.5 C, an initial reversible capacity of 160.3 mAh g^-1 and 158.5 mAh g^-1 at the 100th cycle with 98.87% capacity retention.

PE-60% LCE@PEG is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in Li/Li symmetric cells and LiFePO4//Li batteries. For PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60), the ionic conductivities at 60 °C are 1.21×10^-4, 1.73×10^-4, and 2.11×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively (thus 2.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 for PE-60%). The Li+ transference numbers are 0.415, 0.425, and 0.5 (thus 0.5 for PE-60%). The critical current densities are 1.1, 1.1, and 0.9 mA cm^-2 (thus 0.9 mA cm^-2 for PE-60%). LSV shows no obvious oxidation peak for PE-x% LCE@PEG until 4.3 V vs Li/Li+, applicable to PE-60% as well.

GPE-0 (0 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12) is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU gel polymer electrolyte evaluated for Na-ion batteries. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity is 1.87×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4b). Additional evidence on GPE-0 performance: electrochemical window 5.06 V (Fig. 4d); Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.33 (Fig. 5a); in Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPE-0|Na cell at 0.5C, first discharge capacity 88.8 mAh g^-1 and 85.0 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles with 95.7% retention (Fig. 6a); cell impedance 422 Ω initially and 1030 Ω after 100 cycles (Fig. 6c).

For Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na cells at 0.5C, the first discharge specific capacity was 88.8, 92.1, 92.7, 90.9 and 90.6 mAh·g−1 for GPE-0, GPE-2, GPE-4, GPE-6 and GPE-8, respectively. After 100 cycles, the discharge capacity was 85.0, 90.9, 92.0, 90.1 and 89.8 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention ratios of 95.7%, 98.7%, 99.2%, 99.1% and 99.1%, respectively. Additionally, the ionic conductivity at room temperature for GPE-2 was 2.40×10^−3 S·cm^−1.

GPE-4 (PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU with 4 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12) was evaluated as the electrolyte in Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPE|Na coin cells. At 0.5C, the first discharge specific capacity is 92.7 mAh/g and after 100 cycles it is 92.0 mAh/g (99.2% retention). The areal capacity of the GPE-4 cell is 0.177 mAh/cm2. GPE-4 shows room-temperature ionic conductivity 2.83e-3 S/cm, Na+ transference number 0.63, and an electrochemical window of 5.16 V. Cell interfacial resistance with GPE-4 is 365 Ω initially and 615 Ω after 100 cycles. Rate capacities for GPE-4 are 99.8 (0.1C), 98.9 (0.2C), 98.6 (0.5C), 97.6 (1C), 95.5 (2C), and 83.9 mAh/g (5C).

Sample GPE-6 (6 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12 in PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU) is used as the electrolyte in Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na coin cells. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity is 2.15×10^-3 S/cm. In 0.5C cycling tests of Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na, GPE-6 delivered first discharge capacity 90.9 mAh/g and 90.1 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 99.1% retention (conditions: 2.5–4.0 V vs Na/Na+ at RT).

GPE-8 is PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU with 8 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12. In Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na cells at 0.5C, GPE-8 delivered first discharge specific capacity of 90.6 mAh·g^-1 and 89.8 mAh·g^-1 after 100 cycles (99.1% retention). Room-temperature ionic conductivity for GPE-8 was 1.89×10^-3 S·cm^-1; porosity was 10.16%. Cells were cycled between 2.5–4.0 V vs. Na/Na+ at RT.

PVDF (Solef 5130) used as binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes: discharge capacity at C-rate after 50 cycles is 129 mAh·g−1 with 101% retention (Fig. 6d; Conclusions). Also reported: 146 mAh·g−1 at C/10 and 125 mAh·g−1 at C (Fig. 5b-c), but a representative long-term value is 129 mAh·g−1 at C-rate after 50 cycles.

PVDF-HFP (Solef 21216) was used as the binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes (LFP:Super P:binder = 80:10:10 wt%). After cycling at C-rate, “the discharge capacity value after 50 cycles for the cathodes with PVDF‑HFP … [is] 75.5 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention of 84%.” Additional PVDF-HFP data: fifth-cycle discharge capacities 139 mAh·g−1 (C/10) and 97 mAh·g−1 (C); contact angle 20.4 ± 2.2°; cathode DC conductivity 0.48 ± 0.32 S·cm−1; swelling 21.6 ± 1.3% (Table 3).

PVDF-TrFE (Solvene 250, VDF:TrFE = 70/30) was used as a binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes. Reported PVDF-TrFE-specific metrics include: discharge capacities of 130, 133, 124, and 80 mAh·g^-1 at C/10, C/5, C/2, and C (Fig. 5a); at C/10 and C across all binders: 131 and 81 mAh·g^-1 for PVDF‑TrFE (Fig. 5b–c); after 50 cycles at C-rate: 94.3 mAh·g^-1 with capacity retention of 89% (Fig. 6d). Coulombic efficiency: initial 98% and mean 99% (Fig. 6c). Additional PVDF‑TrFE electrode properties: contact angle 18.9 ± 3.0°, DC conductivity 0.40 ± 0.30 S·cm^-1, swelling 12.2 ± 1.2% (Table 3). Li+ diffusion coefficient 2.2×10^-15 cm^2·s^-1; impedance ~852 Ω (Fig. 4b).

NPH-GPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte in Li-metal and LFP//Li cells. Reported ionic conductivity for NPH-130% is 1.21 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and 3.16 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C (Fig. 2a; Section 3.2). Lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.57 for NPH-GPE (Fig. 2e; Section 3.2). Electrochemical stability window shows no obvious decomposition before 5.15 V vs. Li+/Li at ambient temperature (Fig. 2d; Section 3.2). Symmetric Li/NPH-GPE/Li cell runs 500 h at 25 °C without short circuit, overpotential ~0.11 V at 0.1 mA cm^-2; at 70 °C overpotential ~0.04 V at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3b,c; Section 3.3). In LFP/NPH-130%/Li full cells: capacity retention 70% after 500 cycles at 0.5 C and ~99% average coulombic efficiency at 70 °C; at 25 °C, 150 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles with 98.65% retention at 0.2 C (Fig. 4a–c; Section 3.4).

NPH-130% (NPH-GPE with 130% electrolyte uptake) was selected for further evaluation. At 70 °C, LFP/NPH-130%/Li shows 150.67 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles at 0.5 C, and 70% capacity retention after 500 cycles with ~99% average coulombic efficiency (Fig. 4a,b). At 25 °C, after 100 cycles at 0.2 C, the discharge capacity is 150 mAh g^-1 with 98.65% retention (Fig. 4c). NPH-130% ionic conductivity: 1.21×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and 3.16×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C (Fig. 2a). Electrochemical stability window: no decomposition before 5.15 V vs Li+/Li at ambient temperature and 5.2 V at 70 °C (Fig. 2d, S8).

For PH-SPE (PVDF-HFP based solid polymer electrolyte membrane), the paper reports: "The PH-SPE electrolyte with salt concentration of 80 wt.% shows the highest conductivity, 4.786 × 10^−5 S cm^−1 at 25 °C (Fig. S7). Whereas, the conductivity of PH-SPE is still too low for real application." No other performance metrics (e.g., transference number, ESW, cycling) are provided for PH-SPE.

PH-GPE is used as the electrolyte in LFP/PH-GPE/Li cells. At 25 °C (0.2 C), the discharge capacity of LFP/PH-GPE/Li is 134.8 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 4c). At 70 °C (0.5 C), the initial discharge capacity for the PH-GPE cell is 147 mAh g^-1 (Fig. 4a). In Li//Li symmetric cells, PH-GPE shows higher overpotential (>0.2 V) and short-circuits after 300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3b).

The authors define PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2–X% (PPZ–X%, X = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8), so the X = 0 sample corresponds to PPZ-0%. Table 2 lists the ionic conductivity of PVDF-HFP/PMMA (PPZ-0%) as 1.31 mS cm−1 at 25 °C. Additional related performance for the same sample includes t_Li+ = 0.35 (Figure 5c–e) and an electrochemical stability window of 4.46 V (Figure 5f), but only one scalar field can be populated here, so the 25 °C ionic conductivity is used.

PPZ-2% is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 (2 wt%) gel polymer electrolyte used in Li batteries: "The lithium anodes, as-prepared GPEs, and LiFePO4 cathodes were assembled as 2025-type coin cells" (Section 2.3). Electrochemical stability: "all the PVDF-HFP matrix GPEs can withstand 4.4 V; thus they could assemble a battery with a LiFePO4 cathode" (Figure 5f context). Performance (Table 2): for PPZ-2% ionic conductivity (mS cm-1) at 25/35/45/55/65/75 °C = 1.11/1.28/1.32/1.84/2.25/2.42; activation energy Ea = 0.175 eV.

Table 2 lists ionic conductivity for PPZ-4%: 1.22 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (and 1.72, 2.02, 2.30, 2.56, 2.84 mS cm-1 at 35–75 °C), with Ea = 0.165 eV. Other detailed electrochemical metrics (e.g., t_Li+, stability window, battery cycling) are only reported for PPZ-6%, not PPZ-4%.

PPZ-6% is a gel polymer electrolyte used in Li|PPZ-6%|LiFePO4 cells. Electrochemical results show that the cell with PPZ-6% delivered 153.0 mAh g^-1 and ~99% Coulombic efficiency after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (Figure 7a,b). At 1 C, it delivered 150.2 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles with 99.8% capacity retention (Figure 7e). Supporting electrolyte metrics for PPZ-6% include ionic conductivity 1.46 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C (Table 2), Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.50 (Figure 5e), and an electrochemical stability window up to ~4.65 V (Figure 5f). Symmetric Li|PPZ-6%|Li showed stable plating/stripping without short circuit up to 0.8 mA cm^-2 (Figure 6d).

PPZ-8% is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 (8 wt%) gel polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Table 2 reports its ionic conductivity: 0.86 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (with values across 25–75 °C) and activation energy Ea = 0.151 eV. Battery cycling and LSV data are provided mainly for PPZ-6%, not specifically for PPZ-8%, so only ionic conductivity is extracted for PPZ-8%.

No direct application performance is reported for C-1 alone. All performance data are for LiFePO4/C composites that use C-1 as the carbon support (e.g., LFP/C-1). Evidence: 'The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively.' Electrochemical tests and capacities are presented for 'LiFePO4/C composites' (Fig. 6, Table 2: 'LFP/C-1', etc.), not for C-1.

C-2 is the carbon support used to make composite LFP/C-2: "The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively." Table 2 reports the electrochemical capacities for LFP/C-2 (based on the entire weight of the LiFePO4/C composite): 0.1C: 106.5 mA h g-1; 1C: 102.3 mA h g-1; 2C: 95.9 mA h g-1; 5C: 78.4 mA h g-1; 10C: 54.8 mA h g-1. The initial capacity is also stated: "Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1 (based on the weight of composite...)." Cycling note applicable to all composites: "the cycling stabilities are almost the same for all of the LiFePO4/C composites" and they show "nearly 100% capacity retention over 50 cycles" (Fig. 6b). Test conditions: CR2025 half-cells, 2.5–4.2 V, room temperature; electrodes with 80 wt% active, 15 wt% carbon black, 5 wt% PVDF.

No direct application/performance metrics are reported for sample C-3 alone. All electrochemical results (e.g., 144.6 mAh/g at 0.1 C; 128.4 mAh/g at 1 C; 83 mAh/g at 10 C; 96.7% capacity retention over 1000 cycles; LiFePO4 loading 85.4 wt%) are for the composite LFP/C-3 (LiFePO4 loaded into C-3), not for the standalone C-3 material.

Fig. 6a/Table 2: "Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1 (based on the weight of composite, the same below…)"; Table 2 lists LFP/C-1 rate capacities (mA h g−1, composite-basis): 0.1 C 68.1; 0.2 C 59.9; 0.5 C 53.8; 1 C 50.3; 2 C 43.9; 5 C 33.5; 10 C 23.1. Fig. 6 caption: "The specific capacity is based on the entire weight of a LiFePO4/C composite."

LFP/C-2 is evaluated as a LIB cathode: “The electrochemical properties of LiFePO4/C composites were tested in CR-2025 coin half-cells… The specific capacity is based on the entire weight of a LiFePO4/C composite.” Table 2 reports for LFP/C-2 rate capacities (mA h g−1): 0.1 C 106.5; 0.2 C 105.8; 0.5 C 102.2; 1 C 102.3; 2 C 95.9; 5 C 78.4; 10 C 54.8. The text states: “sample LFP/C-3 shows the best electrochemical performance of 128.4 mA h g−1 at 1 C followed by LFP/C-2 (102.3 mA h g−1) … with nearly 100% capacity retention over 50 cycles.”

LFP/C-3 is evaluated as a cathode for lithium-ion batteries: “The electrochemical properties of LiFePO4/C composites were tested in CR-2025 coin half-cells…”. Table 2 lists capacities; for LFP/C-3a (based on the weight of LiFePO4/C), the capacity at 1 C is 128.4 mA h g−1. Figure 6b caption clarifies “The specific capacity is based on the entire weight of a LiFePO4/C composite.” Additional performance for LFP/C-3 includes 144.6 mA h g−1 at 0.1 C, 83 mA h g−1 at 10 C, and long-term cycling: “the LFP/C-3 cell can retain 96.7% of its initial capacity at a high rate of 10 C over 1000 cycles… average specific discharge capacity … 83 mA h g−1 … specific power of 5114 W kg−1.”

Table 2 lists 'Pure-LFP' rate capacities: 0.1 C = 70 mA h g-1; 0.2 C = 58.7; 0.5 C = 46.8; 1 C = 39.1; 2 C = 31.2. Tests used CR2025 coin half-cells, 2.5–4.2 V at room temperature (Fig. 6 caption and Section 2.3). The text also notes pure LiFePO4 shows an initial discharge capacity of ~70 mA h g-1 at 0.1 C.

MDPE is applied as a solid polymer electrolyte (and binder) in lithium metal batteries. Reported performance includes: “a wide electrochemical window (4.9 V vs Li+/Li)” and “the highest ionic conductivity of 3×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C of MDPE … when the lithium salt concentration was 70 wt.%” (Figure 2d,e). The Li+ transference number is “tLi+ = 0.76” (Figure 3f,g). Symmetric cells “Li/MDPE/Li can cycle stably for 1000 h without short-circuiting” (Figure 4a). Full-cell performance: “The cycling stability is significantly improved reaching 170 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C with a capacity retention of more than 80% and coulombic efficiency close to 100% at 60 °C.” Additionally, “the all-solid-state cell with MDFE[MDPE] film as the electrolyte delivers a discharge capacity of >50 mAh g^-1 at 2 C.”

UPE is the ultraviolet-polymerized gel electrolyte membrane without UPy-CNTs. Electrochemical properties reported for UPE include: ionic conductivity 0.177 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 4e–f: “the UPE without the addition of UPy-CNTs is only 0.177 mS cm^-1”); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.36 (Fig. 4g–i); activation energy Ea = 0.224 eV (Fig. 4e); critical current density CCD = 0.3 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4d); in Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2, UPE-based cell cycles 1795 h with overpotential reaching 108 mV at 1795 h (Fig. 5a), and at 0.2 mA cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh cm^-2 it short-circuits at 278 h with initial overpotential 40 mV (Fig. 5b). In Li||LFP full cells, UPE shows larger polarization (e.g., 250 mV at 2C) than UPEC1.0 (Fig. 7a–c).

For UPEC_0.5, the paper reports: "The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC_0.5, UPEC_1.5, and UPEC_2.0 is 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.3 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.8 mA cm^-2, 0.7 mA cm^-2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13)." It also states for Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2: "cells with UPEC_0.5 ... cycle to 1159 h" (Fig. S14). Additionally, at 0.2 mA cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh cm^-2, UPEC_0.5 cycles for 88 h before a short circuit (Fig. S15). These values are directly tied to the UPEC_0.5 electrolyte.

UPEC1.0 full-cell performance is explicitly reported: "The specific capacity of Li||UPEC1.0||LFP cell can reach 146 mAh g^{-1} even after 200 cycles at 0.5C" (Fig. 7d). Additional UPEC1.0 metrics supporting its electrolyte application include: Li||Li symmetric cycling "stable cycling for 2500 h under ... 0.1 mA cm^{-2} and ... 0.1 mAh cm^{-2}. ... overpotential is 22 mV initially and 54 mV at 2500 h" (Fig. 5a); rate performance "167, 160, 150, 141, and 120 mAh g^{-1} (0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, and 2C), respectively" and recovery to "166 mAh g^{-1}" (Fig. 7a); ionic transport "UPEC_{1,0} has an ionic conductivity of 0.226 mS cm^{-1} at 30°C" and "the activation energy ... 0.183 eV" (Fig. 4e,f); transference number "0.79" (Fig. 4g–i); CE in Li||Cu "coulombic efficiency of 97.82% at the 100th cycle"; and CCD "the critical current density of UPEC_{1.0} reaches 0.9 mA cm^{-2}" (Fig. 4c–d). These data are for UPEC_{1.0} (same as UPEC_1).

Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2: “cells with UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 cycle to 1159 h, 1917 h, and 1727 h, respectively” (Fig. S14). Additional related metric: CCD for UPEC1.5 is 0.8 mA cm^-2 (“The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 is 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 0.7 mA cm^-2, respectively,” Fig. 4d and S13). Under 0.2 mA cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh cm^-2, UPEC1.5 cycles 500 h before short circuit (Fig. S15).

UPEC_2.0 is used as an asymmetric gel electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to UPEC_2.0: (1) Critical current density (CCD) = 0.7 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 4d). (2) In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA/cm^2 and 0.1 mAh/cm^2, cycling lasts 1727 h before short circuit (Fig. S14). (3) At 0.2 mA/cm^2 and 0.2 mAh/cm^2, cycling lasts 360 h before short circuit (Fig. S15). No explicit ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, or full-cell capacity values were provided for UPEC_2.0.

Use as LIB anode is explicit: "Application of the carbon-coated silica macroparticles to anodes of Li-ion batteries..." Experimental: coin cell, Li metal counter, electrolyte 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC, current 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+. For SiO2@C specifically: "At the first cycle, the discharge capacity was 281 mAhg^-1 for SiO2@C." Additionally for the same sample: "further cycling gradually enhanced the discharge capacity from 95 mAhg^-1 to 222 mAhg^-1 for SiO2@C."

Relevant to SiO2@C + B: Electrochemical tests for LIB anodes were run at 0.2 mA cm^-2 over 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+ (coin cell, Li counter). In Results 3.3: “enhancement of the specific capacity from 195 mAh^−1 to 503 mAh^−1 was observed especially from SiO2@C + B.” Also: first-cycle discharge capacity was 382 mAh g^-1 for SiO2@C + B. The Conclusions/Highlights note “carbon-coated silica macroparticles processed with boric acid show highest charge capacity of 500 mAh g^-1 after 103 cycles,” consistent with the ~503 mAh g^-1 reported in R&D.

file_name: Adv Funct Materials - 2024 - You - High‐Voltage All‐Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries Enabled by Localized.md

parse.why: NCM811/F-PISE/Li cell exhibits an initial discharge capacity of 157.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 30 °C, with ~98% capacity retention after 500 cycles (Figure 6a). The study evaluates PISEs (specifically F-PISE) as solid polymer electrolytes in NCM811/Li batteries.

DFNCA electrolyte (0.25 M LiNO3 + 0.9 M LiDFOB in DFNCA/FEC 1:1) enabled Li||Cu CE as high as 99% by Aurbach's method (Figure 2a) and an average CE ~97% for 400 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 2b). Li||Li symmetric cells in DFNCA showed stable overpotential (~50 mV) over >1000 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 2i). In Li||NCM811 full cells (0.25C after two 0.1C formation cycles, 3–4.3 V), DFNCA delivered 148.2 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 77% retention (Figure 4a–c). In anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells, DFNCA yielded 64% retention after 50 cycles and 212 Wh/kg energy density at 0.3C charge/0.5C discharge (Figure 4d).

The Carbonate electrolyte is defined as 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1 by volume). In Li/Cu cells, its Coulombic efficiency was 83% and the voltage hysteresis suddenly increased after ~95 h, indicating prefailure (Figure 2a). Under 0.5 mA cm^-2 and 0.5 mAh cm^-2, CE plummeted after 75 cycles. In Li||Li symmetric cells, the overpotential in the Carbonate electrolyte rose from ~40 mV at ~200 h to 313.4 mV at ~350 h (Figure 2i). In Li||NCM811 full cells at 0.25C (after two cycles at 0.1C, 3–4.3 V), capacity showed a sharp decline after 25 cycles and was nearly zero after 100 cycles (Figure 4a,b). In anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells (nominal 200 mAh) at 0.3C charge/0.5C discharge, the capacity dropped below 50 mAh with 23% retention after 40 cycles (Figure 4d). The lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ of the Carbonate electrolyte was 0.30 (Figure 1f).

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: CSPE is used as a quasi-solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries (e.g., LCO/CSPE/Li, LFP/CSPE/Li). Results report: ionic conductivity of CSPE at 30°C is 0.32 mS/cm; oxidative decomposition at 5.3 V vs. Li+/Li (LSV); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.58; Li/CSPE/Li symmetric cells stable for 700 h at 0.5 mA/cm2; LCO/CSPE/Li at 4.3 V shows 80% capacity retention after 400 cycles with 99.5% CE; at 4.4 V retains 130.5 mAh/g after 200 cycles; at 4.5 V delivers 146.7 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 99.3% CE; LFP/CSPE/Li retains 88.6% after 850 cycles at 0.5C and ~102 mAh/g at 5C. Evidence: “the ionic conductivity of CSPE … at 30°C are 0.32 mS/cm … oxidative decomposition at 5.3 V … (tLi+) of CSPE … 0.58 … Li/CSPE/Li cell can be stably cycled … 700 h … 4.3 V LCO/CSPE/Li cell shows … capacity remains 80% after 400 cycles and high Coulombic efficiency of 99.5% … discharge capacity remains 130.5 mAh/g after 200 cycles … 146.7 mAh/g after 100 cycles … LFP/CSPE/Li … capacity retention … 88.6% … and … ~102 mAh/g at 5C.”

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: SN-LiDFOB electrolyte performance evidence: (1) Ionic conductivity at 30 °C = 0.34 mS/cm (Fig. 2a). (2) Oxidative decomposition at 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 2b). (3) Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.30 (Figs. 2c–d). (4) Li/SN-LiDFOB/Li symmetric cell fails in about 6 h at 0.5 mA/cm2 (Fig. 2f). (5) In 4.3 V LCO/SN-LiDFOB/Li coin cell, discharge capacity drops to nearly 0 after 20 cycles (Fig. 3a).

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: Abstract: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." Results & Discussion: "In a future study, the most promising membrane composition will be investigated for proton transport with varying amounts and types of proton transporting groups." and "As the planned future use for these membranes in this laboratory is proton exchange membranes... Imidazole has shown to use a different proton transporting mechanism that does not require water. We are planning to use imidazole to develop a dry fuel cell membrane."

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: LAE1 was used as a linear perfluorinated aryl ether polymer to toughen membranes via blending/crosslinking with HBFP: "To produce free-standing, robust membranes, HBFP was blended with varying amounts of a linear, decafluorobiphenyl aryl ether polymer (LAE1) with and without crosslinking LAE1 into the network." As a control, "LAE1 was cast from DMAc without any HBFP, which resulted in a transparent film. The AFM micrograph (Fig. 5) showed a surface free of pores, but with cavities of >5 μm." Blended systems with LAE1 were mechanically characterized (Tables 1–2), indicating its role as a membrane toughening component; no transport/catalytic metrics were reported for LAE1.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: LAE2 (a linear polyaryl ether sulfone) was used as a toughening polymer in membranes aimed at anhydrous proton exchange applications: “These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes.” LAE2 was blended and crosslinked with HBFP-derived ionomers; improved mixing and mechanical behavior vs. LAE1 are reported: “To improve blending, the LAE1 polymer was replaced with the poly ether sulfone derivative, LAE2… When HBFP and functional HBFPs were crosslinked to the polyarylether sulfone, membranes exhibited a yield point before failure.” Quantitative performance for LAE2-containing blends (mechanical metrics) is provided: Table 3 (30% FPSA50/70% LAE2, TMP 5–20%: Young’s modulus 1460–2310 MPa, UTS up to 78.8 MPa, strain at fracture up to 6.60%; 30% TMP brittle) and Table 4 (30% FPIM50/70% LAE2, TMP 5–30%: UTS up to 78.0 MPa and strain at fracture up to 8.43% at 20% TMP). Pure LAE2 thermal data: Tg 175.3°C; decomposition onset 421.8°C. No proton transport, adsorption, catalysis, or regeneration metrics were measured; hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are null.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: Abstract: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." Blending/Crosslinking Functionalized HBFP with Linear Aryl Ether Polymer LAE2: "As a starting point, the FPSA50 (50% sulfonated HBFP) was used for FPSA/HBFP blends... Each film was characterized by DMA in triplicates (Table 3)." Table 3 caption: "Average mechanical properties of TMP crosslinked FPSA50 (30%) LAE2 (70%) blends." Discussion: "The TMP crosslinked 30FPSA50/70LAE2 membranes were not creasable but could be cut for mechanical testing when hydrated... The lowest mechanical strength was seen for noncrosslinked systems..." Context for application: "As the planned future use for these membranes in this laboratory is proton exchange membranes, HBFP was substituted with two different proton transporting groups, sulfonates, and imidazoles. Sulfonate is the proton-transporting group used in current proton exchange membranes, such as Nafion."

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: Only qualitative mechanical outcomes are reported for FPSA85 blends; no application/performance metrics (e.g., proton conductivity, catalysis, separations) are provided. Evidence: “Polymers with three different sulfonate contents were prepared (50, 85, and 100%, designated as FPSA50, FPSA85, and FPSA100, respectively). Blends of 85% sulfonated polymer with LAE1 were prepared at 30 by 70% mass ratios… For each blend with LAE1 and no crosslinking agent, membranes were too brittle for mechanical analysis and phase separated on the macroscale. No sulfonated HBFP/LAE1 blend was further characterized.” And: “When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2… the mechanical properties were much worse. The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP).” No proton transport or other application data involving FPSA85 were measured.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: The sulfonated HBFP set includes a fully sulfonated sample designated FPSA100: "Polymers with three different sulfonate contents were prepared (50, 85, and 100%, designated as FPSA50, FPSA85, and FPSA100, respectively)." The intended application is proton exchange membranes: "As the planned future use for these membranes in this laboratory is proton exchange membranes..." and "Sulfonate is the proton-transporting group used in current proton exchange membranes, such as Nafion." FPSA100 was blended with LAE2 but showed poor mechanics: "When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2... The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP)." No proton conduction performance values were reported for FPSA100.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPIM50 is a 50% imidazole-substituted HBFP: “A 50% imidazole substituted HBFP (FPIM50) was blended with LAE2 with TMP crosslinking… Each cast was characterized by DMA (Table 4).” The intended application is anhydrous proton exchange membranes: “We are planning to use imidazole to develop a dry fuel cell membrane.” and “These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes.” No proton transport/conductivity data were reported for FPIM50; only mechanical properties, so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are not extractable.

file_name: Long-chain fluorocarbon-driven hybrid solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: LFSPE ionic conductivity: 6.78×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and 1.37×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 80 °C (Fig. 4a); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.47 (Fig. 4d); electrochemical stability window up to 4.713 V (Fig. 4c). Symmetric PDDA-TFSI@Li|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li cells showed stable low overpotentials across 0.05–0.2 mA cm^-2 and sustained low, steady overpotential for 2000 h at 0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4e,f). In full cells: LFP|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li delivered 163.8 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C and retained 98.8% after 500 cycles at 0.5C (Fig. 5); LCO|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li delivered 122.3 mAh g^-1 initially at 0.5C and 99.6 mAh g^-1 after 300 cycles (Fig. 6e); NCM811|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li showed 174.8 mAh g^-1 initially at 0.5C with >71.9% retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 6f).

PC-1 was tested as a solid polymer electrolyte (30 wt% LiTFSI). The ionic conductivity at 25 °C is explicitly reported: “At 25 °C, … PC-3 … 3.75 × 10^{-5} S·cm^{-1} … 7.8 times higher than that obtained for PC-1 (4.86 × 10^{-6} S·cm^{-1}).” This applies directly to PC-1 and no battery performance values are given for PC-1.

PC-2 was prepared as a solid polymer electrolyte by mixing the polymer with 30 wt% LiTFSI and casting ("We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI in acetone, followed by solvent removing."). Ionic conductivity of the three SPEs (including PC-2) was evaluated from 100 to 25 °C (Figure 1a), but explicit numeric conductivity values are only reported for PC-1 and PC-3 in the text; for PC-2 no numeric value is given. The Tg of the SPE based on PC-2 is reported as −41 °C ("The Tg of SPEs decreased with the molar mass of PEO, from −37 °C for PC-1, to −41 °C for PC-2 and to −44 °C for PC-3."). Therefore, no quantitative ionic conductivity or battery performance specific to PC-2 can be extracted from the text.

PC-3 was tested as a solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) with 30 wt% LiTFSI. Quote: "We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI... At 25 °C, an ionic conductivity of 3.75 × 10^−5 S.cm^−1 was measured for PC-3... The ionic conductivity of PC-3 (3.75 × 10^−5 S.cm^−1 at room temperature and 1.86 × 10^−4 S.cm^−1 at 60 °C) is comparable or even better to those determined for the most efficient polycarbonate-based SPEs."

PC-4 is the cross-linkable polycarbonate used to form the semi-interpenetrated network electrolyte (SIN-PC-3): "All solid cross-linked SPE. 60 mg of PC-3, 20 mg of cross-linkable polycarbonate (PC-4), 20 mg of diethylene glycol diacrylate, 42.85 mg of LiTFSI..." and formulation for rheology: "PC-3 (41.4 wt%), LiTFSI (29 wt%), PC-4 (13.8 wt%), diethylglycol diacrylate (13.8 wt%) and the photoinitiator (2 wt%)." Performance of this PC-4–containing electrolyte: "the ionic conductivity of SIN-PC-3 (1.11×10^{-5} S.cm^{-1} at rt)" and "the cationic transference number (tLi+) ... was tLi+ = 0.28 for SIN-PC-3 (Figure S7)."

F2DEM (as 1.75 M LiFSI/F2DEM) is evaluated explicitly as an electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Key performance metrics reported: (1) Li||Cu CE by modified Aurbach method: 99.53 ± 0.03% (higher than F5DEE and DEM). (2) Li||Li symmetric cells: at 1 mA cm^-2 and 3 mAh cm^-2 per cycle, overpotential ≈ 30 mV for F2DEM (vs ≈ 55 mV for F5DEE); soft short observed > 6 mA cm^-2. (3) Oxidative stability by LSV: leakage current onset ~6 V on Al; ~4 V on Pt (greater than DEM). (4) Anode-free Cu||LFP pouch cells (2.5–3.65 V): improved capacity retention and CE vs F5DEE/F4DEE across C/2→2C and C/2→C/5 conditions. (5) Li||LFP coin cells (3.5 mAh cm^-2 LFP, 20 μm Li, 2.5–3.8 V): 80% capacity retention over 125 cycles at 1.5 mA cm^-2 charge / 3 mA cm^-2 discharge; 80% retention over 350 cycles at 0.4 mA cm^-2 charge / 2 mA cm^-2 discharge; 80% retention over 200 cycles at 1 mA cm^-2 charge / 2 mA cm^-2 discharge. The ionic conductivity peaks near 1.75 M (Figures 2a–b), and Li+ transport numbers > 0.4 (Figure 2c), but exact conductivity values are not provided in text.

Electrochemical stability (Li||Cu, Aurbach method): "The average CE of F2DEM was measured as 99.53 ± 0.03% ... and 99.19 ± 0.07% (4 cells) with DEM." Also, for Li||Cu long-term cycling at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (1 mAh cm^-2), cycles 5–50: "the average CE ... DEM (99.29%)." Oxidative stability (LSV): Li||Al leakage current onset ~6 V for DEM; Li||Pt onset ~3 V for DEM. Under faster Li plating/slow stripping, "DEM fails to achieve a stable cycling over 99%" CE.

Electrolyte characterization: "Under a current density of 1 mA cm−2, an overpotential of around 55 mV was observed for F5DEE, similar to our previous report." Electrochemical stability: "The average CE of F2DEM was measured as 99.53 ± 0.03% ... higher than the 99.46 ± 0.01% (3 cells) previously observed with F5DEE." Additional context for oxidative stability: LSV shows F5DEE leakage current onset around 6.5 V on Al and ~4.5 V on Pt, indicating higher oxidative stability with higher fluorination.

Electrochemical stability section: "The average CE of F2DEM was measured as 99.53 ± 0.03% for four cells" in Li||Cu half cells using the modified Aurbach method, explicitly for 1.75 M LiFSI / F2DEM. Context confirms application as an electrolyte for Li metal batteries across Li||Cu, Li||Li, Li||LFP, and Cu||LFP cells.

PU-SN-LiTFSI is used as an elastic solid polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Its ionic conductivity reaches 2.865×10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature when SN content is 41.1 wt%. The electrolyte shows an electrochemical window with no obvious current peak below 4.068 V and a Li+ transference number of 0.511 (SN 41.1 wt%). In application tests, Li//Li symmetric cells cycle for 600 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 with stable overpotential. Solid-state Li/LiFePO4 batteries deliver ~100 mAh/g after 700 cycles at 0.5C (retention ~92%) and exhibit rate capacities of 150, 143, 134, 121, 88, 56, 33, and 17 mAh/g at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, 3C, 4C, and 5C, respectively.

LLZO-F0.2 was used as a fiber filler in a composite solid electrolyte (CSE) for Li batteries. The LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE displays the highest ionic conductivity of 5.37 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure 4a). It also shows an oxidation potential of 5.18 V (Figure 4d) and a lithium-ion transference number of 0.61 (Figure 4e). In symmetric Li|CSE|Li cells, stable cycling for >800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 was demonstrated (Figure 5c). In Li|CSE|LFP full cells, discharge capacities of 160.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C and 130.6 mAh g^-1 at 2C were reported, with 149.6 mAh g^-1 after 800 cycles at 0.5C (97.7% retention) (Figure 6a–d).

Table 1 lists LiBC-1 ionic conductivity σ at 25 °C as 2.3×10^-6 S cm^-1. LiBC-1 was used as the solid polymer electrolyte and binder in Li/LiBC-1/LiFePO4 cells, which delivered up to 130 mAh g^-1 at C/15 and 70 °C (Figure 5c). Additional electrochemical properties for LiBC-1 include anodic limit up to ~4.5 V vs Li+/Li at 70 °C (Figure 4) and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.83 at 70 °C.

Table 1 reports ionic conductivity at 25 °C for LiBC-2: σ = 1.5 × 10^-6 S cm^-1. LiBC-2 is a poly(PEGM)-b-poly(LiMTFSI) single-ion conducting polymer evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte; no cell performance or electrochemical stability data are provided specifically for LiBC-2.

Table 1 shows for LiBC-3: 'σ at 25 °C (S cm^{-1})' = '1.2 × 10^{-6}'. The paper states: 'The ionic conductivity of the polyelectrolytes was measured via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy,' referring to the poly(PEGM)-b-poly(LiMTFSI) block copolymer electrolytes.
Table 1 reports LiBC-4 (poly(PEGM)-b-poly(LiMTFSI)) ionic conductivity σ at 25 °C = 1.6 × 10^-7 S cm^-1, demonstrating its use as a single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte.

Evidence specific to CPE: (i) Ionic conductivity: "the ionic conductivity of CPE is only 1.64×10^-5 mScm^-1 at 25 °C" and "1.7×10^-6 mScm^-1 of CPE at 0 °C" (Figure S3/S4 discussion). (ii) Electrochemical window: "oxidation stability (vs. Li/Li+) for ... CPE (4.5 V)" (Figure 3c). (iii) Symmetric Li/CPE/Li stability: "Li/CPE/Li had a soft short circuit after only 30 h of cycling at 0.2 mA cm^-2 ... When the current density is increased to 0.5 mA cm^-2, Li/CPE/Li cell is short circuited after working for 3 h"; and "the polarization potential has reached 255 mV in the first hour". (iv) Full cell impedance with CPE: "Initial Rct of LFP/CPE/Li is up to 2264 Ω. Furthermore, a Rsei = 685 Ω has been tested". (v) Capacity with CPE: "the specific discharge capacity of LFP/CPE/Li cells at room temperature is very poor" and at 0 °C the cells "unable to work" (no numeric value reported). Given schema fields, no compatible numeric entries for capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration could be populated for CPE.

GCPE was used as the quasi-solid polymer electrolyte in LFP/GCPE/Li cells. At room temperature, the cell delivered a specific discharge capacity of 164.7 mAh/g at 0.1C and maintained >99% capacity retention and >99% Coulombic efficiency after 100 cycles. Additional GCPE-specific performance supporting its electrolyte role: ionic conductivity 1.19×10^-4 mS/cm at 25 °C (and 2.51×10^-5 mS/cm at 0 °C); oxidation stability up to 4.6 V vs. Li/Li+; Li/GCPE/Li symmetric cells showed stable Li plating/stripping without short circuit for 700 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 with low overpotentials (76 mV at 2 h, 131 mV at 300 h, 187 mV at 700 h) and for 150 h at 0.5 mA/cm^2.

For sample PVA10 (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=10:0), Table 1 lists room-temperature ionic conductivity σ=1.80×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C). Section 2.6 reports t_Li+ for PVA10 gel electrolyte as 0.71. Section 2.7 (LSV) gives the oxidation limit for PVA10 as 4.03 V vs Li/Li+.

The optimized electrolyte of PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1 was used in Li|electrolyte|LFP half-cells and delivered an initial discharge capacity of 145.7 mAh·g^-1 at 0.5 C with a capacity retention of 79.0% after 200 cycles (Fig. 10 and Conclusion). Additional device-relevant metrics specifically for PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1 include room-temperature ionic conductivity 2.87×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (with 10 wt% TPGDA), t_Li+ = 0.85, oxidation potential 4.34 V vs Li/Li+, and symmetric Li|electrolyte|Li cycling stability >1800 h. No selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration data apply to this electrolyte.

Table 1 reports for PVA8-P(AMPSLi-IL)2 (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=8:2) a room-temperature ionic conductivity σ = 2.56×10^-3 S·cm^-1. The work states the nanofiber membranes were in situ gelated in 1 mol/L LiFSI in EC with TPGDA and used as polymeric solid electrolytes.

Table 1 reports for PVA7-P(AMPSLi-IL)3 (weight ratio 7:3): porosity 78.4%, electrolyte uptake 518%, and room-temperature ionic conductivity σ = 2.12×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (i.e., 2.12 mS/cm) at 25 °C. Other electrochemical metrics (t_Li+, oxidation potential, cycling) were only given for PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1, not for the 7:3 sample.

Table 1 lists the PVA6-P(AMPSLi-IL)4 membrane (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=6:4) with porosity 75.3%, electrolyte uptake 493%, and room-temperature ionic conductivity σ = 1.83×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C). Other electrochemical metrics (e.g., tLi+, LSV limits, Li|Li cycling, Li|LFP performance) were reported for the 9:1 sample, not for the 6:4 sample, so no further values are assigned to this material.

Direct statement: "For GPE with a P(HFBMA - co - PEGMA) / PVDF mass ratio of 1.7 / 10, the ionic conductivity reached 3.19 mS cm^-1." Also summarized in Conclusions: "When the mass ratio of P(HFBMA-co-PEGMA) and PVDF was 1.7/10, the liquid electrolyte uptake and ionic conductivity were up to 387% and 3.19 mS cm^-1, respectively." The GPEs are used in Li-ion cells (Graphite|GPE|LiMn2O4), indicating Li+ as the conducting species.

Table 1 reports DBU-catalyzed transesterification of poly(HFIPA) with benzyl alcohol in THF: with 2 equiv BnOH and 0.5 equiv DBU, 99% conversion in 2 h at 50 °C; also 98% conversion in 5 h at 25 °C. These data directly quantify poly(HFIPA) post-polymer functionalization performance.
For poly(HFIPMA), Table 2 reports aminolysis with benzyl amine: 2 equiv amine and 2 equiv DBU in DMF at 75 °C for 12 h gave 98% conversion.

The paper reports bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide (1) only as a synthesized intermediate: “Synthesis of bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide (1)… A white powder was obtained with a yield of 69%… indicates high purity of the bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide.” It is then functionalized to give monomer (2) and subsequently polymerized to form the lithiated polymer (5). All application and performance data (ionic conductivity, ESW, Li transference number, battery cycling) are for the polymer electrolyte membranes derived from the lithiated polymer, not for (1). No catalysis, adsorption, or battery performance measurements were conducted on (1) itself.

No direct application/performance data are reported for potassium(4-carboxyl benzene sulphonyl)imide monomer (2). It is used as a monomer for polycondensation: "Polycondensation of 4,4' (hexafluoroisopropylidene)dianiline and potassium(4-carboxyl benzene sulphonyl)imide (2) was performed..." (Section 2.2.2). All performance results (ionic conductivity, transference number, cycling) are for blend membranes using the lithiated polymer (5). Although the text notes that adding a "lithiated monomer" to the cathode can reduce interfacial resistance, in this study "Rather than the lithiated monomer, 3 wt% of lithiated terephthalic acid was added to the cathode slurry" (Section 3.3.3). Therefore, no extractable application metrics apply specifically to monomer (2).

No application performance is reported for material (3) itself. The membranes and electrochemical tests use the lithiated polymer (5) blended with PVdF-HFP: “Four different membrane compositions were made, where the actual amount of lithiated polymer increased…” (Section 2.3). Conductivity and related transport data explicitly refer to PVdF‑HFP:lithiated polymer (5) blends (e.g., “PVdF-HFP to lithiated polymer ratios of 1:1, 3:5, 1:3 and 1:7” and “ionic conductivities of 0.5 mS cm−1 at 20 °C… for the polymer membranes with composition 1:3”, Section 3.1). Electrochemical stability window, Li stripping/plating, and NMC111/Li cycling are also measured with the SIPE membrane containing lithiated polymer (5), not (3) (Sections 3.3.1–3.3.3). Material (3) is the polycondensation product prior to ion substitution/lithiation (Section 2.2.2), with subsequent conversion to (4) and then to the lithiated form (5) used in applications (Section 2.2.3).

Material (4) is the H+-form polymer obtained by ion substitution of polymer (3) (Section 2.2.3). All membrane/electrochemical performance (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, ESW, Li plating/stripping, NMC111 cycling, diffusion) is reported for the lithiated polymer (5) membranes; no application results specific to (4) are provided.

Polymer (5) is the lithiated single-ion conducting polymer used to form blend SIPE membranes with PVdF-HFP and EC:PC (1:1) for Li battery applications. For the 1:3 (PVdF-HFP:lithiated polymer) membrane, ionic conductivities of 0.5 mS cm−1 at 20 °C and 1.08 mS cm−1 at 60 °C were reported (“Regarding the polymer membranes with composition 1:3, ionic conductivities of 0.5 mS cm−1 and 1.08 mS cm−1 were achieved at 20 °C and 60 °C, respectively”). The lithium-ion transference number was 0.9 for the investigated membrane (sample 1:3) (“the lithium-ion transference number tLi+ of the investigated polymer membrane was determined to be 0.9 (for sample 1:3)”). The membrane showed an electrochemical stability window up to 4.6 V vs. Li/Li+ and enabled cycling of NMC111/Li cells (e.g., 163 mA h g−1 at 0.05C and 161 mA h g−1 at 0.1C at 60 °C; 142 and 116 mA h g−1 at 20 °C), but these are whole-cell metrics not encoded in the provided fields.

PEL is used as a composite solid electrolyte in Li batteries. Reported metrics directly tied to PEL include: ionic conductivity up to 5.1×10^-4 S/cm at 10 wt.% LLZTO (Table 1; “exhibit the highest ionic conductivity of 5.1×10^-4 Scm^-1”), electrochemical stability window 4.6 V (Figure 5d), lithium-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.41 (Figure 5f), and tensile strength 17.7 MPa (Figure 5a). Full-cell performance with LFP/PEL/Li shows initial discharge capacity 132 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C and 108 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles (81% retention) (Results; Conclusions). After high-rate cycling at 1 C, PEL cell retained ~20 mAh g^-1 (Figure 6a). These data substantiate the electrolyte application and the capacity value recorded here.

PMHM is used as an in situ polymerized electrolyte for Li metal batteries. For the NCA/PMHM/Li battery, a discharge capacity of 162.2 mAh g^-1 with 91.8% retention is maintained after 200 cycles at 0.5C (Fig. 3c and related text). The study also reports high ionic conductivity and wide electrochemical stability window for PMHM, supporting its role as a battery electrolyte.

Results show PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE ionic conductivity = 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 ('The ionic conductivity ... PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPEs can be calculated to be 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1.'). Additional performance (context): anodic limiting potential 4.7 V ('PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE showed the highest anodic limiting potential of 4.7 V.'), Li+ transference number 0.49 ('PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE reached a high tLi+ of 0.49.'), LiFePO4 cell initial discharge capacity 152.5 mAh g^-1 and 146.3 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles (~100% retention), rate 115.5 mAh g^-1 at 2C, and symmetric Li/Li cycling for 1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 600 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 without short-circuit.

Pure PVDF-HFP was used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) for Li-ion batteries. Reported values specific to the pure PVDF-HFP GPE: (1) Ionic conductivity: “The ionic conductivity of the … pure PVDF-HFP … GPEs can be calculated to be … 2.46 × 10^{-4} S cm^{-1} …” (Fig. 4b text). (2) Li-ion transference number: “The PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE reached a high t_Li+ of 0.49, which was higher than … the pure PVDF-HFP GPE (t_Li+ = 0.39) …” (Fig. 5 text). Additional context for the same material (not encoded in fields): thermal shrinkage only ~4% at 150 °C and no significant change at 175 °C; decomposition onset ~415 °C; porosity 41% and electrolyte uptake 181%.

TF+HCE exhibits high ionic conductivity (4.02 mS cm^-1) at 30°C and the highest Li+ conductivity (2.53 mS cm^-1) due to a high Li+ transference number (0.63). It enables stable Li|Cu cycling: 300 cycles at 1 mA cm^-2, 2 mAh cm^-2 with avg CE 99.17%, and 1000 cycles at 1 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2 with avg CE 98.73%.

PPMBB served as a gel‑polymer electrolyte in Li//LFP cells. At 1C and room temperature, Li/PPMBB10‑GPEs/LFP showed an initial discharge specific capacity of 146.8 mAh/g with ~100% Coulombic efficiency and 76% capacity retention over 1000 cycles (Figure 5a). At 2C, it showed 151.5 mAh/g initially with 84% retention over 364 cycles (Figure 5c). Supporting properties for PPMBB10 include ionic conductivity 1.03×10^-3 S/cm at 25 °C (Figure 3a), Li+ transference number 0.7901 (Figure 3c), and an electrochemical stability window up to ~5.5 V for PPMBB10 (Figure 3d).

FGPE is used as the electrolyte in Li|FGPE|NCM811 and Li|FGPE||Li cells: "Li|NCM811 cells were assembled using a gel electrolyte precursor solution as the electrolyte. The assembled cells were occurred to in situ polymerization at 60 °C for 4 h." The ionic conductivity performance of FGPE is explicitly reported: "The ionic conductivity of FGPE at 25 °C was 4.53 × 10^-4 S cm^-1". FGPE targets Li+ transport: "The Li+ mobility number of the FGPE was 0.43" and diffusion behavior is discussed for Li+ (GITT and CV). High-voltage stability as an electrolyte is also shown: "the FGPE maintains a low oxidation current until 5 V before a significant oxidation peak occurs." Additionally, stable Li plating/stripping verifies electrolyte function: "the Li|FGPE||Li cell ... achieving a stable cycle of over 2000 h."
The provided excerpt includes only associated content, author information, and acknowledgments, with no application or performance data related to LTOC-0%F.
No application/performance data for sample LTOC-5%F is present in the provided text, which only includes Associated Content, author information, and acknowledgments.
The provided excerpt contains only Associated Content, author information, notes, and acknowledgments, with no application description or performance data for sample LTOC-10%F.
The provided excerpt contains only Supporting Information notice, author information, notes, and acknowledgments, with no application or performance data for LTOC-15%F.
The provided text contains only Supporting Information notice, author/affiliation details, notes, and acknowledgments, without any application or performance data for material LTOC-20%F.

The Supporting Information explicitly mentions the "preparation of PVT electrolyte" and provides electrochemical data including "lithium ion transference numbers and activation energies of nine samples in orthogonal experiments" as well as the "effect of different levels of each factor on ionic conductivity, oxidation potential and lithium ion transference number" and a "leakage current curve of PVT electrolyte," indicating PVT is used as an electrolyte conducting Li+.
No application/performance data for PVE appears in the provided text. The excerpt discusses a PVT electrolyte and includes references and supporting information; 'PVE' is not mentioned.

Electrolyte P(HFBA_{1:1}-co-MEA)/LiTFSI used in SSLMBs: ionic conductivity reached 4.83×10^-5 S cm^-1 (Figure 2a, text: "The ionic conductivity of P(HFBA_{1:1}-co-MEA)/LiTFSI reached 4.83×10^-5 Scm^-1"). Electrochemical window exceeded 4.9 V vs PMEA/LiTFSI 4.2 V (Figure 2f). Symmetric Li|P(HFBA_{1:1}-co-MEA)/LiTFSI||Li exhibited extremely stable cycling for >1000 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with polarization increasing from 75 mV initially to 100 mV after 1050 h (Figure 4a). Full cells with LiNi0.5Co0.3Mn0.2O2 delivered 135.5, 121.3, and 93.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 C, respectively, and sustained cycling for 170 cycles at room temperature (Figure 5b,c). Li|P(HFBA_{1:1}-co-MEA)/LiTFSI|LiFePO4 showed initial discharge capacity 159.4 mAh g^-1 and ~99% Coulombic efficiency over 200 cycles (Figure 5d,e).

PMEA/LiTFSI was evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Specific PMEA/LiTFSI data reported: (1) Electrochemical window of PMEA/LiTFSI is 4.2 V (LSV; Figure 2f). (2) Li+ transference number t_Li+ for PMEA/LiTFSI is 0.17 (Figure S1). (3) In symmetric Li|PMEA/LiTFSI|Li cells at 0.05 mA cm^-2, initial polarization voltage is 160 mV and the cell fails after 109 h (Figure 4a). (4) In Li|PMEA/LiTFSI|LiNi0.5Co0.3Mn0.2O2 full cells at 0.1C and room temperature, both electrolytes have similar initial discharge capacity of ~120 mAh g^-1, but with PMEA/LiTFSI the discharge capacity decreases to 7.8 mAh g^-1 after 40 cycles (Figure 5c).
