parse.why: The sample Li-X-4% is defined as a PVDF-HFP composite electrolyte containing 4 wt.% Li-X zeolite: “the composite electrolytes with different content of Li-X (0 wt.%–4 wt.%) in PVDF-HFP were marked as pristine, Li-X-1%, Li-X-2%, Li-X-3%, and Li-X-4%.” Specific quantified electrochemical metrics are only reported for Li-X-3% (e.g., ionic conductivity 1.98×10^-4 S/cm at 80 °C, t_Li+ = 0.55), while the text states that “excessive zeolite (such as 4%) could impede the migration of lithium ions,” without giving numeric values for 4%. LSV shows that after introducing zeolite, the electrochemical window is stable up to 4.7 V for the zeolite-containing samples collectively, but no 4%-specific value is isolated. No cycle-life, symmetric-cell, or full-cell performance data are provided specifically for Li-X-4%.

As shown in Fig. 3(c): "Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively." This value pertains directly to PSPEM-1.
Electrolyte absorptivity of membranes: "the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively."
Electrolyte absorptivity of the membranes: PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively (Fig. 3(c)).

Figure 3(c) states: "Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively." This directly gives an application-relevant metric (electrolyte absorptivity) for PPEM.

Direct measurements on PSGPE-1: (i) Ionic conductivity: "At 30°C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ~0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm−1, respectively." (ii) Oxidation stability window: "The oxidation voltage slightly decreases to 5.17 V (PSGPE-1 and PSGPE-2)." Cell-level context with PSGPE-1: LiFePO4/graphite cells (2.5–3.65 V, 25°C) show rate capability; at 1 C, the PSGPE-1 cell delivers 98.3 mAh/g ("As the current density increases to 1 C, the specific capacities of the cells with PSGPEs ... are 98.3, 106.2, and 109.5 mAh·g−1, respectively.").

PSGPE-2 shows a Li-ion transference number t_Li+ of 0.69 (Fig. 4(b) text: “The PSGPE-2 reaches a high t_Li+ of 0.69”). Its ionic conductivity at 30 °C is reported as 1.07 mS·cm−1 among the series (Fig. 4(c): “At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ~0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm−1, respectively”). Additional performance with PSGPE-2 in LiFePO4//graphite cells includes 106.2 mAh·g−1 at 1 C and 119.2 mAh·g−1 after 350 cycles at 0.5 C with 93.1% retention, and an anodic stability of ~5.17 V per LSV, but these are not mapped due to schema limits.

Direct data for PSGPE-3: (1) Ionic conductivity: "At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for ... PSGPE-3 are ... 1.35 mS·cm−1" (Fig. 4(c)). (2) Electrochemical stability window: "PSGPE-3 shows the lowest anodic limiting potential of 4.92 V" at 25 °C (Fig. 4(a)). (3) Full-cell performance with LiFePO4//graphite (2.5–3.65 V, ~25 °C): at 1C, the specific capacity is 109.5 mAh/g; after rate cycling and returning to 0.5C, capacity drops from 125.3 to 117.1 mAh/g; long-term cycling at 0.5C starts at 127.3 mAh/g and retains 56.4% after 350 cycles (Figs. 5(c), 5(e), text). These results pertain specifically to the PSGPE-3 sample (PVDF-HFP:SN = 7:3).

PGPE (pure PVDF-HFP GPE) exhibits ionic conductivity ~0.81 mS/cm at 30 °C (Fig. 4(c)); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.58 (text near Fig. 4(b)); It also shows the highest oxidation voltage 5.34 V at 25 °C (Fig. 4(a)). In LiFePO4/graphite full cells at 0.5 C after activation at 0.1 C, PGPE delivers 125.8 mAh/g (Fig. 5(e)), though no retention value is reported for PGPE.

Sample 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB is explicitly abbreviated as SGPE: “35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB GPE was selected for subsequent tests and abbreviated SGPE.” Its electrolyte performance is reported: “SGPE exhibits high ionic conductivity of 1.03 mS cm−1 at 30 °C,” with additional metrics (t_Li+ = 0.41; oxidation onset 5.1 V vs Li/Li+; Li|SGPE|Li stable >1000 h at 0.1 mA cm−2; LCO|SGPE|Li 139.4 mAh g−1 initial and 106.1 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles; abstract notes >120 mAh g−1 over 200 cycles). Ionic conductivity is taken as the key scalar performance value directly tied to this electrolyte formulation.

CGPE is the HEMA-based comparative gel polymer electrolyte. Performance in full cells: “As for the LFP||CGPE||Li battery, it delivers an ultralow practical capacity of even ∼50 mAh g−1 at 0.3 C... with LFPI|CGPE|Li even dropping to ∼26 mAh g−1 after 250 cycles.” Additional CGPE electrochemical properties (context): ionic conductivity 0.37–0.39 mS cm−1 at ~30 °C; initial oxidation potential 4.2 V; Li|CGPE|Li symmetric cells fail <500 h and show sharp fluctuations at 0.15 mA cm−2; rate capacities: 88.8, 54.6, 21.3, 12.6, 6.6 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 C. These support CGPE’s role as a (comparative) battery electrolyte and the extracted capacity value.

The sample 35TFMA-65TEP was prepared for FTIR verification of the anchoring effect (mixed TEP with TFMA at 35:65 vol%, polymerized with 1 wt% AIBN at 60 °C for 12 h) and used to analyze the proportion of anchored TEP (55.6%). No electrochemical application data (ionic conductivity, transference number, cycling, flammability, battery performance) were reported for 35TFMA-65TEP. All reported battery/electrolyte performance metrics refer to SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or CGPE, not 35TFMA-65TEP.

The sample 40TFMA-60TEP was prepared for FTIR verification of the anchoring effect (Section 2.2.2) and referenced in Fig. 7b–c. No electrochemical or battery performance data (e.g., ionic conductivity, cycling, transference number) were reported for this specific composition. Quantitative anchored-TEP percentages were not explicitly given for 40TFMA-60TEP in the text. Reported application metrics in the paper pertain to SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or other systems, not to 40TFMA-60TEP.

The material 60TFMA-40TEP is only mentioned as an FTIR sample for anchoring-effect verification (Section 2.2.2: samples named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP, 80TFMA-20TEP). No electrochemical/battery application tests or performance metrics are reported specifically for 60TFMA-40TEP.

The paper only mentions 80TFMA-20TEP as an FTIR characterization sample: “the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP according to gradient volumetric fraction in the corresponding samples.” These were prepared “for the Verification (FTIR and Raman Characterization) of Anchoring Effect,” with no electrochemical/application performance reported for 80TFMA-20TEP.

No application/performance data were reported for sample "35HEMA-65TEP". It was prepared only for spectroscopic verification: "The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA in volumetric ratios... After ... heating at 60 °C for 12 h, the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP... Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process." No battery/electrochemical or other application metrics were provided for 35HEMA-65TEP.

40HEMA-60TEP was prepared for FTIR verification of the anchoring effect (Section 2.2.2: “Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA… The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared…”). No electrochemical or battery performance results were reported for 40HEMA-60TEP; performance data in the paper pertain to SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or CGPE, not to 40HEMA-60TEP.

60HEMA-40TEP appears only as an FTIR sample (HEMA:TEP 60:40, polymerized with 1 wt% AIBN at 60 °C for 12 h) prepared to verify anchoring effects (Section 2.2.2). No battery-electrolyte performance (e.g., ionic conductivity, cycling, voltage window) or other application metrics were reported for this specific composition.

80HEMA-20TEP appears only as an FTIR-prepared sample with no application performance reported. The paper states: 'The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared... After... heating... the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP... Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process.' No conductivity, cycling, or other electrochemical metrics are provided for 80HEMA-20TEP.

Only structural/coordination characterization was reported for this specific sample label. The paper states: “The samples for Raman characterization ... were labeled as 1 M-SGPE and 1 M-CGPE” (Section 2.2.2). Later, Raman analysis compares ion-pairing states: “TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE” (Section 3.5). Battery/electrochemical performance metrics (ionic conductivity, cycling, etc.) are reported for SGPE (e.g., 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) rather than for 1 M-SGPE. Therefore, no direct application performance data specific to 1 M-SGPE can be extracted.

The paper defines the specific sample: “The final samples can be obtained after heating at 60 °C for 12 h and were labeled as 1 M-SGPE and 1 M-CGPE, respectively.” It is used for Raman coordination analysis: “As demonstrated in the peak deconvolution results, TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE…”. No electrochemical performance (conductivity, tLi+, cycling, capacity, CCD, etc.) is reported for 1 M-CGPE; those results correspond to CGPE with 0.5 M LiTFSI (35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or to SGPE. Therefore, no application metrics specific to 1 M-CGPE can be extracted.

F-QSCE@30 exhibits high ionic conductivity of 1.21 mS cm−1 at 25 °C; F-QSCE@30 demonstrates ... wide electrochemical windows of 5.20 V, and stable cycling stability for Li//Li symmetric cells over 4000 h; the optimal electrolyte was ... designated as F-QSCE@30; As presented in Fig. 2h, the Li/F-QSCE@30/Li symmetric cell demonstrates a low overpotential of 24.2 mV and remains stable cycling for over 4000 h; The inductive effect weakens the coordination between Li+ and TFSI−, enhancing Li+ transport.

QSCE@30 (the fluorine-free QSCE) shows an ionic conductivity of 0.34 mScm−1 at 25°C: “F-QSCE@30 has an ionic conductivity of 1.21 mScm−1 at 25°C, whereas QSCE@30 demonstrates a lower ionic conductivity of 0.34 mScm−1 at 25°C.” Electrochemical stability window for QSCE@30 is ~4.50 V: “F-QSCE@30 exhibits a high oxidation potential of approximately 5.20 V, while QSCE@30 shows an oxidation potential occurring at 4.50 V.” Li//Li symmetric cell with QSCE@30: initial overpotential 42.5 mV and short circuit after 897 h: “the Li/QSCE@30/Li demonstrates a high initial overpotential of 42.5 mV… sharply increases after 897 h with a short circuit.” Overpotentials for QSCE@30 at different currents: 47.9 mV (0.1 mA cm−2), 106.1 mV (0.2 mA cm−2), 571.9 mV (0.4 mA cm−2). Exchange current density I0 = 0.016 mA cm−2 (Fig. S4). tLi+ = 0.34 at 60°C (Fig. S5). In LFP/QSCE@30/Li full cell at 60°C: first-cycle discharge capacity 86.2 mAh g−1 at 0.5C, CE 98.7%, capacity retention 53.2% after 150 cycles. Upon rate recovery, capacity 134.3 mAh g−1 at 0.1C. After 50 cycles, Rct = 412.9 Ω for the QSCE@30-based cell (Fig. 3e).

SiO2@IL was 'used as the fillers to prepare QSCEs' (Sect. 2.2) and is included at 5 wt% in the optimized electrolyte (F-QSCE@30) (Sect. 3.1). The text further notes: 'The weakened coordination number is conducive to the Li+ transport in the IL on the surface of SiO2@IL or in the polymer chains' (Sect. 3.3). No standalone performance metrics specific to SiO2@IL were reported.

21-β-CD-Br is described as a multifunctional macroinitiator for ATRP rather than the final functional material: "Tailor-made 21-β-CD-Br with multiple active sites ... acted as a highly efficient multifunctional macroinitiator for initiating the ATRP of monomers with double bonds. The fluorine-containing acrylate monomer TFEMA..." and "a series of 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA ... were synthesized by ATRP using 21-β-CD-Br as a multifunctional macroinitiator." No direct performance metrics (capacity, selectivity, etc.) are reported for 21-β-CD-Br itself.

21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA was incorporated to form fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolytes (FMC-ASPEs) for Li metal batteries. The text states: "The fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolyte, which includes 21-β CD-g PTFEMA ... FMC-ASPE-Li ... was prepared" and that the addition of 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA improves properties. Ionic conductivity data directly reported for FMC-ASPE-Li: "the ionic conductivity reached 6.43×10^{-4} S cm^{-1} at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Li" (Fig. 3a,b; Supplementary Tables 6,7). Additional relevant performance tied to the same composite including this material: oxidation potential increased to "~4.7 V" vs Li+/Li (Fig. 2a), and the cation transference number "t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li was 0.88" (Fig. 3c,d; Supplementary Table 11). Long-term symmetric-cell cycling with Li|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li showed >3800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~0.1 V polarization (Fig. 5n). These results are for the electrolyte that explicitly contains 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA.
The determined t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li (0.88) was ~4.4 times larger than that for PEO-ASPE-Li (t_Li+ = 0.21, see Fig. 5a, Supplementary Fig. 53, and Supplementary Table 11).

PEO-ASPE-Na exhibited a sodium-ion transference number t_Na+ = 0.2 (reported alongside FMC-ASPE-Na where t_Na+ = 0.71 and noted as 3.5× higher). In Na full cells using PEO-ASPE-Na, the discharge capacity retention was 61% for NVOPF|PEO-ASPE-Na|Na (60 mA g^-1, 2.5–4.2 V, 80 °C) and 60% for NCNFM|PEO-ASPE-Na|Na (60 mA g^-1, 2.0–4.0 V, 80 °C), values cited as baselines improved by FMC-ASPE-Na.

FMC-ASPE-Li is an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries: “The fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolyte… (FMC-ASPE-Li…)”. Ionic conductivity: “the ionic conductivity reached 6.43×10^-4 Scm^-1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Li…”. Li-ion transference number: “The calculated t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li was 0.88.” Additional corroborative performance (not mapped to fields): oxidation stability ~4.7 V vs Li+/Li from LSV (Fig. 2a); Li|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li symmetric cycling >3800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 70 °C with ~0.1 V polarization (Fig. 5n). No regeneration procedure/data reported.

Defined as FMC-ASPE-Na (fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for AS-SMBs). Electrochemical window for FMC-ASPEs reached ~5.0 V in AS-SMBs (Fig. 2b). Ionic conductivity reached 8.43×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Na (Fig. 3b; Supplementary Tables 6,7). The calculated t_Na+ of FMC-ASPE-Na was ~0.71 (Fig. 3e,f; Supplementary Tables 12–14).

P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC) is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Li|P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC)|NCM523 full cells (cutoff 4.5 V, 30 °C) showed an initial discharge capacity of 164.19 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C and retained 146.96 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles (~90% retention). The electrolyte exhibits ionic conductivity of 1.37 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C and Li+ transference number ~0.40, and enables stable Li|Li symmetric cycling (average polarization ~43 mV for up to 2500 h). LFP full cells (cutoff 4.0 V, 30 °C) delivered 161.20 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C and 140.72 mAh g^-1 after 600 cycles.

For P(IL-OFHDODA), Fig. 2a: 'The molar ratio of [IL] to [OFHDODA] was tuned ... at 25 °C, which reached a maximum ionic conductivity of 0.16 mS cm−1 under 8.5:2.' Also, Fig. 3b reports ESW specific to P(IL-OFHDODA): '... P(IL-OFHDODA) ... 5.31 V (vs. Li+/Li)'. Other cycling metrics shown in the paper pertain to P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC), not this material.

Electrochemical stability window (ESW): “the extracted ESW of … P(IL-VEC) … is … 4.39 V (vs. Li+/Li)” (Fig. 3b). Li|Li symmetric cell durability (no OFHDODA i.e., P(IL-VEC)): “the SPE without OFHDODA-based cell short-circuited after 388 h” at 0.1 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 4a).

Electrochemical stability window (ESW) of P(OFHDODA-VEC) is reported as 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li): "Figure 3b shows that the extracted ESW of ... P(OFHDODA-VEC) is 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li)." No ionic conductivity, cycling, or other performance metrics are provided for P(OFHDODA-VEC) specifically.

Only ESW for PIL is explicitly reported: ESW = 5.51 V (vs. Li+/Li) from LSV (Fig. 3b). No PIL-specific ionic conductivity, battery capacity, tLi+, cycling, or regeneration data are given. ESW is not a field in the schema, so performance fields are left null.
Figure 3b reports the electrochemical stability window (ESW) of POFHDODA as 5.13 V (vs. Li+/Li), supporting its use as a polymer electrolyte component in lithium metal batteries.

PVEC was directly evaluated for electrochemical stability as an SPE component: 'Figure 3b shows that the extracted ESW of PIL, POFHDODA, PVEC, P(IL-VEC), P(IL-OFHDODA) and P(OFHDODA-VEC) is 5.51, 5.13, 4.35, 4.39, 5.31, and 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li), respectively. Compared with PIL and POFHDODA, PVEC possessed the narrowest ESW.'

PVCA is used as a functional component of the PVFH-PVCA polymer electrolyte for Li-metal batteries and directly participates in interphase formation: “PVCA participates in the formation of the cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI), suppressing electrolyte decomposition…” and the electrolyte “can generate a stable SEI on the Li-metal anode” (Fig. 1a). It is further supported by: “PVCA has the lowest LUMO energy level and a relatively high HOMO energy level, indicating its ability to preferentially participate in the formation of SEI and CEI” (Fig. 2e), and “the preferential participation of PVCA in SEI formation at the Li anode leads to a dual-layered SEI enriched in N and F” (Summary). All quantitative performance metrics reported (e.g., ionic conductivity 2.04×10^-3 S cm^-1, Li+ transference number 0.61, symmetric Li||Li cycling 4500 h) are for the PVFH-PVCA blend electrolyte, not PVCA alone; therefore no numeric capacity/selectivity/breakthrough specific to PVCA can be extracted.

PVFH soaked in the LE (PVLE) was used as a control electrolyte host. The PVFH-PVCA gel electrolyte exhibited a significantly higher ionic conductivity at 25 °C (2.04×10^-3 S cm^-1) than that of PVLE (0.94×10^-3 S cm^-1; Fig. 3b). Furthermore, it presented a higher Li+ transference number (0.61) than PVLE (0.31) (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Fig. S15). According to the LSV curves in Fig. 3a, the oxidation potentials of PVLE and CLE are similar at ~4.7 V vs. Li+/Li. At a current density of 8 mA cm^-2, the voltage curve of the Li||Li cell with the PVLE showed a sustained increase in polarization.

PVFH-PVCA is used as a polymer electrolyte in Li-metal batteries. The PVFH-PVCA gel electrolyte exhibited a significantly higher ionic conductivity at 25°C (2.04×10^−3 S cm^−1) than that of PVLE (0.94×10^−3 S cm^−1) (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, it presented a higher Li+ transference number (0.61) than those of CLE (0.34) and PVLE (0.31) (Fig. 3c). Symmetric Li||Li cells with PVFH-PVCA could be stably cycled for more than 4500 h at 8 mA cm^−2 (Fig. 5a) and sustained ~32 mV overpotential at 10 mA cm^−2 (Fig. 5b). Full cells show durable cycling: LMNCO|PVFH-PVCA|Li retained 84.8% after 400 cycles at 2.1–4.6 V, 1C (Fig. 6a) and 78.8% after 340 cycles at 2.1–4.8 V, 1C (Fig. 6c); LCO|PVFH-PVCA|Li retained 89.2% after 1500 cycles (1C, 3.0–4.5 V) (Fig. 7a–c).

PVLE (PVFH soaked in the liquid electrolyte) is used as a control polymer electrolyte in Li-metal batteries (Li|Cu, Li||Li, and Li|LMNCO). Electrochemical metrics reported for PVLE include an oxidation potential of ~-4.7 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3a), ionic conductivity of 0.94×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3b), and a Li+ transference number of 0.31 (Fig. 3c). In Li|Cu cells, PVLE yields loose, porous Li deposits with a plated layer thickness of ~49.7 μm at 8 mA cm^-2 for 4 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4a,d). In Li||Li symmetric cells at 8 mA cm^-2 (4 mAh cm^-2), the PVLE cell shows sustained polarization increase (Fig. 5a). In Li|LMNCO full cells at 1C, 2.1–4.6 V, capacity retention with PVLE is 33.9% after 400 cycles (Fig. 6a); at 2.1–4.8 V the PVLE cell fails after ~100 cycles (Fig. 6c). These data establish PVLE’s role and performance as a lithium battery electrolyte transporting Li+.

CLE is Celgard with 60 μL liquid electrolyte. Reported CLE performance: (i) Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.34 (Fig. 3c). (ii) Electrochemical stability window: oxidation potential similar to PVLE at ~4.7 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3a). (iii) Li||Li symmetric cell at 8 mA cm−2 and 4 mAh cm−2 failed after ~1100 h (Fig. 5a). (iv) Li deposition with CLE is porous/fibrous (Fig. 4b,e) and forms thicker, inhomogeneous SEI without N (Supplementary Fig. S24–S25). (v) LMNCO|CLE|Li shows 64.0% capacity retention after 400 cycles at 1C between 2.1–4.6 V; at 2.1–4.8 V the cell was damaged after 207 cycles (Fig. 6a,c).

WSGPE is used as an electrolyte in Li metal batteries and enables high performance. Key reported results directly tied to WSGPE: electrochemical window up to 5.05 V; Li+ transference number 0.83; ionic conductivity 4.40e-4 S cm-1 at RT. Li|WSGPE|NCM811 coin cells: 154.8 mAh g-1 with 92.5% retention after 300 cycles at 188 mA g-1 (3–4.3 V); 130.4 mAh g-1 after 200 cycles with 90.8% retention at 376 mA g-1; 120.8 mAh g-1 after 300 cycles at 188 mA g-1 with 4.5 V cutoff; at -20 °C: 131.2 mAh g-1 with 97.1% retention after 200 cycles at 37.6 mA g-1; at 80 °C: 172.2 mAh g-1 with 94.0% retention after 100 cycles at 188 mA g-1. Wide operating range −30 to 80 °C. Li|WSGPE|Li symmetric cells: stable plating/stripping ≥1500 h at 0.5 mA cm-2 and 0.5 mAh cm-2 with ~60 mV overpotential; ~200 mV overpotential up to 1 mA cm-2. Pouch cells with WSGPE: designed 750 mAh cell delivers 726.8 mAh at ~3.9 V, specific energy 490.8 Wh kg-1; large-format 5.4 Ah pouch cell shows 451 Wh kg-1. Nonflammability demonstrated and self-discharge suppressed (stable ~4.2 V after 15 days).

The study prepared a control sample by replacing FEP in WSGPE with an equal amount of FEC, denoted as FEP-free gel polymer electrolyte (FEP-free GPE). Performance observations specific to FEP-free GPE include: (1) Exchange current density at −20 °C: j0 = 0.16 mA cm^-2, lower than WSGPE and liquid electrolyte ("The j0 in WSGPE (0.38 mA cm^-2) is markedly larger than those in liquid electrolyte (0.29 mA cm^-2), PTFMA-free GPE (0.21 mA cm^-2) and FEP-free GPE (0.16 mA cm^-2)"). (2) Li symmetric cells with FEP-free GPE short-circuited at 293 h under 0.5 mA cm^-2 / 0.5 mAh cm^-2 ("liquid electrolyte, FEP-free GPE and PTFMA-free GPE cells are short-circuited at 166 h, 293 h and 236 h, respectively"). (3) Control electrolytes including FEP-free GPE could not generate stable interfacial layers ("all the control samples (liquid electrolytes, FEP-free GPE and PTFMA-free GPE) are unable to generate stable interfacial layers"). These data indicate its application as a Li metal battery electrolyte but with inferior interfacial stability and kinetics compared to WSGPE.

PTFMA-free GPE is evaluated as an electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Quantitative performance reported includes interfacial Li+ transfer kinetics at −20 °C with j0 = 0.21 mA cm−2: "the j0 in WSGPE (0.38 mA cm−2) is markedly larger than those in liquid electrolyte (0.29 mA cm−2), PTFMA-free GPE (0.21 mA cm−2) and FEP-free GPE (0.16 mA cm−2)". In Li|Li symmetric cells (0.5 mA cm−2, 0.5 mAh cm−2), the PTFMA-free GPE cell short-circuited at 236 h: "liquid electrolyte, FEP-free GPE and PTFMA-free GPE cells are short-circuited at 166 h, 293 h and 236 h, respectively... the generated SEI layer is unstable". It is also stated that PTFMA-free GPE fails to form stable interfacial layers in Li||NCM811 cells (Supplementary Fig. 20).

“Like PEO, PPC is immiscible with PVDF-HFP, resulting in a phase-separated PPC-LPIFD (Supplementary Fig. 45). In addition, the reduction of polycarbonate forms an organic-rich SEI compared to PEO-LPIFD. As a result, the PPC-LPIFD has the lowest CCD (0.3 mA cm^-2) and CE (<90%) (Supplementary Fig. 45) among these polymer blends.”

PEO-LPIFD was tested as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal cells. Reported performance: (1) Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.38 for PEO-LPIFD (Supplementary Fig. 26a). (2) In Li||Li symmetric cells, short-circuiting was quickly observed at 0.6 mA cm^-2 (Supplementary Fig. 35a). (3) In Li||Cu half-cells, the Coulombic efficiency at 0.3 mA cm^-2 quickly reached ~98% but the cell short-circuited soon after (Fig. 4e red; Supplementary Fig. 42a). (4) Li deposition at 0.3 mA cm^-2, 0.6 mAh cm^-2 showed a non-uniform, porous Li layer of ~9.2 µm thickness (Fig. 5a–c). These results are attributed to phase separation between PEO and PVDF-HFP leading to non-uniform Li deposition and SEI formation.

PMMA-LPIFD is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal plating/stripping. Reported Li||Cu Coulombic efficiency: "the single-phase PMMA-LPIFD shows a much longer cycling stability... but the Li CE could reach only 95% at the same current of 0.3 mA cm−2 after 300 cycles (Fig. 4e, pink)." It short-circuits at higher currents: "short-circuiting is observed when the current density reaches 1.4 mA cm−2 (Supplementary Fig. 35b)" and at 0.5 mA cm−2 it short-circuited within 100 cycles with further reduced CE (Supplementary Fig. 43). Its Li-ion transference number is 0.53 (Supplementary Fig. 26b).

PTFEP-LPIFD is a single-phase polymer electrolyte enabling Li-metal batteries. It shows no short-circuiting until 3.7 mA cm^-2 in Li||Li symmetric cells (critical current density). It achieves Li||Cu Coulombic efficiency ≈99.1% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 after 300 cycles. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity is 3.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 with Ea=0.18 eV. It supports high-voltage operation: anodic decomposition above 4.9 V, increasing to 5.4 V after passivation. In Li||NMC811 full cells (2.8–4.5 V), it delivers >400 cycles with ~80% capacity retention at 0.5C and average CE 99.95%. At 4.6 V, it retains 79.7% after 200 cycles with average CE 99.91%, and enables high-rate capacities (e.g., 186 and 101.2 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 2C at 30°C; up to 4C at 45°C). These results are specific to PTFEP-LPIFD among the compared LPIFDs.

For PTFEP-LPIFD: "shows no short-circuiting until a high current of 3.7 mA cm^-2" (Li||Li symmetric). Also, "even at a current density of 0.5 mA cm^-2, the Li||Cu half-cell using PTFEP-LPIFD reached a high CE ≈ 99.1% after 300 cycles." Additional electrolyte metrics for this sample include "a high ionic conductivity 3.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature" and "t_Li+ = 0.64."

PEO-LPIFD was evaluated as a polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to PEO-LPIFD include: (1) Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.38 (Supplementary Fig. 26a: “its values are 0.38 for PEO-LPIFD”). (2) In Li|Cu half-cells, the Coulombic efficiency quickly reaches 98% at 0.3 mA cm−2, but the cell soon short-circuits (“the CE of PEO-LPIFD at a low current density of 0.3 mA cm−2 can quickly reach 98% ... However, phase separation leads to a quick short circuit”). (3) Short-circuiting was observed at 0.6 mA cm−2 in CCD testing (“For PEO-LPIFD, short-circuiting was quickly observed at 0.6 mA cm−2”). These demonstrate its use as a battery electrolyte transporting Li+.

PMMA-LPIFD was evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li metal plating/stripping. In Li|PMMA-LPIFD|Cu cells, the Li CE reached ~95% at 0.3 mA cm^-2 after 300 cycles; raising the current density to 0.5 mA cm^-2 led to short-circuit within 100 cycles with lower CE (Fig. 4e; Supplementary Fig. 43). In critical current density tests, short-circuiting was observed when the current density reached 1.4 mA cm^-2 (Supplementary Fig. 35b). The Li-ion transference number t_Li+ was 0.53 (Supplementary Fig. 26b).

The table summarized in Fig. 5j states: “PPC-LPIFD has the lowest CCD (0.3 mA cm−2) and CE (<90%) (Supplementary Fig. 45) among these polymer blends.” This directly provides the CCD and the (less-than) CE for PPC-LPIFD.

The paper states: "In contrast, PVSF is miscible with PVDF-HFP... Therefore, PVSF achieves both a higher CE (99%) and a higher CCD (2.4 mA cm^-2) (Supplementary Fig. 46)." These values are reported specifically for PVSF-LPIFD.

PFPE1000-DMC is used as a lithium-ion battery electrolyte: Li/(PFPE1000-DMC·LiTFSI)/LiNMC coin cells cycled at 30°C delivered capacities of 145 (C/20), 140 (C/15), 120 (C/10), and 105 mAh·g^-1 (C/8) (Fig. 5A,B). PFPE1000-DMC exhibits ionic conductivity of 2.5×10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30°C (Fig. 4A) and near-unity Li+ transference number t+ ≈ 0.91–1.0 at r = 0.04 in the temperature range studied (Fig. 4B, text). It is electrochemically stable up to 4.3 V (SI Fig. S7). Safety metrics for PFPE1000-DMC: Td(5%) = 212°C, no sustained burning observed, flash point not detected (<200°C) (Fig. 2B table); no volatility or degradation below 200°C (text).
